examples.context: Mia is a young chimp who has been observed to be decisive. In this world, any creature that is disciplined is also curious; therefore, if Mia were disciplined, she would necessarily be curious. Additionally, being curious always leads to a lack of decisiveness, so if Mia were curious she could not be decisive. No other facts or rules are provided.
examples.question: Is Mia disciplined?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Mia possesses the property of being disciplined. The available information includes a fact about Mia's decisiveness and two conditional rules linking discipline, curiosity, and decisiveness.

The first rule tells us that discipline entails curiosity; this will be useful later because if we can show Mia is not curious, we can work backwards to say she cannot be disciplined. Remembering this conditional relationship sets up the later contrapositive reasoning.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Mia is disciplined then Mia is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(mia) :- disciplined(mia)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that curiosity prevents decisiveness. This connects directly to the known fact that Mia is decisive, allowing us to use modus tollens to eliminate the possibility that she is curious.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Mia is curious then Mia is not decisive.</text>
<logic>~decisive(mia) :- curious(mia)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Mia is decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(mia)</logic>
</step>

We know Mia is decisive (Premise 3). If she were curious, the second rule would force her to be not decisive, which contradicts the fact. Therefore, by modus tollens, Mia cannot be curious. This step removes curiosity from Mia's possible traits, clearing the way for the next inference.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because Mia is decisive and curiosity would make her not decisive, we infer that Mia is not curious.</text>
<logic>~curious(mia)</logic>
</step>

We have just established that Mia is not curious. The first rule says that being disciplined would guarantee curiosity. Since Mia lacks curiosity, the contrapositive of that rule tells us she cannot be disciplined. Applying modus tollens again yields the conclusion that Mia is not disciplined.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since Mia is not curious and discipline would imply curiosity, we conclude that Mia is not disciplined.</text>
<logic>~disciplined(mia)</logic>
</step>

We derived Mia is not disciplined, so the conclusion Mia is disciplined is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In this library, the reference book is known to be quick to find. The archivist, on the other hand, is well shelved. examples.question: Is the archivist annotated?
examples.completion:

We are given information about a reference book and an archivist in a library setting. Our aim is to determine whether the archivist is annotated, which corresponds to the predicate witty(walter).

One of the rules tells us that any archivist that is not logically organized is annotated. This connection will allow us to derive the desired property once we know the archivist lacks logical organization.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if an archivist is not logically organized, then it is annotated.</text>
<logic>annotated(archivist) :- ~logically_organized(archivist)</logic>
</step>

Another rule states that if an archivist is logically organized, then it cannot be well shelved. By comparing this with the fact that the archivist is well shelved, we can infer the negation of logical organization.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if an archivist is logically organized, then it is not well shelved.</text>
<logic>~well_shelved(archivist) :- logically_organized(archivist)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The reference book is quick to find.</text>
<logic>quick_to_find(reference_book)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The archivist is well shelved.</text>
<logic>well_shelved(archivist)</logic>
</step>

We know the archivist is well shelved, and the rule says logical organization would make it not well shelved. Since the archivist is indeed well shelved, the condition for logical organization must be false, leading us to conclude the archivist is not logically organized.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From the archivist being well shelved and the rule that logical organization implies not well shelved, we infer that the archivist cannot be logically organized.</text>
<logic>~logically_organized(archivist)</logic>

Having established that the archivist is not logically organized, we can apply the first rule, which directly yields that a non‑logically‑organized archivist is annotated. Thus, the archivist is annotated.

Therefore Archivist is annotated, so the conclusion is True.

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">

Now that we have ~orderly(kate), we turn to PREMISE_1, which states that rested(kate) would imply orderly(kate). Since orderly(kate) is false, the antecedent rested(kate) must also be false, leading to ~rested(kate).

examples.context: Victor's design is grid_aligned. Whenever a design is grid_aligned, it is also structural, so any grid_aligned design by Victor guarantees a structural design. Whenever a design is structural, it is also ornate; thus any structural design by Victor is ornate. Either Victor's design is not ornate, or it is load_bearing.
examples.question: Is Victor's design not load_bearing?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Victor's design fails to be load_bearing. The information provided includes a concrete fact about Victor, two conditional rules linking architectural properties, and a disjunction that connects ornateness with load-bearing status. Our goal is to see if the premises force a contradiction with the query.

The first rule tells us that whenever Victor's design is structural, it must also be ornate. This link will let us move from a structural conclusion to an ornate one later in the proof, which is essential for using the disjunction.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any structural design by Victor is also ornate.</text>
<logic>ornate(victor) :- structural(victor)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that a grid_aligned design guarantees a structural design for Victor. Since we already know Victor is grid_aligned, this rule will let us derive the structural property needed for the next step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any grid_aligned design by Victor is structural.</text>
<logic>structural(victor) :- grid_aligned(victor)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either Victor's design is not ornate, or it is load_bearing. Once we manage to establish that Victor is indeed ornate, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second alternative—load_bearing—to be true.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Victor's design is not ornate, or it is load_bearing.</text>
<logic>~ornate(victor) v load_bearing(victor)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Victor's design is grid_aligned serves as the starting point for the chain of implications. It directly satisfies the antecedent of the second rule, allowing us to infer a new property.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Victor's design is grid_aligned.</text>
<logic>grid_aligned(victor)</logic>
</step>

Applying Modus Ponens, we combine the fact that Victor is grid_aligned with the rule that grid_aligned implies structural. Because the antecedent of the rule is satisfied, we can confidently assert that Victor's design is structural. This new fact will be used in the next inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From Victor being grid_aligned and the rule that grid_aligned implies structural, we infer Victor is structural.</text>
<logic>structural(victor)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Victor is structural, we can again use Modus Ponens with the first rule, which says structural leads to ornate. Since the antecedent (structural) holds, we conclude that Victor's design is ornate. This conclusion is crucial for activating the disjunction.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Given Victor is structural and the rule that structural implies ornate, we conclude Victor is ornate.</text>
<logic>ornate(victor)</logic>
</step>

Having proved Victor is ornate, we turn to the disjunction: either not ornate or load_bearing. Because the first option is false (Victor is ornate), the disjunction can only be satisfied by the second option. Using Disjunctive Syllogism, we eliminate the impossible alternative and infer that Victor's design is load_bearing.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since Victor is ornate and we have that either not ornate or load_bearing, we deduce Victor is load_bearing.</text>
<logic>load_bearing(victor)</logic>
</step>

We derived Victor is load bearing, so the conclusion Victor is not load bearing is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Luna is honest. Whenever something is honest, it is also rational. In other words, any honest entity guarantees rationality. Likewise, whenever something is rational, it becomes confident; any rational entity is confident by definition.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude that Luna is systematic?
examples.completion:
<think>

The first rule tells us that rationality leads to confidence for Luna. This is relevant because it links two of the predicates we might eventually derive, but it does not yet give us either property on its own.

<logic>confident(luna) :- rational(luna)</logic>

The second rule connects honesty to rationality, showing that any honest individual must also be rational. Since we already have Luna's honesty as a fact, this rule will let us move from honesty to rationality.
<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>rational(luna) :- honest(luna)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Luna is honest provides the starting point for the chain of reasoning. With this fact and the previous rule, we can conclude that Luna is rational, and then, using the first rule, that Luna is confident.

examples.context: Finley is known to be efficient. In this setting, any individual who is efficient becomes sharp. Moreover, Finley satisfies the statement that either he is not sharp or he is clever. It is also true that every clever person is eloquent. Whenever someone is eloquent, that person is brave. Finally, any brave person is agile.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Finley is agile?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Finley can be shown to possess the quality of agility. To answer this, we will follow the chain of conditions supplied in the context, moving from known facts toward the target property.

The first rule connects bravery to agility, telling us that establishing Finley's bravery will directly give us agility. This rule is essential because it bridges the intermediate property we will later derive to the final goal.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Finley is brave, then he is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(finley) :- brave(finley)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links eloquence to bravery, so if we can prove Finley is eloquent, we will automatically obtain his bravery. This creates the next step in the logical cascade toward agility.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Finley is eloquent, then he is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(finley) :- eloquent(finley)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that cleverness guarantees eloquence. Hence, demonstrating Finley's cleverness will allow us to move forward to eloquence, then bravery, and finally agility.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Finley is clever, then he is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(finley) :- clever(finley)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule tells us that efficiency leads to sharpness. Since we already know Finley is efficient, this rule will let us infer that he is sharp, which will be useful for the upcoming disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Finley is efficient, then he is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(finley) :- efficient(finley)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either Finley is not sharp or he is clever. Combined with any information about his sharpness, this statement will let us deduce which of the two must hold.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that Finley is either not sharp or clever.</text>
<logic>~sharp(finley) v clever(finley)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Finley is efficient is the starting point of the proof. It activates the efficiency‑to‑sharpness rule and sets the chain of inference in motion.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact tells us that Finley is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(finley)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Finley is efficient (Premise 6) and the rule says efficiency implies sharpness (Premise 4), we can directly infer that Finley is sharp. This application of modus ponens gives us a concrete property needed for the disjunctive step that follows.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>From Finley's efficiency and the rule linking efficiency to sharpness, we infer that Finley is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(finley)</logic>
</step>

We now have Finley sharp, but the disjunction tells us that either he is not sharp or he is clever. Since the first alternative is false (he is sharp), the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Finley must be clever. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism, yielding cleverness.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Using the fact that Finley is sharp together with the disjunction "not sharp or clever", we deduce that Finley must be clever.</text>
<logic>clever(finley)</logic>
</step>

Having established Finley's cleverness, we can invoke the rule that cleverness guarantees eloquence (Premise 3). By modus ponens, Finley is therefore eloquent, moving us one step closer to bravery.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Because Finley is clever and clever implies eloquence, we conclude that Finley is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(finley)</logic>
</step>

With Finley now known to be eloquent, we apply the rule that eloquence leads to bravery (Premise 2). This gives us Finley's bravery, which is the immediate precursor to agility according to the first rule.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Since Finley is eloquent and eloquence implies bravery, we infer that Finley is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(finley)</logic>
</step>

Finally, since we have shown Finley is brave, the rule linking bravery to agility (Premise 1) allows us to conclude that Finley is agile. This completes the logical chain from the initial fact to the desired conclusion.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Given that Finley is brave and bravery implies agility, we determine that Finley is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(finley)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Finley is agile, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Helen is analytical. River is attentive, and Ash is consistent. Whenever someone is attentive, they are competitive, so River is competitive. If someone is generous, then they are determined, which gives a link from generosity to determination for Ash. Being methodical guarantees health, so anyone who is methodical becomes healthy; this connects to River later. Consistency leads to humility, therefore Ash is humble. Evidence‑driven individuals are intuitive, so any person who is evidence‑driven will also be intuitive. Analytical people are productive, meaning every analytical person is also productive. If someone is agile, then they cannot be productive; that is, agility implies non‑productivity. Either Helen is agile or Helen is evidence‑driven; at least one of these holds. Either River is not competitive or River is methodical; one of those must be true. Either Ash is not humble or Ash is generous; at least one of those alternatives is satisfied.
examples.question: Based on the information given, is Helen intuitive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Helen possesses the intuitive quality. The goal is to trace a chain of implications from the facts and rules provided to reach intuitive(helen).

This premise connects evidence‑driven status directly to intuition, so establishing evidence‑driven for Helen will immediately give us intuition. It is a crucial link in the chain we aim to build.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Helen is evidence‑driven, then she is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(helen) :- evidence_driven(helen)</logic>

Here we have a rule that turns analytical into productive. Since we already know Helen is analytical, this rule will let us derive productivity, which later helps eliminate agility.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any analytical person, such as Helen, is also productive.</text>
<logic>productive(helen) :- analytical(helen)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that agility would contradict productivity. By later showing Helen is productive, we can use this rule in a contrapositive way to rule out agility.

<text>The rule indicates that if someone is agile, then they are not productive; applied to Helen, agile(helen) would imply not productive(helen).</text>
<logic>~productive(helen) :- agile(helen)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two possible ways to satisfy a condition about Helen: either she is agile or she is evidence‑driven. Once we eliminate one option, the other must hold.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>We are told that either Helen is agile or Helen is evidence‑driven; one of these must be true.</text>
<logic>agile(helen) v evidence_driven(helen)</logic>

The fact about River being attentive is not directly used for Helen, but it fulfills the requirement that every premise mentioned in the context is recorded.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>River is attentive, as a stated fact.</text>
<logic>attentive(river)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Ash is consistent likewise satisfies the completeness of the context; it does not play a role in the current derivation.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Ash is consistent, as a stated fact.</text>
<logic>consistent(ash)</logic>
</step>

Helen being analytical is the starting fact we need to trigger the productive rule, setting the chain in motion toward intuition.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Helen is analytical, as a stated fact.</text>
<logic>analytical(helen)</logic>
</step>

By combining Helen's analytical fact with the rule that analytical implies productive, we apply modus ponens to obtain productive(helen). This new fact is essential for the next step where we will address agility.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Helen being analytical and the rule linking analytical to productive, we conclude that Helen is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(helen)</logic>
</step>

Now we have productive(helen). The rule "~productive(helen) :- agile(helen)" tells us that agility would force non‑productivity. Using modus tollens, since Helen is productive, she cannot be agile, giving us ~agile(helen).
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since Helen is productive and agility would force non‑productivity, we infer that Helen cannot be agile.</text>
<logic>~agile(helen)</logic>
</step>

We have established ~agile(helen) and we also know from the disjunction that Helen is either agile or evidence‑driven. Because agility is ruled out, the only remaining way for the disjunction to be true is for Helen to be evidence‑driven, so we infer evidence_driven(helen).
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Given that Helen is not agile and the disjunction that she is either agile or evidence‑driven, we deduce that Helen must be evidence‑driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(helen)</logic>
</step>

With evidence_driven(helen) now established, the earlier rule that evidence‑driven leads to intuition allows us to apply modus ponens once more, concluding intuitive(helen).
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Because Helen is evidence‑driven and the rule that evidence‑driven implies intuitive, we infer that Helen is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(helen)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Helen is intuitive, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The engine prototype operates in performance mode. Whenever the engine prototype is efficient, it activates a warning. If the engine follows sequential control, it switches to diagnostic mode. Being in diagnostic mode guarantees that the engine remains operational. A functioning feedback system causes the engine to employ sequential control. When the engine is in performance mode, it becomes durable. If the engine is durable, it operates efficiently. If the engine is adjustable, it does not activate a warning. The engine prototype is either adjustable or equipped with a feedback system (or both).
examples.question: Is the engine prototype innovative?
examples.completion:

We are asked whether the engine prototype possesses the quality of being innovative. To answer, we must see if the given facts and rules allow us to infer the predicate innovative for the engine prototype, or its negation.

<logic>warning_active(engine_prototype) :- efficient(engine_prototype)</logic>
</step>

This conditional tells us that sequential_control leads to diagnostic_mode. It establishes a chain that can later be used to derive operational status, which may be relevant for other inferences.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the engine follows sequential control, it switches to diagnostic mode.</text>
<logic>diagnostic_mode(engine_prototype) :- sequential_control(engine_prototype)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that diagnostic_mode guarantees the engine stays operational. This provides a way to move from a control-related property to a health-related one, expanding the network of derivable facts.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the engine is in diagnostic mode, it remains operational.</text>
<logic>operational(engine_prototype) :- diagnostic_mode(engine_prototype)</logic>
</step>

The rule that a feedback_system causes sequential_control connects a hardware feature to a control strategy. By linking these, we can later propagate effects from the feedback system through the chain we have already identified.

<logic>sequential_control(engine_prototype) :- feedback_system(engine_prototype)</logic>

This statement shows that being in performance_mode makes the engine durable. It starts a chain from the known fact (performance_mode) toward durability, which will be useful for subsequent steps.

<logic>durable(engine_prototype) :- performance_mode(engine_prototype)</logic>
</step>

If the engine is durable, it becomes efficient. Together with PREMISE_5, this creates a loop where performance_mode leads to durability, which in turn yields efficiency, and efficiency loops back to warning activation via PREMISE_1.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If the engine is durable, it operates efficiently.</text>
<logic>efficient(engine_prototype) :- durable(engine_prototype)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that an adjustable engine does not trigger a warning gives us a way to obtain a negated warning predicate. It will be relevant when we consider the disjunction that guarantees either adjustability or a feedback system.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If the engine is adjustable, it does not activate a warning.</text>
<logic>~warning_active(engine_prototype) :- adjustable(engine_prototype)</logic>

The explicit fact that the engine prototype is in performance mode supplies the starting point for the durability and efficiency chain described in PREMISE_5 and PREMISE_6. This fact is the only concrete property we begin with.

<text>The engine prototype is set to performance mode.</text>
<logic>performance_mode(engine_prototype)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Engine_prototype is innovative: no rule or disjunction has "innovative" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(innovative(engine_prototype))</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Engine_prototype is not innovative.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~innovative(engine_prototype))</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="derived">
<text>Neither Engine_prototype is innovative nor Engine_prototype is not innovative can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(innovative(engine_prototype))</logic>
</step>

examples.context: Volcanic ash is known to be diligent. Whenever something is diligent, it is optimistic. If something is optimistic, then it is not ruled out that it could be either not optimistic or meticulous; in fact, the situation is captured by the statement that either ash is not optimistic or ash is meticulous. Any meticulous thing is relaxed, and any relaxed thing is methodical. Whenever something is methodical, it becomes ambitious, and any ambitious entity is perceptive. All these relationships hold for ash.
examples.question: Is it true that ash is not perceptive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the statement that ash is not perceptive holds. To answer, we must see if the chain of given facts and rules can produce the property perceptive for ash, because if ash is perceptive, the negation would be false. The context supplies a network of conditionals that we can follow step by step.

This rule tells us that methodical behavior guarantees ambition. It will become useful later when we manage to show that ash is methodical, allowing us to move forward to ambition and eventually perceptiveness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If ash is methodical, then ash is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(ash) :- methodical(ash)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that being relaxed automatically makes something methodical. Once we can prove ash is relaxed, we will be able to invoke this rule to get methodical, which is needed for the ambition chain.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If ash is relaxed, then ash is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(ash) :- relaxed(ash)</logic>
</step>

The diligent-to-optimistic rule gives us a direct way to turn the initial fact about ash's diligence into optimism. Optimism will later interact with the disjunction to give us meticulousness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If ash is diligent, then ash is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(ash) :- diligent(ash)</logic>
</step>

This rule links ambition to perceptiveness, the final property we care about. After we have established ambition, we can immediately infer perceptiveness, completing the proof.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If ash is ambitious, then ash is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(ash) :- ambitious(ash)</logic>
</step>

The meticulous-to-relaxed rule bridges the gap between meticulousness (which we will obtain from the disjunction) and relaxedness, which then leads to methodicalness via another rule.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If ash is meticulous, then ash is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(ash) :- meticulous(ash)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either ash is not optimistic or ash is meticulous. Since we will soon have ash optimistic, the only way for the disjunction to stay true is for ash to be meticulous, giving us a crucial stepping stone.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either ash is not optimistic or ash is meticulous.</text>
<logic>~optimistic(ash) v meticulous(ash)</logic>
</step>

The only explicit fact we start with is ash's diligence. This fact is the seed from which we will generate optimism, and subsequently the entire chain of properties.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Ash is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(ash)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that ash is diligent together with the rule that diligence implies optimism, we can directly assert that ash is optimistic. This gives us the positive side of the disjunction needed for the next step.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>From ash being diligent and the rule that diligent implies optimistic, we infer that ash is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(ash)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know ash is optimistic, the disjunction "not optimistic or meticulous" can only be satisfied by the second option, so we infer that ash must be meticulous. This deduction follows the principle of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>Given ash is optimistic and the disjunction that either ash is not optimistic or ash is meticulous, we conclude that ash must be meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(ash)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained ash meticulous, we apply the meticulous‑to‑relaxed rule, concluding that ash is relaxed. This moves us one link forward in the chain toward methodicalness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Since ash is meticulous and meticulous implies relaxed, we derive that ash is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(ash)</logic>
</step>

With ash relaxed, the relaxed‑to‑methodical rule tells us that ash is methodical. This satisfies the antecedent of the ambition rule introduced earlier.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because ash is relaxed and relaxed implies methodical, we obtain that ash is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(ash)</logic>
</step>

Since ash is now methodical, the methodical‑to‑ambitious rule allows us to claim ash is ambitious. Ambition is the direct precursor to perceptiveness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>From ash being methodical and the rule that methodical implies ambitious, we deduce that ash is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(ash)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using the ambition‑to‑perceptive rule, we turn ash's ambition into perceptiveness, establishing that ash is perceptive.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>With ash ambitious and the rule that ambitious leads to perceptive, we establish that ash is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(ash)</logic>
</step>

We derived Ash is perceptive, so the conclusion Ash is not perceptive is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is Nell sharp?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Nell possesses the sharp property. The goal is to derive sharp(nell) from the information provided in the context and the logical rules.

The conditional "if Nell is not supportive then Nell is sharp" will be crucial because it lets us turn a negated supportive status into the desired sharpness. We keep this rule in mind for later use.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Nell is not supportive, then she is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(nell) :- ~supportive(nell)</logic>
</step>

The rule "if Nell is supportive then Nell is not scholarly" connects Nell's supportiveness with her scholarly status. This relationship will help us reason about whether Nell can be supportive at all.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Nell is supportive, then she is not scholarly.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(nell) :- supportive(nell)</logic>
</step>

Kira being experienced is a fact, but it does not directly involve Nell. We note it anyway because it participates in other rules that could affect the overall knowledge base.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Kira is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(kira)</logic>
</step>

Rowan's skillful nature is recorded as a fact. Like the previous fact, it does not directly touch on Nell, yet it is part of the complete set of premises we must acknowledge.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Rowan is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(rowan)</logic>
</step>

Nell's scholarly status is explicitly given. This fact will interact with the rule about supportive implying not scholarly, allowing us to draw a conclusion about her supportiveness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Nell is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(nell)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Nell is scholarly and we have the rule that supportive leads to not scholarly, we can apply the contrapositive: if someone is scholarly, they cannot be supportive. Hence, we conclude that Nell is not supportive. This step uses a form of modus tollens to flip the implication.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From Nell being scholarly and the rule that supportive implies not scholarly, we infer that Nell cannot be supportive.</text>
<logic>~supportive(nell)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Nell is not supportive, we can now invoke the rule from PREMISE_1: not supportive implies sharp. By applying modus ponens, we directly infer that Nell is sharp.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Since Nell is not supportive, the rule linking non‑supportiveness to sharpness gives us that Nell is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(nell)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Nell is sharp, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Peter is industrious. Rose is patient. Marlowe is responsible, and Peter is also responsible. Whenever someone is responsible, that person is consistent; in particular, if Marlowe is responsible then Marlowe is consistent. Any consistent individual is industrious, so if Marlowe is consistent then Marlowe is industrious. Being industrious guarantees optimism, meaning if Marlowe is industrious then Marlowe is optimistic. If someone is patient then they are strong, so Rose being patient makes Rose strong. If someone is enthusiastic then they are well prepared, giving a rule that enthusiastic Rose would be well prepared. Finally, either Rose is not strong or Rose is enthusiastic.
examples.question: Is it true that Marlowe is not optimistic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to evaluate whether Marlowe lacks optimism. To answer, we must examine the chain of properties that connect Marlowe's known responsibilities to optimism. The goal is to see if the premises force Marlowe to be optimistic, which would make the negated claim false.

The rule linking responsibility to consistency is essential because it starts the chain that could lead to optimism. Recognizing that Marlowe is responsible gives us a foothold to apply this conditional.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Marlowe is responsible, then Marlowe is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(marlowe) :- responsible(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

The second rule moves us from consistency to industriousness, continuing the progression toward optimism. It shows that once we have consistency, we can automatically obtain industriousness for the same individual.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Marlowe is consistent, then Marlowe is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(marlowe) :- consistent(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

The third rule is the final link: it tells us that any industrious person must also be optimistic. This rule will allow us to reach the target property if we can establish industriousness for Marlowe.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Marlowe is industrious, then Marlowe is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(marlowe) :- industrious(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

Peter being industrious is a factual statement that, while not directly about Marlowe, demonstrates that the property of industriousness is present in the domain. It reinforces that the predicate is meaningful in this context.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Peter is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(peter)</logic>
</step>

Rose's patient status is another factual piece that supports other rules about strength and enthusiasm, though it does not affect Marlowe's optimism. Including it satisfies the requirement to state all given facts.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Rose is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(rose)</logic>
</step>

Peter's responsibility mirrors Marlowe's responsibility and shows that the responsibility predicate applies to multiple individuals. This helps confirm the relevance of the responsibility‑to‑consistency rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Peter is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(peter)</logic>
</step>

Marlowe being responsible is the crucial fact that activates the first rule. Without this fact, we could not start the chain that ultimately yields optimism.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Marlowe is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

From Marlowe's responsibility and the rule that any responsible entity is consistent, we can directly infer that Marlowe is consistent. This step uses existential instantiation to apply the rule to the specific individual Marlowe.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Marlowe is responsible and any responsible person is consistent, we infer that Marlowe is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

Having established Marlowe's consistency, we now invoke the second rule: a consistent individual is industrious. By modus ponens, this yields Marlowe's industriousness, moving us one step closer to optimism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because Marlowe is consistent and a consistent person is industrious, we conclude that Marlowe is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

With Marlowe confirmed as industrious, the universal rule that every industrious being is optimistic allows us to deduce Marlowe's optimism. This universal instantiation completes the logical chain from responsibility to optimism.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Given that Marlowe is industrious and any industrious individual is optimistic, we deduce that Marlowe is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

We derived Marlowe is optimistic, so the conclusion Marlowe is not optimistic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In this laboratory scenario, the reaction vessel is known to be stable. Whenever a reaction vessel is stable, it is also unstable. Whenever a reaction vessel is highly reactive, it becomes electrophilic. If a reaction vessel is not nucleophilic, then it is catalytic. If a reaction vessel is nucleophilic, then it is not electrophilic. Moreover, either the vessel is not unstable or it is highly reactive. No other information is provided.
examples.question: Based on the information, can we conclude that the reaction vessel is wise?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the reaction vessel can be said to be wise. To answer this, we must see if any of the supplied facts or conditional statements allow us to infer the predicate wise for the vessel.

I need to determine whether Reaction_vessel is wise can be derived from the given premises.

The first conditional tells us that stability implies instability for the vessel. This is relevant because we already know the vessel is stable, so it will let us derive that the vessel is also unstable, should we need that later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any stable reaction vessel is also unstable.</text>
<logic>unstable(reaction_vessel) :- stable(reaction_vessel)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links high reactivity to electrophilicity. Although we do not yet have information about the vessel being highly reactive, keeping this relationship in mind is important for any chain that might eventually produce the target property.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any highly reactive reaction vessel is electrophilic.</text>
<logic>electrophilic(reaction_vessel) :- highly_reactive(reaction_vessel)</logic>
</step>

The third conditional says that lacking nucleophilicity guarantees catalytic behavior. This gives us a way to obtain the catalytic property if we can ever establish that the vessel is not nucleophilic.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any reaction vessel that is not nucleophilic is catalytic.</text>
<logic>catalytic(reaction_vessel) :- ~nucleophilic(reaction_vessel)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule provides the converse: being nucleophilic ensures the vessel is not electrophilic. This will be useful for checking consistency if we ever derive nucleophilicity from other premises.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any nucleophilic reaction vessel is not electrophilic.</text>
<logic>~electrophilic(reaction_vessel) :- nucleophilic(reaction_vessel)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact confirms that the reaction vessel is stable. Combined with PREMISE_1, we can immediately infer that the vessel is also unstable, but neither of these properties mentions wisdom.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the reaction vessel is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(reaction_vessel)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Reaction_vessel is wise: no rule or disjunction has "wise" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(wise(reaction_vessel))</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Reaction_vessel is not wise.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~wise(reaction_vessel))</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">
<text>Neither Reaction_vessel is wise nor Reaction_vessel is not wise can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(wise(reaction_vessel))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: In the game world, the adventurer, the warrior, and the mage are each known for their courage. Specifically, the adventurer is brave, the warrior is brave, and the mage is brave. The design rules state that whenever a character is brave, they become decisive; thus the adventurer's bravery makes them decisive. Another rule says that any decisive character is active, so the adventurer would be active if they are decisive. A further rule declares that if the adventurer is content, then they are not active. No other rules, disjunctions, or defaults are mentioned.
examples.question: Is the adventurer adaptable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide if the adventurer possesses the property of being adaptable. To answer, we must examine all the facts and rules that involve the adventurer and see whether they force adaptability one way or the other.

I need to determine whether Adventurer is is adaptable can be derived from the given premises.

The first rule ties the adventurer's decisiveness to their activity level. Knowing this connection is important because activity could be a factor that influences adaptability, even though we do not yet know if the adventurer is decisive.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the adventurer is decisive, then the adventurer is active.</text>
<logic>is_active(adventurer) :- is_decisive(adventurer)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that bravery leads directly to decisiveness. This creates a chain: if we can establish bravery, we can infer decisiveness, and then use the previous rule to talk about activity. It therefore links a known fact to a later condition.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being brave guarantees that the adventurer is decisive.</text>
<logic>is_decisive(adventurer) :- is_brave(adventurer)</logic>
</step>

The third rule introduces a potential conflict: if the adventurer happens to be content, they must be not active. This opposite outcome to the first rule means that the adventurer could be either active or not active, depending on whether they are content, which we have no information about.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Whenever the adventurer is content, the adventurer is not active.</text>
<logic>~is_active(adventurer) :- is_content(adventurer)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the adventurer is brave activates the chain described in the second rule, allowing us to conclude that the adventurer is decisive. With decisiveness established, the first rule now tells us the adventurer would be active—unless the content rule interferes.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The adventurer is brave.</text>
<logic>is_brave(adventurer)</logic>
</step>

The warrior being brave is stated, but it does not involve the adventurer and therefore does not affect the query about adaptability. It is included to satisfy the requirement of mentioning every fact from the premise list.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The warrior is brave.</text>
<logic>is_brave(warrior)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the mage’s bravery is a factual detail that does not interact with the adventurer's properties relevant to adaptability. It is recorded to keep the context complete.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The mage is brave.</text>
<logic>is_brave(mage)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Adventurer is is adaptable: no rule or disjunction has "is_adaptable" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(is_adaptable(adventurer))</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Adventurer is not is adaptable.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~is_adaptable(adventurer))</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived">
<text>Neither Adventurer is is adaptable nor Adventurer is not is adaptable can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(is_adaptable(adventurer))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: Patient Ash is thorough. Patient Dawn is thorough and also seasoned. Patient Kent is thorough and seasoned as well. Patient Simon is receptive. If a person is accountable, then they are informed, and being accountable follows from being seasoned. Moreover, being seasoned makes a person accountable. Finally, if someone is purposeful, then they are not committed. Additionally, either Patient Ash is purposeful or Patient Ash is seasoned, and either Patient Simon is not insightful or Patient Simon is assertive.
examples.question: Is patient_ash informed?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Patient Ash possesses the informed property. The query hinges on a chain of implications that start from the known facts about thoroughness and seasoned status, moving through commitment, purposefulness, accountability, and finally informedness. By following the logical dependencies step by step, we can establish the truth of the statement.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any thorough individual is also committed; thus, because Patient Ash is thorough, we can infer Patient Ash is committed.</text>
<logic>committed(patient_ash) :- thorough(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is accountable then they are informed; this will be used later once we establish accountability for Patient Ash.</text>
<logic>informed(patient_ash) :- accountable(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>According to the rule, being seasoned implies accountability, so if we can show Patient Ash is seasoned we will get accountability.</text>
<logic>accountable(patient_ash) :- seasoned(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that a purposeful person cannot be committed; it will let us eliminate the purposeful option later.</text>
<logic>~committed(patient_ash) :- purposeful(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>We know that either Patient Ash is purposeful or Patient Ash is seasoned.</text>
<logic>purposeful(patient_ash) v seasoned(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Patient Dawn is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(patient_dawn)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Patient Kent is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(patient_kent)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Patient Dawn is seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(patient_dawn)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Patient Kent is seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(patient_kent)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Patient Simon is receptive.</text>
<logic>receptive(patient_simon)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Patient Ash is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

Because the context tells us that any thorough individual is also committed, and we have a direct fact that Patient Ash is thorough, we can immediately label Patient Ash as committed. This uses the straightforward forward inference from a property to another guaranteed by the rule.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>From the fact that Patient Ash is thorough and the rule linking thoroughness to commitment, we derive that Patient Ash is committed.</text>
<logic>committed(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

The rule that a purposeful person cannot be committed says that if someone were purposeful, they would not be committed. Since we have just shown Patient Ash is committed, the only way to keep the rule consistent is to reject the possibility that Patient Ash is purposeful. Hence we derive that Patient Ash is not purposeful.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Combining Patient Ash being committed with the rule that a purposeful person cannot be committed yields the conclusion that Patient Ash is not purposeful.</text>
<logic>~purposeful(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provided in the context states that Patient Ash is either purposeful or seasoned. Having concluded that Patient Ash cannot be purposeful, the disjunction forces the alternative to be true, namely that Patient Ash must be seasoned. This is a classic case of eliminating one disjunct to affirm the other.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,5">
<text>Given that Patient Ash is not purposeful and the disjunction that Patient Ash is either purposeful or seasoned, we infer that Patient Ash must be seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

There is a rule linking seasoned status to accountability: all seasoned individuals are accountable. Since we have just established that Patient Ash is seasoned, we can apply this rule and conclude that Patient Ash is accountable. This moves us one step closer to the target property.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,3">
<text>Since Patient Ash is seasoned, the rule that seasoned individuals are accountable lets us conclude Patient Ash is accountable.</text>
<logic>accountable(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule that accountability leads to being informed tells us that any accountable person is informed. With Patient Ash now known to be accountable, we can directly infer that Patient Ash is informed, satisfying the query.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,2">
<text>With Patient Ash accountable, the rule linking accountability to being informed allows us to assert that Patient Ash is informed.</text>
<logic>informed(patient_ash)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Patient_ash is informed, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the chess club, Elliot, Emma, and Sky are all known to be competitive players. Elliot and Sky also happen to be relaxed after their matches, while Sasha is recognized as trustworthy. The club’s guidelines state several conditional relationships: if Sasha is strong then Sasha is dedicated; being trustworthy guarantees that Sasha is prepared; whenever Emma is relaxed, she becomes proficient; if Emma is competitive, she is rational; and if Emma is rational, she is relaxed. Additionally, the club notes a logical alternative concerning Sasha: either Sasha is not prepared or Sasha is strong. These facts and rules together form the basis for reasoning about the players’ abilities.
examples.question: Is Emma not proficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Emma lacks proficiency. The goal is to see if the available facts and conditional rules force Emma to be proficient, which would make the negation false.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Emma is relaxed then she is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(emma) :- relaxed(emma)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Emma is competitive then she is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(emma) :- competitive(emma)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Emma is rational then she is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(emma) :- rational(emma)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Elliot is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(elliot)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Sky is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(sky)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Elliot is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(elliot)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Sky is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(sky)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Sasha is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(sasha)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Emma is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(emma)</logic>
</step>

Emma is explicitly stated to be competitive, and the club’s rule tells us that any competitive Emma must be rational. By applying this rule to the fact about Emma, we can safely conclude that Emma is rational. This step uses existential instantiation of the competitive fact together with the conditional rule.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>From Emma being competitive we infer that Emma is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(emma)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Emma is rational, we now turn to the rule linking rationality to relaxation. The rule says that a rational Emma is necessarily relaxed, so we can directly infer Emma's relaxed status. This inference follows the classic modus ponens pattern: the antecedent holds, therefore the consequent holds.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>From Emma being rational we infer that Emma is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(emma)</logic>
</step>

With Emma confirmed as relaxed, we can invoke the rule that a relaxed Emma becomes proficient. The rule is universally applicable to any relaxed individual, and since Emma meets the condition, we deduce her proficiency. This step is an instance of universal instantiation of the relaxed‑to‑proficient rule.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>From Emma being relaxed we infer that Emma is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(emma)</logic>
</step>

We derived Emma is proficient, so the conclusion Emma is not proficient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

The fact that Bridge Gamma is well planned is the cornerstone of our chain of reasoning. Through the second rule, this fact lets us infer that Bridge Gamma is expertly designed, which then triggers the first rule to conclude that it is on schedule.

examples.context: Climber Hazel is experienced. Whenever Hazel is not physically fit, she is well prepared. If Hazel is physically fit, then she is not experienced.
examples.question: Is climber Hazel well prepared?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether climber Hazel is well prepared. The problem gives us a fact about her experience and two conditional statements linking her physical fitness to both experience and preparation.

The first conditional tells us that lacking physical fitness guarantees preparation. This will become useful once we know whether Hazel is physically fit or not, because it directly connects the property we care about—being well prepared—to the opposite of fitness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Hazel is not physically fit, then she is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(climber_hazel) :- ~physically_fit(climber_hazel)</logic>
</step>

The second conditional links physical fitness to a lack of experience. Since we already have a statement about Hazel's experience, this rule will allow us to reason about her fitness by considering the contrapositive of the implication.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Hazel is physically fit, then she is not experienced.</text>
<logic>~experienced(climber_hazel) :- physically_fit(climber_hazel)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Hazel is experienced gives us a concrete starting point. Knowing that she possesses the experienced property will let us test the condition in the second rule and see what it implies about her fitness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>We are told that Hazel is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(climber_hazel)</logic>
</step>

Because the rule says that if Hazel were physically fit she would not be experienced, and we know she is experienced, we can apply modus tollens: the consequent is false, so the antecedent must also be false. Hence Hazel cannot be physically fit, giving us the conclusion that she is not physically fit.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>From Hazel being experienced and the rule that physical fitness would make her not experienced, we infer that Hazel cannot be physically fit.</text>
<logic>~physically_fit(climber_hazel)</logic>
</step>

<logic>steady(dragon_fist)</logic>
</step>

We now have steady for the dragon_fist, which appears in the disjunction "not steady or fluid". Because steady is true, the first alternative (~steady) is false, forcing the second alternative (fluid) to be true. Thus we deduce that the dragon_fist is fluid.

examples.context: In the composition, the melody line is reliable. Whenever a melody line is reliable, it is also harmonious. The music theory notes also state that the melody line is either not harmonious, or it is improvisational.
examples.question: Is it the case that the melody line is not improvisational?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the melody line lacks improvisational ability. To answer this, we must trace how the given musical properties interact and see what they imply about improvisation.

The first premise tells us that reliability guarantees harmony for the melody line. Recognizing this conditional link is essential because it lets us turn a fact about reliability into a fact about being harmonious later on.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any reliable melody line is also harmonious.</text>
<logic>harmonious(melody_line) :- reliable(melody_line)</logic>
</step>

The second premise offers an either-or situation: either the melody line is not harmonious, or it possesses improvisational skill. This disjunction will become useful once we know whether the melody line is harmonious, allowing us to eliminate one side and derive the other.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The statement that the melody line is either not harmonious or improvisational.</text>
<logic>~harmonious(melody_line) v improvisational(melody_line)</logic>
</step>

The third premise explicitly states that the melody line is reliable. Having a concrete fact about reliability gives us a solid starting point for applying the conditional rule from Premise 1.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the melody line is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(melody_line)</logic>
</step>

Because we know the melody line is reliable (Premise 3) and we have the rule that reliability implies harmony (Premise 1), we can directly infer that the melody line is harmonious. This step follows the standard modus ponens pattern, converting a condition and its antecedent into its consequent.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From the reliability of the melody line and the rule, we infer that the melody line is harmonious.</text>
<logic>harmonious(melody_line)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the melody line is harmonious, we can revisit the either-or statement from Premise 2. Since the first alternative ('not harmonious') is false, the disjunction forces the second alternative to be true, giving us that the melody line is improvisational. This reasoning uses disjunctive syllogism to eliminate the impossible option.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Using the fact that the melody line is harmonious together with the either-or statement, we deduce that the melody line is improvisational.</text>
<logic>improvisational(melody_line)</logic>
</step>

We derived Melody_line is improvisational, so the conclusion Melody_line is not improvisational is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the workshop, the gearbox is known to be durable. Whenever a component is durable, it is also responsive. The gearbox is either not responsive or it is optimized. Moreover, the gearbox is either not optimized or it is efficient.
examples.question: Is the gearbox not efficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the gearbox fails to be efficient. The goal is to evaluate the statement that the gearbox is not efficient based on the given facts and logical relationships.

The first premise links durability to responsiveness, telling us that any part that is durable automatically has the responsive property. This connection will let us turn the fact about durability into a fact about responsiveness later on.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any durable component is also responsive.</text>
<logic>responsive(gearbox) :- durable(gearbox)</logic>
</step>

The second premise presents a choice: the gearbox cannot be both responsive and unoptimized at the same time; it must be either not responsive or optimized. This disjunction will become useful once we know whether the gearbox is responsive.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The gearbox is either not responsive or it is optimized.</text>
<logic>~responsive(gearbox) v optimized(gearbox)</logic>
</step>

The third premise gives another alternative: the gearbox is either not optimized or it is efficient. Once we determine the optimization status, we can deduce the efficiency status from this statement.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the gearbox is not optimized or it is efficient.</text>
<logic>~optimized(gearbox) v efficient(gearbox)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise directly tells us that the gearbox is durable, providing the concrete fact we need to start the chain of reasoning.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The gearbox is durable.</text>
<logic>durable(gearbox)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that the gearbox is durable with the rule that durability implies responsiveness, we can apply modus ponens. Because the condition of the rule is satisfied, we conclude that the gearbox is responsive.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the gearbox being durable and the rule that durability implies responsiveness, we infer that the gearbox is responsive.</text>
<logic>responsive(gearbox)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the gearbox is responsive, we look back at the second premise. The disjunction says the gearbox is either not responsive or optimized; since the 'not responsive' option is false, the only way for the statement to hold is that the gearbox must be optimized. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since the gearbox is responsive, the disjunction that it is either not responsive or optimized forces it to be optimized.</text>
<logic>optimized(gearbox)</logic>
</step>

With the gearbox confirmed as optimized, we turn to the third premise. That premise states the gearbox is either not optimized or efficient. The 'not optimized' alternative is contradicted by the fact we just derived, so the remaining alternative must be true: the gearbox is efficient.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given the gearbox is optimized, the disjunction that it is either not optimized or efficient forces it to be efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(gearbox)</logic>
</step>

We derived Gearbox is efficient, so the conclusion Gearbox is not efficient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

Whenever the glaze expert is passionate, she turns experimental, so enthusiasm creates curiosity. Whenever the glaze expert is experimental, she becomes seasoned, thus curiosity produces wisdom. Either the glaze expert is not malleable, or she is logical; this disjunction captures a choice between lack of adaptability and rationality. Either the kiln master is not vigorous, or he is deadline_driven, expressing a similar alternative for energy and goal orientation.
examples.question: Is the glaze expert logical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several properties of the kiln master and the glaze expert, together with conditional relationships among those properties. Our goal is to determine whether the glaze expert can be concluded to be logical, i.e., rational.

The first rule tells us that wisdom guarantees adaptability for the glaze expert. This will be useful later when we manage to show she is seasoned, because it will let us infer she is malleable.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the glaze expert is seasoned, then the glaze expert is malleable.</text>

The second rule connects passion to experimentation. Since we already know the glaze expert is passionate, this rule provides a direct route to her being experimental.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the glaze expert is passionate, then the glaze expert is experimental.</text>
<logic>experimental(glaze_expert) :- passionate(glaze_expert)</logic>
</step>

The third rule creates a loop: if the glaze expert is experimental, she becomes seasoned. This reciprocal relationship will allow us to move from curiosity to wisdom and back again.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the glaze expert is experimental, then the glaze expert is seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(glaze_expert) :- experimental(glaze_expert)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either the glaze expert lacks adaptability or she is logical. Once we establish she is adaptable, the left side becomes false, forcing the right side to hold.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the glaze expert is not malleable, or the glaze expert is logical.</text>
<logic>~malleable(glaze_expert) v logical(glaze_expert)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the kiln master is market_focused establishes his competitiveness, which will later support other rules about energy and goals, though those are not needed for the query at hand.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The kiln master is market_focused.</text>
<logic>market_focused(kiln_master)</logic>
</step>

Knowing the glaze expert is passionate gives us the starting point for applying the rule that passion leads to experimentation.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The glaze expert is passionate.</text>
<logic>passionate(glaze_expert)</logic>
</step>

By applying the rule from PREMISE_2 to the fact from PREMISE_6, we infer that the glaze expert is experimental. This step transforms the given enthusiasm into curiosity, moving us forward in the chain of implications.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since the glaze expert is passionate and passion leads to being experimental, we conclude the glaze expert is experimental.</text>
<logic>experimental(glaze_expert)</logic>
</step>

With the experimental status now established, we invoke PREMISE_3, which says experimentation yields wisdom. Hence, the glaze expert becomes seasoned, setting up the condition needed for adaptability.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Given the glaze expert is experimental and being experimental leads to being seasoned, we infer the glaze expert is seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(glaze_expert)</logic>
</step>

Having shown the glaze expert is seasoned, we can now use PREMISE_1, which converts wisdom into malleability. This gives us the adaptability fact required for the disjunctive reasoning later.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Because the glaze expert is seasoned and seasoned implies being malleable, we deduce the glaze expert is malleable.</text>
<logic>malleable(glaze_expert)</logic>
</step>

We possess the fact that the glaze expert is malleable, which contradicts the left side of the disjunction in PREMISE_4. Since the left disjunct is false, the only way the disjunction can hold is if the right disjunct is true, so the glaze expert must be logical.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>We have that the glaze expert is malleable, and the statement that either the glaze expert is not malleable or is logical; the first alternative is false, so the glaze expert must be logical.</text>

</step>

Therefore Glaze_expert is logical, so the conclusion is True.

The fact that the industry giant is market confident is recorded, though it does not directly affect the startup firm’s properties. It is part of the overall knowledge base and could be used by other rules if needed.

We are explicitly told that the startup firm is financially solid. This concrete fact will allow us to eliminate the negated alternative in the first disjunction.

Having the firm financially solid and the disjunction that says either not financially solid or innovative, we can discard the 'not financially solid' side. The only remaining way for the disjunction to be true is that the firm must be innovative. Thus we derive that the startup firm is innovative.

Now that we know the startup firm is innovative, we look at the second disjunction: either not innovative or socially responsible. Consequently, we infer that the startup firm is socially responsible.

examples.context: In the grand chess tournament, the white knight, the black rook, the white bishop, and the black knight are all studied players. Whenever a piece is studied, it is also steady; thus any studied piece enjoys a steady style of play. However, if a piece is alert, then it cannot be steady, because alertness contradicts a steady approach. The white knight is studied. The white bishop is studied. The black knight is studied. The black rook is studied.
examples.question: Is it true that the black rook is not alert?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given information about several chess pieces and how their studied status relates to other qualities. Our goal is to determine whether the black rook can be described as not alert.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the black rook is studied, then it is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(black_rook) :- studied(black_rook)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the black rook is alert, then it is not steady.</text>
<logic>~steady(black_rook) :- alert(black_rook)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The white knight is studied.</text>
<logic>studied(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The white bishop is studied.</text>
<logic>studied(white_bishop)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The black knight is studied.</text>
<logic>studied(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The black rook is studied.</text>
<logic>studied(black_rook)</logic>
</step>

The context tells us explicitly that the black rook is studied, and we also have the rule that every studied piece is steady. By applying this conditional relationship, we can directly deduce that the black rook possesses the steady quality.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From the fact that the black rook is studied and the rule that studied pieces are steady, we infer that the black rook is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(black_rook)</logic>
</step>

We now have the steady property for the black rook, and we also know the rule that an alert black rook would make it not steady. Because the black rook is already steady, the possibility of it being alert would create a contradiction, so we must reject the alert condition, concluding the rook is not alert.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Since the black rook is steady and an alert black rook would have to be not steady, we conclude that the black rook cannot be alert.</text>
<logic>~alert(black_rook)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Black_rook is not alert, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the coastal research bay, a dolphin named Cyrus is known to be content with his environment. Whenever a dolphin is content, it is also brave, so Cyrus’s contentment makes him brave. If a dolphin shows persistent behavior, it will be considered rational, meaning persistence guarantees rationality for Cyrus. On the other hand, if a dolphin is alert, that alertness prevents it from being rational, so alertness would make Cyrus not rational. Additionally, the marine biologists have observed that either a dolphin is not brave or it is persistent; applied to Cyrus, this means that at least one of the statements "Cyrus is not brave" or "Cyrus is persistent" must hold.
examples.question: Based on the information about Cyrus, can we conclude that Cyrus is not alert?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the dolphin Cyrus is not alert, using the supplied facts and logical relationships. Our goal is to chain the given information to reach a statement about Cyrus's alertness.

The first rule tells us that contentness guarantees bravery for any subject, so it will be useful once we know Cyrus is content. This connects the fact about Cyrus's emotional state to a behavioral attribute we can later combine with other premises.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Cyrus is content, then Cyrus is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(cyrus) :- content(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links persistence to rationality, providing a pathway from a potential future conclusion (Cyrus being persistent) to a mental property (rational). We will need this link after we establish persistence.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Cyrus is persistent, then Cyrus is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(cyrus) :- persistent(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that alertness forces a creature to be not rational. This contrapositive relationship will allow us to turn a rationality conclusion into a negation of alertness later in the proof.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Cyrus is alert, then Cyrus is not rational.</text>
<logic>~rational(cyrus) :- alert(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a choice: either Cyrus lacks bravery or he exhibits persistence. This will become crucial once we have established bravery, because it will force the other disjunct—persistence—to be true.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Cyrus is not brave or Cyrus is persistent.</text>
<logic>~brave(cyrus) v persistent(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

The fact directly asserts that Cyrus is content, which activates the first rule and starts the chain of deductions toward bravery and beyond.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Cyrus is content.</text>
<logic>content(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

By combining the fact that Cyrus is content with the rule that content implies bravery, we can directly assert that Cyrus is brave. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens, moving us from emotional state to a behavioral trait.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Cyrus being content and the rule that content implies bravery, we infer that Cyrus is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Cyrus is brave, the disjunction "not brave or persistent" cannot be satisfied by the first option, because the first option is false. Therefore, the second option—Cyrus is persistent—must hold. This uses disjunctive syllogism to extract persistence from the given alternatives.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Given that Cyrus is brave and that either he is not brave or he is persistent, we deduce that Cyrus must be persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Cyrus is persistent, we apply the second rule, which tells us that persistence guarantees rationality. Thus, we can conclude Cyrus is rational, preparing the ground for the final step involving alertness.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Since Cyrus is persistent and persistence leads to rationality, we conclude that Cyrus is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

We now have Cyrus as rational, and the third rule says that if Cyrus were alert, he would be not rational. Since we already know he is rational, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to assert that Cyrus cannot be alert. This is a classic modus tollens move, converting rationality into the negation of alertness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Because Cyrus is rational and alertness would make him not rational, we infer that Cyrus cannot be alert.</text>
<logic>~alert(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Cyrus is not alert, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The engine called engine_max is responsive. Whenever something is eco_friendly, it is not high_performance. In addition, either the engine is not responsive, or it is high_performance. These statements capture all the given facts and rules.
examples.question: Is it true that engine_max is not eco_friendly?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the engine called engine_max fails to be eco_friendly. The available information includes a fact about its responsiveness, a conditional linking eco_friendly to the lack of high performance, and a disjunction involving responsiveness and high performance.

The conditional rule is important because it connects the property we are trying to refute (eco_friendly) with the opposite of high performance. Knowing this relationship will let us reason about eco_friendliness once we learn about high performance.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule tells us that any eco_friendly object cannot be high_performance.</text>
<logic>~high_performance(engine_max) :- eco_friendly(engine_max)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possible ways to describe the engine: either it is not responsive or it possesses high performance. This structure will become useful when we later establish the truth of one of the two alternatives.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>We are told that either the engine is not responsive or it is high_performance.</text>
<logic>~responsive(engine_max) v high_performance(engine_max)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the engine is responsive directly addresses the first part of the disjunction. By confirming responsiveness we can eliminate the "not responsive" option, narrowing the possibilities offered by the disjunction.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is explicitly stated that the engine is responsive.</text>
<logic>responsive(engine_max)</logic>
</step>

Having confirmed that engine_max is responsive, the clause "not responsive" in the disjunction is false. A disjunction is true only if at least one component is true, so the remaining option—high performance—must hold. Thus we deduce that engine_max is high_performance.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because the engine is responsive, the alternative "not responsive" is false, so the disjunction forces the engine to be high_performance.</text>
<logic>high_performance(engine_max)</logic>
</step>

The rule states that eco_friendly implies not high_performance. Taking the contrapositive, if something is high_performance, it cannot be eco_friendly. Since we have just shown that engine_max is high_performance, we can conclude that it is not eco_friendly.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since the engine is high_performance and eco_friendly would prevent high_performance, the engine cannot be eco_friendly.</text>
<logic>~eco_friendly(engine_max)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Engine_max is not eco friendly, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True
