The second premise links activity to the negation of strength. It provides a way to connect the concept of being active with a property we already know about patient_lia—her strength—so we can later reason about whether she can be active.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if patient_lia is active then she is not strong.</text>
<logic>~strong(patient_lia) :- active(patient_lia)</logic>
</step>

The third premise gives a disjunctive fact: patient_lia is either active or brave. This sets up a choice that we can resolve once we have information eliminating one of the alternatives.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The statement that patient_lia is either active or brave.</text>
<logic>active(patient_lia) v brave(patient_lia)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise is a straightforward fact that patient_lia is strong. This fact will interact with the rule about activity to let us rule out the possibility that she is active.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact that patient_lia is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(patient_lia)</logic>
</step>

Because patient_lia is strong, the conditional that activity would make her not strong cannot hold if she were indeed active. Using Modus Tollens, we infer that she must not be active; otherwise the rule would contradict the known fact of her strength.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From patient_lia being strong and the rule that active implies not strong, we infer that patient_lia cannot be active.</text>
<logic>~active(patient_lia)</logic>
</step>

Now we know that patient_lia is not active, and we also know she must be either active or brave. By Disjunctive Syllogism, the only remaining option is that she is brave. This step bridges the gap between the disjunction and a concrete property of patient_lia.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Given that patient_lia is not active and that she is either active or brave, we deduce that she must be brave.</text>
<logic>brave(patient_lia)</logic>
</step>

Having established that patient_lia is brave, we can apply the earlier rule that bravery entails supportiveness. Through Modus Ponens, we conclude that patient_lia is supportive.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Since patient_lia is brave and the rule that bravery leads to being supportive, we conclude she is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(patient_lia)</logic>
</step>

We derived Patient_lia is supportive, so the conclusion Patient_lia is not supportive is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Can we conclude that router_victor is not stable?

The disjunction "not responsive or optimized" gives us two alternatives concerning the router's state. It will be useful when we later have a definite statement about responsiveness, letting us deduce optimization.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either the router is not responsive or it is optimized.</text>
<logic>~responsive(router_victor) v optimized(router_victor)</logic>
</step>

The third disjunction offers a choice between the router not being optimized or being stable. Once we know the router is optimized, this clause will let us infer stability.
<step n="3" type="premise">

</step>

Stating that the switch is reachable supplies a concrete fact about the switch's network status. Although this fact does not directly involve the router, it fulfills the requirement to list all given facts.

The fact that the router is configured is the cornerstone for applying the rule about responsiveness. Having this fact explicitly mentioned ensures the rule can be activated.
<step n="5" type="premise">

</step>

Using the configured status of router_victor together with the rule that a configured router is responsive, we can confidently assert that the router is responsive. This step bridges the gap between a static configuration fact and a dynamic property.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">

<logic>responsive(router_victor)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the router is responsive, the disjunction "not responsive or optimized" can no longer be satisfied by the first alternative. Consequently, the only way for the statement to hold is for the router to be optimized, so we conclude it is optimized.

We derived Router_victor is stable, so the conclusion Router_victor is not stable is False.

Answer: False

examples.context: Team Alpha is disciplined. Whenever a team has high morale, it is also a team player. If a team is coachable, then it is not a team player. Either the team does not practice fair play, or it has high morale. Either the team is not disciplined, or it practices fair play.
examples.question: Is team_alpha coachable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a collection of facts and conditional statements about Team Alpha's attributes in a competitive sports setting. Our goal is to determine whether Team Alpha can be described as coachable based on the supplied information.

The rule linking high morale to being a team player will later allow us to turn a morale claim into a player‑type claim. Recognizing this connection is essential for propagating properties forward in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any team with high morale is also a team player.</text>
<logic>team_player(team_alpha) :- high_morale(team_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that being coachable forces the negation of the team‑player status. This contrapositive relationship will be used to derive a negative statement about coachability later on.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a team is coachable, then it is not a team player.</text>
<logic>~team_player(team_alpha) :- coachable(team_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction about fair play and high morale gives us an either‑or condition that can be resolved once we know one of the two components. It sets up a potential pathway to establish high morale.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the team does not practice fair play, or it has high morale.</text>
<logic>~fair_play(team_alpha) v high_morale(team_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The other disjunction involving discipline and fair play works similarly: if we can eliminate the possibility of not being disciplined, we will be forced to accept fair play. This will be the key step to start the chain of deductions.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the team is not disciplined, or it practices fair play.</text>
<logic>~disciplined(team_alpha) v fair_play(team_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Team Alpha is disciplined directly contradicts the left side of the previous disjunction, allowing us to activate a disjunctive syllogism. This concrete fact grounds the reasoning in reality.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Team Alpha is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(team_alpha)</logic>
</step>

Because Team Alpha is disciplined, the alternative "not disciplined" in the disjunction "not disciplined or fair play" is false. Disjunctive syllogism therefore lets us conclude that Team Alpha must practice fair play. This gives us a solid positive property to work with next.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,4">
<text>From the fact that Team Alpha is disciplined and the disjunction that either the team is not disciplined or it practices fair play, we infer that Team Alpha practices fair play.</text>
<logic>fair_play(team_alpha)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established fair play, we revisit the other disjunction which says either "not fair play" or "high morale". Since "not fair play" is false, the only remaining option is that Team Alpha has high morale. This step converts the fair‑play result into a morale claim.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given that Team Alpha practices fair play and the disjunction that either the team does not practice fair play or it has high morale, we conclude that Team Alpha has high morale.</text>
<logic>high_morale(team_alpha)</logic>
</step>

With high morale confirmed, we can apply the first rule: any high‑morale team is also a team player. By modus ponens, Team Alpha inherits the team‑player attribute. This creates the antecedent needed for the next inference.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Team Alpha has high morale and any high morale team is a team player, we deduce that Team Alpha is a team player.</text>
<logic>team_player(team_alpha)</logic>
</step>

We now have that Team Alpha is a team player, while the second rule states that if a team were coachable, it could not be a team player. Using modus tollens, the presence of the team‑player property forces the negation of coachability, so we infer that Team Alpha is not coachable.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because Team Alpha is a team player and if a team is coachable then it is not a team player, we infer that Team Alpha is not coachable.</text>
<logic>~coachable(team_alpha)</logic>
</step>

We derived Team_alpha is not coachable, so the conclusion Team_alpha is coachable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the pharmacy, the drug aspirin is known to be precise, and the drug ibuprofen is also precise. Whenever a medication is precise, it is automatically considered targeted, meaning its therapeutic action is directed toward a specific goal. On the other hand, any medication that is effective cannot be targeted; effectiveness guarantees that the drug lacks a targeted profile. These statements together describe how precision, targeting, and effectiveness relate for the two drugs.
examples.question: Is aspirin effective?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given information about two drugs, aspirin and ibuprofen, and how their properties of precision, targeting, and effectiveness interact. Our goal is to determine whether aspirin can be classified as effective based on the supplied facts and rules.

The rule linking precision to targeting is crucial because it allows us to move from a fact about precision to a conclusion about targeting. Recognizing this implication sets up the chain that will later involve aspirin.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any precise medication is also targeted.</text>
<logic>targeted(aspirin) :- precise(aspirin)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that effectiveness precludes a drug from being targeted. This negative relationship will be used to test whether aspirin can be effective once we know its targeting status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a medication is effective, then it is not targeted.</text>
<logic>~targeted(aspirin) :- effective(aspirin)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Ibuprofen is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(ibuprofen)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Aspirin is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(aspirin)</logic>
</step>

We start with the fact that aspirin is precise. Applying the first rule, which states that every precise drug is targeted, we deduce that aspirin must be targeted. This step bridges the factual premise to a new property that will be essential for the next inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Because aspirin is precise and every precise drug is targeted, we can conclude that aspirin is targeted.</text>
<logic>targeted(aspirin)</logic>
</step>

Having established that aspirin is targeted, we now invoke the second rule: an effective drug cannot be targeted. Since aspirin is indeed targeted, the only way to satisfy the rule is for aspirin not to be effective. This yields the conclusion that aspirin is not effective.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since aspirin is targeted and an effective drug cannot be targeted, we infer that aspirin is not effective.</text>
<logic>~effective(aspirin)</logic>
</step>

We derived Aspirin is not effective, so the conclusion Aspirin is effective is False.
</think>
Answer: False

Additionally, James's generosity leads to talent: any generous person like James is talented, and a cheerful person would be trustworthy, though these latter details are not needed for the current argument. Finally, the statement that either Simon is not diligent or Simon is brave captures a logical disjunction that will be used later.
examples.question: Is Simon principled?

The disjunction offers two possibilities about Simon: either he lacks diligence or he is brave. Knowing Simon's actual diligence will let us eliminate one side and possibly deduce his bravery.

examples.question: Is Tom intuitive?

The disjunction provides a logical alternative: either Tom lacks confidence or Tom is humble. When we later establish that Tom is confident, the only way the disjunction can hold is for Tom to be humble, giving us the humility needed for the next step.

The fact that Tom is reflective directly activates PREMISE_2, allowing us to obtain confidence for Tom. This factual anchor is essential for triggering the subsequent reasoning steps.

We have the statement that Tom is confident (Step 8) and the disjunction that either Tom is not confident or Tom is humble (Premise 5). Since the first disjunct is false, the only way the disjunction holds is if Tom is humble. Thus we deduce Tom's humility.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">

examples.context: In the garden, the rose named garden_rose is well fertilized. Whenever a rose is well fertilized, it becomes pruned. Any rose that is methodical is also inquisitive. If a rose is goal driven, then it is not inquisitive. Either the rose is not pruned, or it is methodical.

I need to determine whether Garden_rose is proactive can be derived from the given premises.

Understanding the link between being methodical and inquisitive is useful because it shows how one property can imply another. This rule will allow us to transfer any conclusion about methodical roses over to inquisitive ones, should we ever learn a rose is methodical.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any rose that is methodical is also inquisitive.</text>
<logic>inquisitive(garden_rose) :- methodical(garden_rose)</logic>
</step>

The connection between being well fertilized and becoming pruned tells us that the factual claim about garden_rose being well fertilized automatically gives it the pruned property. This conversion is a straightforward implication that will populate our knowledge base with a new attribute for the rose.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule indicates that a rose that is well fertilized will be pruned.</text>
<logic>pruned(garden_rose) :- well_fertilized(garden_rose)</logic>
</step>

The rule that a goal‑driven rose cannot be inquisitive provides a way to derive a negative property from a positive one. If we ever discover that garden_rose is goal driven, we could immediately conclude it is not inquisitive, which might interact with other rules.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if a rose is goal driven, it cannot be inquisitive.</text>

</step>

The explicit fact that garden_rose is well fertilized is the only concrete datum we start with. By applying Premise 2, this fact lets us infer that garden_rose is pruned, giving us a concrete attribute to work with in later reasoning.

<logic>well_fertilized(garden_rose)</logic>

<step n="5" type="derived">

The disjunction offers two possible ways to satisfy a condition: Pearl is either wise or grateful. Knowing which of these holds will be crucial for moving forward.

The fact that Olive is clever is included for completeness, though it does not directly affect Pearl's adaptability. It illustrates the broader set of knowledge in the scenario.

The fact that Pearl is analytical is the starting point for applying the analytical‑to‑relaxed rule. It gives us a concrete piece of information to work with.

By applying the rule from PREMISE_2 to the fact from PREMISE_6, we see that Pearl's analytical nature forces her to be relaxed. This new fact will interact with the rule about wisdom in the next step.

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">

With ~wise(Pearl) established and the disjunction from PREMISE_4 that says wise(Pearl) or grateful(Pearl), the only way for the disjunction to hold is for grateful(Pearl) to be true. Thus we derive Pearl's gratefulness.

Now that we know Pearl is grateful (STEP_9) and we have the conditional from PREMISE_1 linking gratefulness to adaptability, we can conclude Pearl is adaptable by straightforward implication.

Therefore Pearl is adaptable, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: In this construction crew, Nora is known to be consistent, and Mia is described as humble. Phoenix, the project manager, is rational. Whenever something is consistent, it is also astute, so Nora’s consistency makes her astute. Any articulate thing guarantees brilliance, which means that if Phoenix were articulate then Phoenix would be brilliant. If something is organized, it becomes competitive, so an organized Nora would be competitive. Rationality leads to determination, therefore Phoenix’s rationality implies that Phoenix is determined. If a person is scholarly then they are empathetic, and an empathetic person is vigorous. Moreover, if someone were courageous, they could not be humble; thus courageousness would negate humility. The crew also knows that either Mia is courageous or Mia is scholarly, and either Nora is not astute or Nora is organized, while either Phoenix is not determined or Phoenix is articulate. No other default rules apply.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Mia is vigorous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Mia possesses the property of being vigorous. The goal is to trace a chain of logical implications from the facts and rules provided until we either reach or fail to reach vigorous(mia). The premises include both direct facts about the individuals and conditional statements that let us derive new properties.

The first premise tells us that scholarly status guarantees empathy. This is important because empathy is a stepping stone toward vigor, so we keep this conditional in mind for later use.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Mia is scholarly, then she is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(mia) :- scholarly(mia)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links empathy directly to vigor. Once we have established that Mia is empathetic, this rule will allow us to immediately infer that she is vigorous.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If someone is empathetic, then they are vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(mia) :- empathetic(mia)</logic>
</step>

The third premise says that if Mia were courageous, she could not be humble. This inverse relationship will help us eliminate the possibility of courage by using Mia's known humility.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Mia were courageous, she would not be humble.</text>
<logic>~humble(mia) :- courageous(mia)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise provides a disjunction: Mia is either courageous or scholarly. This gives us two possible routes to follow, and once we rule out one, the other must hold.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Mia is courageous or Mia is scholarly.</text>
<logic>courageous(mia) v scholarly(mia)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise simply records that Nora is consistent, which is not directly needed for Mia's vigor but is part of the overall knowledge base.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Nora is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(nora)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise records that Phoenix is rational, another fact that belongs to the broader scenario but does not affect the current derivation about Mia.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Phoenix is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(phoenix)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise states that Mia is humble. This fact will interact with the courageous‑humble rule to let us dismiss courage as a possibility for Mia.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Mia is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(mia)</logic>
</step>

From Mia being humble and the rule that courageousness would prevent humility, we apply modus tollens: because the consequent (~humble) would be false, the antecedent (courageous) must also be false. Hence we conclude that Mia is not courageous. This eliminates one side of the disjunction from Premise 4.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Because Mia is humble and courageousness would make her not humble, we infer that Mia is not courageous.</text>
<logic>~courageous(mia)</logic>
</step>

Having established ~courageous(mia) and knowing that either courageous(mia) or scholarly(mia) must be true, we use disjunctive syllogism. Since the courageous alternative is ruled out, the only remaining option is that Mia is scholarly. This gives us the missing antecedent needed for the empathetic rule.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Given that Mia is not courageous and that either she is courageous or scholarly, we deduce that Mia must be scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(mia)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have scholarly(mia), we can invoke the rule from Premise 1. By modus ponens, scholarly status directly yields empathy, so we infer empathetic(mia). This moves us one step closer to vigor.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>From Mia being scholarly and the rule that scholarly implies empathetic, we obtain that Mia is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(mia)</logic>
</step>

With empathetic(mia) in hand, we apply the rule from Premise 2. Modus ponens again gives us vigorous(mia), achieving the desired property.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Since Mia is empathetic and empathy leads to vigor, we conclude that Mia is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(mia)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Mia is vigorous, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The rule about analytical individuals says that if Indie were analytical, then Indie would not be loyal. This conditional states that resilience leads to market_watching. It could help us infer market_watching for Leon if we ever establish that Leon is resilient.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a firm is resilient, then it is market_watching.</text>
<logic>market_watching(firm_leon) :- resilient(firm_leon)</logic>
</step>

According to this rule, innovation implies resilience. If we can show Leon is innovative, we could then infer resilience and subsequently market_watching via PREMISE_3.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a firm is innovative, then it is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(firm_leon) :- innovative(firm_leon)</logic>
</step>

The rule connects shrewdness to cooperativeness for consumers. It is relevant only to Consumer Keith, not to Firm Leon, so it will not help us prove Leon's cooperativeness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If a consumer is shrewd, then the consumer is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(consumer_keith) :- shrewd(consumer_keith)</logic>
</step>

This tells us that systematic firms are collaborative. If we can establish that Leon is systematic, we could then use PREMISE_2 to get solvency, but still not cooperativeness directly.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If a firm is systematic, then it is collaborative.</text>
<logic>collaborative(firm_leon) :- systematic(firm_leon)</logic>
</step>

Logical firms are innovative. Since we have the fact that Leon is logical (PREMISE_10), we can infer that Leon is innovative, opening the path to resilience and market_watching.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If a firm is logical, then it is innovative.</text>
<logic>innovative(firm_leon) :- logical(firm_leon)</logic>
</step>

If a firm is hardworking, it cannot be market_watching. This creates a potential conflict with the chain that would give Leon market_watching, but again it does not lead to cooperativeness.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>If a firm is hardworking, then it is not market_watching.</text>
<logic>~market_watching(firm_leon) :- hardworking(firm_leon)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Consumer Keith is risk_averse activates PREMISE_1, making Keith decisive, and also satisfies the left side of the disjunction about Keith, but it remains unrelated to Leon's cooperativeness.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Consumer Keith is risk_averse.</text>
<logic>risk_averse(consumer_keith)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Firm Leon is logical activates PREMISE_7, allowing us to infer that Leon is innovative, then resilient (PREMISE_4), and finally market_watching (PREMISE_3). However, none of these steps produce cooperativeness.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Firm Leon is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(firm_leon)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Firm_leon is cooperative: no rule or disjunction has "cooperative" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(cooperative(firm_leon))</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="derived">

</step>

<step n="13" type="derived">
<text>Neither Firm_leon is cooperative nor Firm_leon is not cooperative can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(cooperative(firm_leon))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: Ada, Fern, and Peter are marine researchers. All three are well prepared for their fieldwork. In this setting, any researcher who is well prepared is courageous. Moreover, any courageous researcher is disciplined. Also, any insightful researcher is methodical. Regarding Fern, either Fern is not disciplined or Fern is insightful. These statements capture every fact, rule, and the disjunction that were given.
examples.question: Based on the information provided, can we conclude that Fern is methodical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a small knowledge base about three marine researchers and several logical relationships among their attributes. Our goal is to determine whether the attribute 'methodical' can be proved for Fern using the supplied facts, rules, and the disjunction.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any being who is well prepared is also courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(fern) :- well_prepared(fern)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any courageous individual is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(fern) :- courageous(fern)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any insightful individual is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(fern) :- insightful(fern)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that Fern is either not disciplined or insightful.</text>
<logic>~disciplined(fern) v insightful(fern)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Ada is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(ada)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Peter is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(peter)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Fern is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(fern)</logic>
</step>

Fern is explicitly stated to be well prepared, and one of the universal rules tells us that every well‑prepared individual is courageous. By applying this rule to Fern we can safely assert that Fern possesses courage. This gives us the first intermediate fact needed for the later steps.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Fern being well prepared and the rule linking well prepared to courageous, we infer that Fern is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(fern)</logic>
</step>

Having established Fern's courage, we turn to the second universal rule which says that any courageous person is disciplined. Since Fern meets the antecedent of this rule, we can infer that Fern is disciplined. This new fact will interact with the disjunction in the next step.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Using Fern's courage and the rule that courage leads to discipline, we deduce that Fern is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(fern)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either Fern is not disciplined or Fern is insightful. Because we have just derived that Fern is disciplined, the first alternative becomes impossible, forcing the second alternative to hold. Consequently, we conclude that Fern must be insightful. This insight is crucial for obtaining the final desired property.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Given that Fern is disciplined and the disjunction that Fern is either not disciplined or insightful, we resolve that Fern must be insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(fern)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Fern is insightful, we can use the third universal rule stating that every insightful individual is methodical. Applying this rule to Fern yields the conclusion that Fern is methodical. This directly answers the original query.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Since Fern is insightful and the rule that insightful implies methodical, we conclude Fern is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(fern)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Fern is methodical, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Angel feels anxious. Luca is determined. Bree, Jade, Vera and Robin are all productive. Bree, Jade and Vera are not cautious. Whenever something is reflective, it is clever, so any reflective Robin would be clever. If something is well prepared then it is energetic, and any energetic thing becomes focused. Any clever entity is also proficient. Productive things are well prepared, therefore Robin, being productive, is well prepared. If something is cautious then it cannot be focused. Either Angel is not diplomatic or Angel is attentive. Either Luca is not relaxed or Luca is patient. Diplomatic follows from attentiveness for Angel, and impartial follows from attentiveness as well. Determined Luca becomes relaxed, and a relaxed Luca would be patient, matching the second disjunction. Reflective Robin follows from the fact that Robin is not cautious, because any non‑cautious entity is reflective. Energetic Robin follows from Robin being well prepared, and focused Robin follows from Robin being energetic. Proficient Robin follows from Robin being clever, which in turn follows from Robin being reflective.
examples.question: Is it true that Robin is not proficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Robin lacks proficiency. The goal is to see if we can derive Robin's proficiency from the given facts and rules. By chaining the appropriate implications, we will determine the truth value of the negated statement.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Robin is reflective, then Robin is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(robin) :- reflective(robin)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If something is well prepared, then it is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(robin) :- well_prepared(robin)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If something is energetic, then it is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(robin) :- energetic(robin)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If something is clever, then it is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(robin) :- clever(robin)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If something is productive, then it is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(robin) :- productive(robin)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If something is cautious, then it is not focused.</text>
<logic>~focused(robin) :- cautious(robin)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Angel is anxious.</text>
<logic>anxious(angel)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Luca is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(luca)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Bree is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(bree)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Jade is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(jade)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Vera is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(vera)</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="premise">
<text>Bree is not cautious.</text>
<logic>~cautious(bree)</logic>
</step>

<step n="13" type="premise">
<text>Jade is not cautious.</text>
<logic>~cautious(jade)</logic>
</step>

<step n="14" type="premise">
<text>Vera is not cautious.</text>
<logic>~cautious(vera)</logic>
</step>

<step n="15" type="premise">
<text>Robin is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(robin)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that Robin is productive. One of the supplied rules says that every productive individual is well prepared. Applying this rule directly yields that Robin is well prepared. This establishes the first link in the chain toward proficiency.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,5">
<text>From Robin being productive and the rule that productivity implies being well prepared, we infer that Robin is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(robin)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Robin is well prepared, we can use the rule that well‑preparedness guarantees being energetic. By modus ponens, the energetic property follows for Robin. This step moves us closer to the focus property needed later.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,2">
<text>Because Robin is well prepared, the rule linking well‑preparedness to energy lets us conclude that Robin is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(robin)</logic>
</step>

With Robin confirmed as energetic, the next rule tells us that any energetic entity becomes focused. Therefore, Robin inherits the focused attribute. At this point we have both focused and well‑prepared statuses for Robin.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,3">
<text>Since Robin is energetic, the rule that energetic things are focused gives us that Robin is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(robin)</logic>
</step>

The rule concerning cautiousness states that a cautious individual cannot be focused. Since we have established that Robin is focused, it would be contradictory for Robin to be cautious. Consequently, we infer that Robin is not cautious, which will be useful for obtaining reflectiveness.
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,6">
<text>Given that Robin is focused and the rule that a cautious entity cannot be focused, we deduce that Robin cannot be cautious.</text>
<logic>~cautious(robin)</logic>
</step>

A specific rule links the lack of cautiousness to reflectiveness: any entity that is not cautious is reflective. Because we have just shown Robin is not cautious, we can conclude that Robin is reflective. This provides the crucial premise for deriving cleverness.
<step n="20" type="derived" from="19">
<text>Having established that Robin is not cautious, we apply the rule that a non‑cautious entity is reflective, yielding that Robin is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(robin)</logic>
</step>

Reflectiveness triggers cleverness via another rule: every reflective thing is clever. Applying this to Robin gives us that Robin is clever. This step bridges the gap between the earlier properties and the final proficiency claim.
<step n="21" type="derived" from="20,1">
<text>From Robin being reflective and the rule that reflective things are clever, we obtain that Robin is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(robin)</logic>
</step>

The final rule in the chain states that cleverness leads to proficiency. Since we have just proven Robin is clever, we can directly infer that Robin is proficient. This completes the forward derivation needed to evaluate the query.
<step n="22" type="derived" from="21,4">
<text>Finally, because Robin is clever and clever things are proficient, we derive that Robin is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(robin)</logic>
</step>

We derived Robin is proficient, so the conclusion Robin is not proficient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Royal is meticulous. The legal principle says that whenever royal is prepared, royal is also polite. It further declares that if royal is adaptable then royal is not meticulous. Additionally, it is asserted that royal is either adaptable or prepared (or possibly both).
examples.question: Is royal polite?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether royal can be considered polite under the given information. The goal is to use the stated facts and logical relationships to derive a proof of politeness.

The rule linking preparation to politeness is crucial because it provides a direct way to obtain politeness once we establish that royal is prepared. We will later try to prove preparation.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If royal is prepared, then royal is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(royal) :- prepared(royal)</logic>
</step>

The second rule shows that adaptability would force royal to be non‑meticulous, which contradicts the known fact of meticulousness. This tension will allow us to rule out adaptability.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If royal is adaptable, then royal is not meticulous.</text>
<logic>~meticulous(royal) :- adaptable(royal)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Royal is either adaptable or prepared.</text>
<logic>adaptable(royal) v prepared(royal)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Royal is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(royal)</logic>
</step>

Royal is known to be meticulous, yet the rule says an adaptable royal would be not meticulous. Because the consequent of that conditional is false, modus tollens lets us infer that the antecedent (adaptable) must be false, so royal is not adaptable. This eliminates one side of the disjunction.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Because royal is meticulous and an adaptable royal would have to be not meticulous, we conclude that royal cannot be adaptable.</text>
<logic>~adaptable(royal)</logic>
</step>

Having established that royal is not adaptable, we turn to the disjunction that royal is either adaptable or prepared. Disjunctive syllogism tells us that if one disjunct is false, the other must hold, so royal must be prepared. This gives us the needed preparation fact.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Since royal is not adaptable and we know royal is either adaptable or prepared, it follows that royal must be prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(royal)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have prepared(royal) and the earlier rule that preparation implies politeness, modus ponens allows us to conclude that royal is polite. This directly satisfies the query.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Given that royal is prepared and that any prepared royal is polite, we infer that royal is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(royal)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Royal is polite, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the protected reserve, the river gazelle is known to be calm. The savanna elephant, on the other hand, is organized. Whenever a savanna elephant is on high alert, it makes wise decisions, so any elephant that is on high alert is also wise in its decisions. If a savanna elephant is on high alert, then it is wise; this captures the rule linking alertness to judiciousness. Any river gazelle that is calm is also peaceful, and any river gazelle that shares resources is fearless. Moreover, the reserve's conditions guarantee that the river gazelle is peaceful whenever it is calm, and that it becomes fearless whenever it shares resources. Regarding uncertainties, either the river gazelle is not peaceful or it shares resources, and either the savanna elephant is not organized or it is on high alert.
examples.question: Is it true that the savanna elephant is not wise in its decisions?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement that the savanna elephant is not wise in its decisions holds. The goal is to see if we can derive the opposite, namely that the elephant is indeed wise, using the information given about alerts and organization.

The premise connects alertness to wisdom for the savanna elephant, so if we ever learn that the elephant is on high alert, we will immediately obtain its wisdom. This link is essential for turning an alert fact into the desired judicious conclusion.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any savanna elephant that is on high alert will also be wise in its decisions.</text>
<logic>wise_decision(savanna_elephant) :- on_high_alert(savanna_elephant)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that at least one of two possibilities is true: either the elephant lacks organization or it is on high alert. Combined with other facts, this will allow us to eliminate the first alternative and secure the alert condition.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either the savanna elephant is not organized, or it is on high alert.</text>
<logic>~organized(savanna_elephant) v on_high_alert(savanna_elephant)</logic>
</step>

Stating that the river gazelle is calm gives background information but does not directly affect the savanna elephant's status. It is included to satisfy the requirement that all facts be mentioned in the context.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The river gazelle is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(river_gazelle)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the savanna elephant is organized directly opposes the first option in the disjunction from PREMISE_2. Knowing the elephant is organized enables us to discard the "not organized" alternative.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The savanna elephant is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(savanna_elephant)</logic>
</step>

Because we know the elephant is organized (PREMISE_4), the clause "not organized" in the disjunction cannot hold, leaving the only remaining possibility: the elephant must be on high alert. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism, turning the disjunction and a contradictory fact into a definitive assertion.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From the fact that the savanna elephant is organized and the disjunction that it is either not organized or on high alert, we infer that it must be on high alert.</text>
<logic>on_high_alert(savanna_elephant)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the elephant is on high alert (STEP_5), we can apply the rule from PREMISE_1. The implication tells us that any alert elephant is wise, so we conclude the savanna elephant is wise in its decisions. This step follows the modus ponens pattern, using a proven antecedent to obtain the consequent.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Because the savanna elephant is on high alert, the rule linking alertness to wisdom lets us conclude that the elephant is wise.</text>
<logic>wise_decision(savanna_elephant)</logic>
</step>

We derived Savanna_elephant is wise decision, so the conclusion Savanna_elephant is not wise decision is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is Ross healthy?

<logic>~capable(ross) :- open_minded(ross)</logic>

The third rule gives a direct link from health to the opposite of well‑preparedness. If we ever manage to derive that Ross is healthy, we can immediately conclude he is not well‑prepared.
<step n="3" type="premise">

<logic>~well_prepared(ross) :- healthy(ross)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction offers two alternatives: either Ross is not proactive or he is rational. Because we already know Ross is proactive, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the rational alternative to be true.
<step n="4" type="premise">

<logic>~proactive(ross) v rational(ross)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction presents another pair of alternatives: either Ross is not rational or he is capable. Once we have established Ross’s rationality, the negated rational alternative becomes false, forcing the capable alternative to hold.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>A second disjunction tells us that either Ross is not rational or Ross is capable.</text>
<logic>~rational(ross) v capable(ross)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Ross is proactive is the starting point for applying the first disjunction. It eliminates the ~proactive side and pushes the reasoning toward rationality.

Since Ross is proactive, the clause ‘~proactive’ in the first disjunction cannot be satisfied. To keep the disjunction true, the other side, rational(Ross), must be true. Thus we conclude Ross is rational.

<logic>rational(ross)</logic>

Having obtained rational(Ross), the second disjunction now has its left side (~rational) falsified. Therefore the right side, capable(Ross), must be true to preserve the truth of the disjunction. This gives us Ross’s capability.

<logic>capable(ross)</logic>
</step>

We now know Ross is capable. The rule '~capable :- open_minded' tells us that if Ross were open‑minded, he would have to be not capable. Since we have the opposite (Ross is capable), the antecedent (open‑minded) must be false, so Ross is not open‑minded.

<logic>~open_minded(ross)</logic>

The rule 'well_prepared :- ~open_minded' says that lacking open‑mindedness guarantees well‑preparedness. Because we have just derived that Ross is not open‑minded, we can directly infer that Ross is well‑prepared.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">

<logic>well_prepared(ross)</logic>
</step>

The rule '~well_prepared :- healthy' means that if Ross were healthy, he would have to be not well‑prepared. We have the opposite situation: Ross is well‑prepared. By modus tollens, the antecedent (healthy) cannot hold, so Ross is not healthy.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">

<logic>~healthy(ross)</logic>

We derived Ross is not healthy, so the conclusion Ross is healthy is False.

Answer: False

The disjunction states that Remy is either humble or healthy (or possibly both). This provides two alternative ways to satisfy the condition needed for the health‑to‑agility rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction that Remy is either humble or healthy.</text>
<logic>humble(remy) v healthy(remy)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Sterling is talented is included in the background information but does not directly affect Remy's supportiveness. It is part of the overall knowledge base.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Sterling is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(sterling)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Remy is eloquent is crucial because it interacts with the humility rule. It gives us a concrete truth we can use to eliminate the humble possibility.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Remy is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(remy)</logic>
</step>

Since Remy is eloquent, the conditional that humble implies not eloquent cannot hold if he were humble. Therefore, by contraposition (modus tollens), we infer that Remy is not humble. This eliminates one side of the earlier disjunction.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Because Remy is eloquent and humility would make him not eloquent, we deduce that Remy is not humble.</text>
<logic>~humble(remy)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Remy is not humble, the disjunction "humble or healthy" can only be satisfied by the healthy alternative. Thus, by disjunctive syllogism we conclude that Remy is healthy.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given that Remy is not humble and the disjunction that he is either humble or healthy, we conclude that Remy must be healthy.</text>
<logic>healthy(remy)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Remy is healthy, we can apply the rule linking health to agility. Using modus ponens, we derive that Remy is agile.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Since Remy is healthy and health implies agility, we infer that Remy is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(remy)</logic>
</step>

Having shown Remy is agile, we can invoke the second rule which makes every agile individual supportive. Again by modus ponens, we obtain that Remy is supportive.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Because Remy is agile and agility implies being supportive, we derive that Remy is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(remy)</logic>
</step>

We derived Remy is supportive, so the conclusion Remy is not supportive is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Freya, Hollis, Tom, and Wade are all known to be knowledgeable. In this setting, if Freya is knowledgeable then she is brilliant. Whenever Freya is brilliant, she is also curious. Moreover, if Freya is confident then she is not curious. These statements capture all the facts and conditional relationships that will be used in the reasoning.
examples.question: Is it true that Freya is not confident?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Freya lacks confidence based on the information provided. The goal is to see if the premises force the negation of the predicate confident for Freya.

The first rule tells us that knowledge is sufficient for brilliance in Freya's case; this will allow us to move from a fact about her knowledge to a fact about her being brilliant. Recognizing this link is essential for the chain of inference.
<step n="1" type="premise">

<logic>brilliant(freya) :- knowledgeable(freya)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects brilliance to curiosity, so once we have established Freya's brilliance we can immediately obtain her curiosity. This intermediate step is needed to later clash with the confidence rule.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Freya is brilliant, then Freya is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(freya) :- brilliant(freya)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that confidence would lead to the opposite of curiosity. By having a statement about curiosity we will be able to test whether confidence can hold without contradiction.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Freya is confident, then Freya is not curious.</text>
<logic>~curious(freya) :- confident(freya)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Hollis is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(hollis)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">

<logic>knowledgeable(tom)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Wade is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(wade)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Freya is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(freya)</logic>
</step>

We apply the first rule to the known fact that Freya is knowledgeable, deriving that she must also be brilliant. This uses the direct implication that knowledge guarantees brilliance for her.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Freya being knowledgeable and the rule that knowledge implies brilliance, we infer that Freya is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(freya)</logic>
</step>

With Freya now known to be brilliant, the second rule lets us infer that she is curious. The reasoning follows straightforwardly: brilliance entails curiosity for Freya.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since Freya is brilliant and the rule that brilliance leads to curiosity, we conclude that Freya is curious.</text>

</step>

We now have Freya being curious, which directly contradicts the consequent of the confidence rule (that confidence would make her not curious). To avoid this contradiction, we must conclude that the antecedent—Freya being confident—is false, giving us ~confident(freya).
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Given that Freya is curious and the rule that confidence would make her not curious, we deduce that Freya cannot be confident.</text>

Therefore Freya is not confident, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Rain has the quality of being eloquent. Whenever rain is found to be articulate, it follows that rain is also creative. If rain happens to be creative, then rain becomes patient. Should rain be impartial, this guarantees that rain is not eloquent. Moreover, rain satisfies at least one of the two possibilities: it is either impartial or it is articulate.
examples.question: Is it the case that rain is not patient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the statement that rain is not patient holds. The goal is to see if we can derive patient(rain) from the supplied facts and rules, which would make the negation impossible. We'll work through the premises step by step, using the logical connections provided.

The first rule is important because it creates a bridge from the property articulate to the property creative. If we later manage to establish that rain is articulate, this rule will let us infer that rain is creative, moving us toward the patient property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule tells us that if rain is articulate, then rain is creative.</text>
<logic>creative(rain) :- articulate(rain)</logic>
</step>

The second rule continues the chain by stating that creativity leads to patience. Thus, once we have creative(rain), we can immediately conclude patient(rain). This rule is the final link we need to reach the query.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule tells us that if rain is creative, then rain is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(rain) :- creative(rain)</logic>
</step>

The third rule gives a way to deny impartiality: if rain were impartial, it would contradict the known fact that rain is eloquent. This will become useful when we combine it with the fact that rain is eloquent to eliminate the impartial alternative.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule tells us that if rain is impartial, then rain is not eloquent.</text>
<logic>~eloquent(rain) :- impartial(rain)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that rain must satisfy at least one of two conditions: being impartial or being articulate. By ruling out one of these options, we can force the other to be true, which is crucial for activating the earlier rules.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>We are given that rain is either impartial or articulate.</text>
<logic>impartial(rain) v articulate(rain)</logic>
</step>

The fact that rain is eloquent is the starting point for applying modus tollens with the third rule. It gives us a concrete truth that we can contrast against the conditional that would make rain not eloquent.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is stated that rain is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(rain)</logic>
</step>

Because we know rain is eloquent and the rule says impartial would make rain not eloquent, the only way for the rule to stay consistent is for rain not to be impartial. This is a classic modus tollens move: the consequent is false, so the antecedent must also be false. Hence we obtain ~impartial(rain).
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From rain being eloquent and the rule that impartial implies not eloquent, we infer that rain cannot be impartial.</text>
<logic>~impartial(rain)</logic>
</step>

Now we have ~impartial(rain) together with the disjunction that rain is either impartial or articulate. Since the impartial option is ruled out, the disjunctive syllogism forces the remaining alternative, articulate, to be true. Therefore we can assert articulate(rain).
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Since rain is not impartial and we know rain is either impartial or articulate, we conclude that rain must be articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(rain)</logic>
</step>

With articulate(rain) established, we can invoke the first rule (articulate → creative). By modus ponens, the truth of the antecedent guarantees the truth of the consequent, giving us creative(rain).
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Given that rain is articulate and the rule linking articulate to creative, we deduce that rain is creative.</text>
<logic>creative(rain)</logic>
</step>

Having creative(rain) allows us to apply the second rule (creative → patient). Again, modus ponens yields patient(rain). This completes the chain from the original facts to the property we are interested in.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because rain is creative and the rule linking creative to patient, we infer that rain is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(rain)</logic>
</step>

We derived Rain is patient, so the conclusion Rain is not patient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The sous chef is thoughtful. Whenever the sous chef is methodical, they are steady. Being hardworking guarantees that the sous chef is methodical. If the sous chef is well_spiced, then they are not thoughtful. Moreover, the sous chef is either well_spiced or hardworking (or possibly both).
examples.question: Is it the case that the sous chef is not steady?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to find out whether the sous chef fails to be steady. The goal is to see if the statement "the sous chef is not steady" can be supported by the information given.

This premise links methodical behavior to steadiness, so whenever we can show the sous chef is methodical, we will automatically have steadiness. It will be useful later when we derive methodicalness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the sous chef is methodical, then they are steady.</text>
<logic>steady(sous_chef) :- methodical(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that hard work leads to methodicalness. If we can establish that the sous chef is hardworking, we will be able to infer methodicalness via this rule.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that being hardworking makes the sous chef methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(sous_chef) :- hardworking(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

This conditional tells us that being well_spiced would contradict thoughtfulness. Since we already know the sous chef is thoughtful, this rule will help us eliminate the possibility of being well_spiced.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that if the sous chef is well_spiced, they cannot be thoughtful.</text>
<logic>~thoughtful(sous_chef) :- well_spiced(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities for the sous chef: either they are well_spiced or they are hardworking. Once we rule out one option, the other must hold.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>We are told that the sous chef is either well_spiced or hardworking.</text>
<logic>well_spiced(sous_chef) v hardworking(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the sous chef is thoughtful is a concrete piece of information we can use together with the rule about well_spiced to narrow down the options.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that the sous chef is thoughtful.</text>
<logic>thoughtful(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

Because the sous chef is thoughtful, and the rule says a well_spiced sous chef would not be thoughtful, the only way both statements can be true is if the sous chef is not well_spiced. This is a classic use of modus tollens: the antecedent of the rule must be false.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From the fact that the sous chef is thoughtful and the rule that a well_spiced sous chef would not be thoughtful, we infer that the sous chef is not well_spiced.</text>
<logic>~well_spiced(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the sous chef is not well_spiced, the disjunction "well_spiced or hardworking" forces the remaining alternative to be true. Hence, the sous chef must be hardworking. This follows the pattern of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Since the sous chef is not well_spiced and we know they are either well_spiced or hardworking, we conclude the sous chef is hardworking.</text>
<logic>hardworking(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the sous chef is hardworking, we can apply the rule that hardworking implies methodical. Therefore, the sous chef is methodical, using modus ponens on the previous step.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Given that the sous chef is hardworking and that hardworking implies methodical, we deduce the sous chef is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

Since we have shown the sous chef is methodical, the earlier rule that methodical leads to steadiness lets us conclude the sous chef is steady. Again, this is a straightforward modus ponens application.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Because the sous chef is methodical and methodical implies steady, we determine the sous chef is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

We derived Sous_chef is steady, so the conclusion Sous_chef is not steady is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Maria is determined, James feels motivated, and Kit is talented. Whenever someone is principled, they are also cooperative, so James would be cooperative if he were principled. If a person is talented, then that person is empathetic, which means Kit would be empathetic because she is talented. Being reflective guarantees that one is knowledgeable, so any reflective individual becomes knowledgeable; in particular, if Maria were reflective, she would be knowledgeable. If someone is motivated, then they are meticulous, which makes James meticulous because he is motivated. Whenever someone is anxious, they become open‑minded, so an anxious Kit would turn open‑minded. A determined person is reflective, therefore Maria’s determination makes her reflective. Additionally, either Kit is not empathetic or Kit is anxious, either Maria is not knowledgeable or Maria is perceptive, and either James is not meticulous or James is principled. No default rules are given.
examples.question: Is Maria perceptive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Maria possesses the quality of being perceptive. To answer this, we will trace how the given facts and logical rules allow us to derive perceptiveness for Maria.

This premise connects reflectiveness to knowledgeability. It will become useful once we have established that Maria is reflective, because then we can immediately infer that she is knowledgeable.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if someone is reflective then they are knowledgeable; thus, Maria being reflective would make her knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(maria) :- reflective(maria)</logic>
</step>

examples.context: Plato is fair. Any adept philosopher is also insightful. Being constructive guarantees credibility. If someone is steadfast, then they are not insightful. examples.question: Is it the case that Plato is not rational?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Plato lacks rationality. To answer, we must trace the chain of philosophical attributes given in the premises until we can either affirm or deny Plato's rational status.

The first premise tells us that any philosopher who is adept also possesses insight. This connection will later allow us to move from an adequacy claim to an insight claim, which is crucial for eliminating other possibilities.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that every adept thinker is also insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(plato) :- adept(plato)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links constructive activity to credibility. Knowing that credibility can be derived from constructiveness will let us later use a disjunction involving credibility to obtain adequacy.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if someone is constructive, then they are credible.</text>
<logic>credible(plato) :- constructive(plato)</logic>
</step>

The third premise states that steadfastness precludes insight. This negative relationship will become important once we have established insight, because it will force us to reject steadfastness for Plato.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that a steadfast individual cannot be insightful.</text>
<logic>~insightful(plato) :- steadfast(plato)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise offers a direct choice between steadfastness and rationality for Plato. This binary will be the final hinge point: if we can rule out steadfastness, rationality must follow.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Plato is either steadfast or rational.</text>
<logic>steadfast(plato) v rational(plato)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise provides another binary: either Plato is not fair, or he is constructive. Because we already know that Plato is fair, the only way to satisfy this disjunction is to accept that he is constructive.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Plato is not fair, or Plato is constructive.</text>
<logic>~fair(plato) v constructive(plato)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise presents a choice between lack of credibility and adequacy. Once we secure credibility, this disjunction will compel us to conclude that Plato is indeed adept.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Plato is not credible, or Plato is adept.</text>
<logic>~credible(plato) v adept(plato)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise simply affirms that Plato is fair. This fact will interact with the fifth premise to eliminate the ‘not fair’ alternative, pushing us toward constructiveness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Plato is fair.</text>
<logic>fair(plato)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that Plato is fair with the disjunction that either he is not fair or constructive, we see that the ‘not fair’ side cannot hold. Therefore, the only remaining option is that Plato is constructive. This step converts a disjunction into a concrete attribute.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>From Plato being fair and the disjunction that either he is not fair or constructive, we infer that Plato is constructive.</text>
<logic>constructive(plato)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Plato is constructive, we invoke the rule that constructive behavior guarantees credibility. Consequently, Plato inherits credibility. This deduction links the earlier constructive conclusion to a new property needed for the next disjunction.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since Plato is constructive, the rule about constructive implying credibility gives us that Plato is credible.</text>
<logic>credible(plato)</logic>
</step>

Now we possess Plato's credibility, and we face the disjunction that either he lacks credibility or is adept. Since credibility is true, the ‘not credible’ alternative is false, forcing the conclusion that Plato must be adept. This moves us from a credibility claim to adequacy.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>Given Plato’s credibility and the disjunction that either he is not credible or adept, we deduce that Plato is adept.</text>
<logic>adept(plato)</logic>
</step>

With Plato confirmed as adept, we apply the rule that every adept thinker is insightful. Hence Plato becomes insightful. This insight will later conflict with steadfastness, allowing us to eliminate that possibility.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Because Plato is adept, the rule linking adept to insightful lets us conclude that Plato is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(plato)</logic>
</step>

We have Plato being insightful, but the rule says that if someone is steadfast, they cannot be insightful. Since insight holds, steadfastness would lead to a contradiction, so we must conclude that Plato is not steadfast. This negation prepares us for the final binary.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Plato is insightful, and the rule that steadfastness leads to not‑insightful forces the negation of steadfastness for Plato.</text>
<logic>~steadfast(plato)</logic>
</step>

The final binary offers that Plato is either steadfast or rational. Because we have just shown that he is not steadfast, the only remaining option is that Plato is rational. This derives the very property we are asked to negate.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>With Plato not being steadfast and the earlier disjunction that he is either steadfast or rational, we determine that Plato must be rational.</text>
<logic>rational(plato)</logic>
</step>

We derived Plato is rational, so the conclusion Plato is not rational is False.
</think>
Answer: False

These statements capture all the facts, conditional rules, and the single disjunction given for the problem.
examples.question: Is Master George robust?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several properties of Master George and two other carpenters, together with conditional relationships among those properties. Our goal is to see whether we can conclude that Master George is robust, the carpentry equivalent of being strong. To answer this, we must trace the implications from the facts through the rules and check if any chain leads to robustness.

I need to determine whether Master_george is robust can be derived from the given premises.

The first rule tells us that carefulness for Master George guarantees nimbleness. This is relevant because it provides a way to move from a known fact (careful) toward another attribute that might be used later. Recognizing this link is the first step in building any possible derivation.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Master George is careful, then he is nimble.</text>
<logic>nimble(master_george) :- careful(master_george)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that if Master George is nimble, then he is ethical. Since the previous rule can give us nimbleness, this rule could allow us to reach the ethical property, extending the chain of inference. We keep this in mind as a potential route toward further conclusions.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Master George is nimble, then he is ethical.</text>
<logic>ethical(master_george) :- nimble(master_george)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects the learned attribute to logical thinking for Master George. Although we do not yet have evidence that he is learned, this rule is recorded because it could become useful if the disjunction or other information later supplies the learned condition.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Master George is learned, then he is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(master_george) :- learned(master_george)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Master George is careful. This fact activates the first rule, allowing us to infer that he is nimble. It is the concrete starting point for any forward reasoning about his other qualities.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Master George is careful.</text>
<logic>careful(master_george)</logic>
</step>

Apprentice Lyric being careful is noted, but it concerns a different individual, so it does not directly affect the properties of Master George. It is included to satisfy the requirement of stating all facts, yet it does not contribute to proving robustness for George.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Apprentice Lyric is careful.</text>
<logic>careful(apprentice_lyric)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, Journeyman Rex is careful; this fact pertains only to Rex and does not help us derive any new attribute for Master George. It remains part of the overall factual landscape without influencing the target query.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Journeyman Rex is careful.</text>
<logic>careful(journeyman_rex)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Master_george is robust: no rule or disjunction has "robust" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(robust(master_george))</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Master_george is not robust.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~robust(master_george))</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived">
<text>Neither Master_george is robust nor Master_george is not robust can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(robust(master_george))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: Ruth is known to be clever, and she is also judicious. Elliot has the motivation trait, and Ruth shares that same motivation. Whenever something is clever, it is also creative, so any clever individual automatically becomes creative. Any creative thing guarantees that the individual is cooperative, establishing a link from creativity to cooperation. If an entity is open‑minded, then it follows that the entity is logical. Being judicious ensures that one is open‑minded, thereby creating a chain from judiciousness to logical reasoning. Motivation leads to rationality, so any motivated person is rational. Conversely, if something is determined, it cannot be rational; determined individuals are not rational. Regarding elliot, either elliot is determined or elliot is clever, and either elliot is not cooperative or elliot is judicious. No other rules or defaults apply.

examples.context: Pilot Kent is watchful. Aircraft Sky is airworthy. Whenever something is watchful, it is versatile, so Kent being watchful makes him versatile. Whenever something is alert, it is agile, and any alert entity is agile; likewise, being alert follows from being trained, and being trained follows from being experienced. If something is experienced, then it is thorough, because experience guarantees thoroughness. If something is airworthy, then it is responsive, so Sky being airworthy makes Sky responsive. If something is neutral, then it is dependable, meaning that any neutral entity is dependable. Also, being thorough leads to experience, so a thorough pilot becomes experienced. Either Kent is not versatile, or Kent is thorough. Either the aircraft Sky is not responsive, or Sky is neutral.
