examples.context: At the downtown library four staff members—cameron, kendall, olga, and scout—are noted for their professional habits. Cameron, olga, and scout are all witty. Cameron, kendall, olga, and scout are each proficient. The library’s guidelines state that any proficient librarian is efficient. Whenever a librarian is witty, that librarian is punctual. Whenever a librarian is punctual, that librarian is consistent. If a librarian is cooperative, then that librarian is not efficient. For kendall specifically, either kendall is cooperative or kendall is witty. No other default rules apply.
examples.question: Is it true that kendall is not consistent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement that kendall is not consistent holds. The goal is to see if the premises force kendall to be consistent, which would make the negation false.

This premise links punctuality to consistency, so any later proof that kendall is punctual will immediately give us consistency. It sets up the final step of the argument.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any punctual librarian is also consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(kendall) :- punctual(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Here we are told that proficiency guarantees efficiency. Since we already have a fact that kendall is proficient, this rule will let us derive that kendall is efficient, a crucial intermediate fact.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any proficient librarian is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(kendall) :- proficient(kendall)</logic>
</step>

This rule connects wit to punctuality. Later we will aim to establish that kendall is witty, and then we can use this premise to get punctuality, moving toward consistency.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any witty librarian is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(kendall) :- witty(kendall)</logic>
</step>

The rule states that if someone is cooperative they cannot be efficient. This will allow us to argue that kendall is not cooperative once we have shown kendall is efficient.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any cooperative librarian is not efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(kendall) :- cooperative(kendall)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities for kendall: cooperative or witty. By eliminating one alternative we can force the other, which will be essential for reaching punctuality.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>For kendall, either kendall is cooperative or kendall is witty.</text>
<logic>cooperative(kendall) v witty(kendall)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>cameron is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(cameron)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>olga is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(olga)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>scout is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(scout)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>cameron is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(cameron)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>olga is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(olga)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>scout is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(scout)</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="premise">
<text>kendall is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that kendall is proficient together with the rule that proficiency implies efficiency, we conclude kendall is efficient. This establishes the premise needed for the next contradiction step.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Because kendall is proficient and proficiency guarantees efficiency, we infer that kendall is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know kendall is efficient, the rule that cooperation leads to not‑efficient creates a conflict if kendall were cooperative. To avoid inconsistency, we infer that kendall cannot be cooperative, i.e., ~cooperative(kendall).
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>Since kendall is efficient and cooperation would make a librarian not efficient, we conclude that kendall cannot be cooperative.</text>
<logic>~cooperative(kendall)</logic>
</step>

We have the disjunction that kendall is either cooperative or witty, and we have just shown kendall is not cooperative. The only way the disjunction can remain true is if kendall is witty, so we derive witty(kendall).
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Given that kendall is not cooperative and the disjunction that kendall is either cooperative or witty, we deduce that kendall must be witty.</text>
<logic>witty(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Having established that kendall is witty, we apply the rule that wit guarantees punctuality. This yields punctual(kendall), moving us one step closer to consistency.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,3">
<text>Because kendall is witty and wit leads to punctuality, we infer that kendall is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Finally, with punctual(kendall) in hand, the first rule tells us that any punctual librarian is consistent. Therefore we infer consistent(kendall).
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,1">
<text>With kendall punctual and the rule that punctuality leads to consistency, we derive that kendall is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(kendall)</logic>
</step>

We derived Kendall is consistent, so the conclusion Kendall is not consistent is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Cameron is honest, and Emerson is also honest. River possesses the quality of being rational, while Dara is systematic. Whenever something is rational, it is also patient, so River is patient because River is rational. If something is agile, then it is perceptive for River, meaning River being agile would make River perceptive. Any systematic thing is reliable, therefore Dara is reliable because Dara is systematic. Optimism leads to resilience, so if Dara were optimistic, Dara would be resilient. Rested follows from being experienced: any experienced individual is rested, and sharp follows from honesty: any honest individual is sharp. Additionally, River faces a choice: either River is not patient or River is agile. Dara also faces a choice: either Dara is not reliable or Dara is optimistic. Finally, Cameron faces a choice: either Cameron is not sharp or Cameron is experienced.
examples.question: Based on the information provided, is Cameron rested?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Cameron enjoys the state of being rested. The context supplies several conditional relationships and some concrete facts about the characters. Our goal is to link the facts about Cameron to the rule that makes someone rested, stepping through the intermediate properties as needed.

The first premise gives us a direct conditional: any individual who is experienced is also rested. This link is crucial because if we can show Cameron is experienced, the rested status will follow immediately.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if someone is experienced, then they are rested.</text>
<logic>rested(cameron) :- experienced(cameron)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that honesty guarantees sharpness. Since we already know Cameron is honest from the facts, this rule will allow us to derive sharpness for Cameron, which will be useful for later reasoning.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if someone is honest, then they are sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(cameron) :- honest(cameron)</logic>
</step>

The third premise presents a logical alternative: either Cameron lacks sharpness or Cameron possesses experience. This disjunction will let us eliminate one possibility once we have information about sharpness, forcing the other alternative to hold.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Cameron is not sharp, or Cameron is experienced.</text>
<logic>~sharp(cameron) v experienced(cameron)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Emerson is honest is recorded here. While this fact does not directly involve Cameron, it demonstrates that the honesty‑to‑sharpness rule is applicable in the world, reinforcing the reliability of that rule for other individuals, such as Cameron.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Emerson is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(emerson)</logic>
</step>

River being rational is a straightforward fact. It activates the rule that rationality implies patient, but this chain is not needed for the final query; it simply shows the consistency of the rule set.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>River is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(river)</logic>
</step>

Dara's systematic nature is stated as a fact. Through the rule that systematic objects are reliable, we can infer Dara is reliable, and via the related disjunction we could later infer optimism, though these are peripheral to Cameron's rested status.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Dara is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(dara)</logic>
</step>

Cameron is explicitly described as honest. This fact is the starting point for applying the honesty‑to‑sharpness rule, which will enable us to generate the sharpness predicate needed for the disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Cameron is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(cameron)</logic>
</step>

Combining Cameron's honesty with the rule that honesty leads to sharpness, we infer that Cameron is sharp. This step is a direct application of the rule from PREMISE_2, using the fact from PREMISE_7, and gives us the positive sharpness fact needed to engage the disjunction in PREMISE_3.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Cameron being honest and the rule that honesty implies sharpness, we infer that Cameron is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(cameron)</logic>
</step>

Now we have sharp(cameron). The disjunction in PREMISE_3 says "not sharp(cameron) or experienced(cameron)". Since sharp(cameron) is true, the first alternative (not sharp) is false, forcing the second alternative to be true; therefore Cameron must be experienced. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism using the derived sharpness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Using the disjunction that Cameron is either not sharp or experienced, together with the newly derived fact that Cameron is sharp, we conclude that Cameron must be experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(cameron)</logic>
</step>

With experienced(cameron) established, we can invoke PREMISE_1, which tells us that any experienced individual is rested. Applying modus ponens yields rested(cameron). This completes the chain from the original facts to the desired conclusion.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Since Cameron is experienced and the rule says experienced individuals are rested, we deduce that Cameron is rested.</text>
<logic>rested(cameron)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Cameron is rested, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Is it true that Jamie is not persistent?

examples.context: Leo, a lion, is known to be persistent. In this setting, any lion that is not dutiful is considered wise. Conversely, if a lion is dutiful, then he cannot be persistent.
examples.question: Is Leo wise?
examples.completion:

</step>

The second rule states that if a lion is dutiful, he cannot be persistent. This relationship will be useful for applying a contrapositive argument based on Leo’s known persistence.
<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>~dutiful(leo)</logic>

Answer: True

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either Senator Rowan is not uneasy, or he is magnanimous. It sets up a logical choice that we will resolve using the known fact about his uneasiness.

examples.context: In the renewable‑energy park, the wind turbine is operational. Whenever a wind turbine is operational, it is also grid‑connected. Either the wind turbine is not grid‑connected, or it has high output. Either the wind turbine does not have high output, or it is efficient.
examples.question: Is the wind turbine not efficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the wind turbine is not efficient. To answer this, we will follow the chain of given information, starting from the fact that the turbine is operational and moving through the conditional and disjunctive statements.

The conditional rule connects the turbine's operational status to being grid‑connected, which will allow us to turn the known fact into a new property. Recognizing this link is essential because it creates the first bridge in our reasoning chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if the wind turbine is operational then it is grid‑connected.</text>
<logic>grid_connected(wind_turbine) :- operational(wind_turbine)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction offers two possibilities: either the turbine lacks grid connectivity or it enjoys high output. This statement will become useful once we have established the turbine's grid‑connected status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>One of the given alternatives is that the wind turbine is either not grid‑connected or it produces high output.</text>
<logic>~grid_connected(wind_turbine) v high_output(wind_turbine)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction similarly gives two alternatives concerning high output and efficiency. Once we know whether the turbine has high output, this clause will let us infer something about its efficiency.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Another alternative states that the wind turbine either lacks high output or it is efficient.</text>
<logic>~high_output(wind_turbine) v efficient(wind_turbine)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that the turbine is operational serves as the starting point for applying the conditional rule. Without this fact, we could not trigger the implication that leads to grid connectivity.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is stated that the wind turbine is operational.</text>
<logic>operational(wind_turbine)</logic>
</step>

Because the turbine is operational and the rule tells us that any operational turbine is grid‑connected, we can directly conclude that the turbine is grid‑connected. This follows the standard pattern of modus ponens, turning a premise and its implication into a new fact.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the turbine being operational and the rule, we infer that it is grid‑connected.</text>
<logic>grid_connected(wind_turbine)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the turbine is grid‑connected, we look back at the first disjunction. Since the turbine is indeed grid‑connected, the alternative "not grid‑connected" is false, leaving high output as the only viable option. Thus we infer that the turbine has high output via disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Using the grid‑connected status and the first disjunction, we deduce that the turbine has high output.</text>
<logic>high_output(wind_turbine)</logic>
</step>

With the high‑output property in hand, we turn to the second disjunction. Because the turbine does have high output, the negation "not high output" cannot hold, so the remaining option is that the turbine is efficient. This again uses disjunctive syllogism to extract the efficient property.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Applying the high‑output fact to the second disjunction yields that the turbine is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(wind_turbine)</logic>
</step>

We derived Wind_turbine is efficient, so the conclusion Wind_turbine is not efficient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The Sunrise Tower is code compliant. Any building that is code compliant is also sustainable. If a building is sustainable, then it is structurally sound. If a building is historically significant, then it is not structurally sound.
examples.question: Is it true that the Sunrise Tower is not historically significant?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several architectural properties of the Sunrise Tower and a set of logical relationships among them. Our goal is to determine whether we can conclude that the Sunrise Tower is not historically significant.

The rule linking code compliance to sustainability tells us that any building meeting code standards automatically satisfies the sustainability criterion. This connection will let us move from the known fact of code compliance toward establishing sustainability for the tower.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any building that is code compliant is also sustainable.</text>
<logic>sustainable(sunrise_tower) :- code_compliant(sunrise_tower)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that sustainability guarantees structural soundness. Once we have shown the tower is sustainable, this implication will let us infer that it is structurally sound, which is essential for the next step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a building is sustainable, then it is structurally sound.</text>
<logic>structurally_sound(sunrise_tower) :- sustainable(sunrise_tower)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a contrapositive relationship: a historically significant building cannot be structurally sound. This will be used to argue that if the tower is structurally sound, it cannot be historically significant.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a building is historically significant, then it is not structurally sound.</text>
<logic>~structurally_sound(sunrise_tower) :- historically_significant(sunrise_tower)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the Sunrise Tower is code compliant is our starting point. It directly activates the first rule and begins the chain of reasoning toward the desired conclusion.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The Sunrise Tower is code compliant.</text>
<logic>code_compliant(sunrise_tower)</logic>
</step>

Applying the first rule to the fact that the Sunrise Tower is code compliant, we see that the tower must be sustainable. This step converts the known property into a new one that we can later use to derive structural soundness.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the Sunrise Tower being code compliant and the rule that code compliance implies sustainability, we infer that the Sunrise Tower is sustainable.</text>
<logic>sustainable(sunrise_tower)</logic>
</step>

With the tower now known to be sustainable, the second rule allows us to infer that it is structurally sound. This bridges the gap between sustainability and the property needed to challenge historical significance.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since the Sunrise Tower is sustainable and sustainability leads to structural soundness, we deduce that the Sunrise Tower is structurally sound.</text>
<logic>structurally_sound(sunrise_tower)</logic>
</step>

Since the tower is structurally sound, the third rule tells us that a building which is historically significant would have to lack structural soundness. Therefore, the tower cannot be historically significant, giving us the negated property we sought.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Because the Sunrise Tower is structurally sound, and a historically significant building would be not structurally sound, we conclude that the Sunrise Tower cannot be historically significant.</text>
<logic>~historically_significant(sunrise_tower)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sunrise_tower is not historically significant, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the kitchen, the chef notes that Onyx, Sasha, and Wren are all insightful cooks. Whenever a cook is insightful, that cook is also principled. If a cook is principled, then the cook is logical. Logical cooks are always compassionate. Compassionate cooks inevitably become sharp. On the other hand, if a cook is methodical, that cook cannot be sharp. Finally, the chef observes that Wren is either methodical or adaptable, but not both at the same time. These observations capture all the facts, rules, and the single disjunction that will be used to reason about Wren's adaptability.
examples.question: Based on the given information, is Wren adaptable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Wren possesses the adaptable quality, using the supplied facts and logical relationships. The goal is to chain the given rules together until we can directly answer the query about Wren's adaptability.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Wren is logical, then Wren is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(wren) :- logical(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Wren is principled, then Wren is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(wren) :- principled(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Wren is insightful, then Wren is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(wren) :- insightful(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Wren is compassionate, then Wren is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(wren) :- compassionate(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Wren is methodical, then Wren is not sharp.</text>
<logic>~sharp(wren) :- methodical(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Wren is either methodical or adaptable.</text>
<logic>methodical(wren) v adaptable(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Onyx is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(onyx)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Sasha is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(sasha)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Wren is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(wren)</logic>
</step>

Wren is explicitly stated to be insightful. The rule that every insightful individual is principled applies universally, so we can instantiate it for Wren. By applying this rule, we safely infer that Wren is principled, establishing the first link in the chain.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From Wren being insightful and the rule that every insightful cook is principled, we infer that Wren is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(wren)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Wren is principled, we turn to the rule connecting principled status to logical status. The rule says any principled cook is logical, so with Wren's principled property we can directly derive that Wren is logical. This moves us one step closer to the compassionate condition we need later.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because Wren is now known to be principled and the rule says a principled cook is logical, we conclude that Wren is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(wren)</logic>
</step>

Having established Wren's logical nature, we use the rule that logical cooks are compassionate. The implication is straightforward: because Wren is logical, the compassionate property follows automatically. This gives us the compassionate fact required for the next deduction.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>Given that Wren is logical and that logical cooks are compassionate, we deduce that Wren is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(wren)</logic>
</step>

With Wren now known to be compassionate, we apply the rule that compassionate cooks become sharp. The rule is a direct implication, so from Wren's compassion we deduce that Wren is sharp. This sharpness will be crucial for disproving the methodical alternative.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Since Wren is compassionate and compassionate cooks become sharp, we obtain that Wren is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(wren)</logic>
</step>

The rule about methodical cooks states that if someone is methodical, they cannot be sharp. Since we have proven Wren is sharp, the antecedent of that rule must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Therefore, we conclude that Wren is not methodical, using modus tollens.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,5">
<text>We have that if Wren were methodical, he would not be sharp; because Wren is sharp, we can infer that Wren is not methodical.</text>
<logic>~methodical(wren)</logic>
</step>

We are left with the original disjunction that Wren is either methodical or adaptable. Because we have just shown Wren is not methodical, the only remaining possibility in the disjunction is that Wren is adaptable. This application of disjunctive syllogism yields the desired adaptable property.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,6">
<text>With the knowledge that Wren is not methodical and the earlier statement that Wren is either methodical or adaptable, we conclude that Wren must be adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(wren)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Wren is adaptable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The fact that August is responsible adds to the background but does not affect Kai directly, so it does not help us infer anything about Kai's eloquence.

examples.context: In the kitchen, the saffron is slow-cooked, the julienne is slow-cooked, the lardon is slow-cooked, and the risotto is slow-cooked. Whenever a dish is slow-cooked, it becomes well-seasoned. Conversely, if a dish is ready to serve, it cannot be well-seasoned. These statements capture all the facts and conditional relationships needed for the reasoning task.
examples.question: Is the risotto ready to serve?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the risotto can be considered ready to serve. To answer this, we must examine the given cooking facts and the conditional rules that relate slow cooking, seasoning, and serving readiness. By tracing the logical consequences of these statements, we can determine the truth value of the query.

The first rule tells us that any dish that has been slow-cooked acquires the property of being well-seasoned. This connection will let us infer a positive property about the risotto once we know it is slow-cooked.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the risotto is slow-cooked, then it is well-seasoned.</text>
<logic>well_seasoned(risotto) :- slow_cooked(risotto)</logic>
</step>

The second rule provides a negative link: if a dish is ready to serve, it cannot be well-seasoned. This will be crucial for deriving a contradiction later on, allowing us to reject the possibility that the risotto is ready to serve.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the risotto is ready to serve, then it is not well-seasoned.</text>
<logic>~well_seasoned(risotto) :- ready_to_serve(risotto)</logic>
</step>

Knowing that saffron is slow-cooked establishes one of the baseline facts, showing that the kitchen has at least one slow-cooked ingredient, though it does not directly affect the risotto.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Saffron is slow-cooked.</text>
<logic>slow_cooked(saffron)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the fact that julienne is slow-cooked adds another example of a slow-cooked item, reinforcing the overall context of slow cooking in the kitchen.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Julienne is slow-cooked.</text>
<logic>slow_cooked(julienne)</logic>
</step>

The lardon being slow-cooked further confirms that slow cooking is a common preparation method among the dishes present.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Lardon is slow-cooked.</text>
<logic>slow_cooked(lardon)</logic>
</step>

Most importantly, the statement that the risotto is slow-cooked gives us the antecedent needed to apply the first rule and deduce that the risotto is well-seasoned.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Risotto is slow-cooked.</text>
<logic>slow_cooked(risotto)</logic>
</step>

By applying modus ponens to the fact that the risotto is slow-cooked and the rule linking slow cooking to being well-seasoned, we can directly conclude that the risotto is well-seasoned. This establishes a positive property about the risotto that will later clash with the serving-ready condition.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Therefore, the risotto is well-seasoned.</text>
<logic>well_seasoned(risotto)</logic>
</step>

Now we use modus tollens: we have derived that the risotto is well-seasoned, but the second rule says a ready-to-serve risotto would not be well-seasoned. Since the risotto is indeed well-seasoned, the antecedent of that rule (being ready to serve) must be false. Hence, the risotto is not ready to serve.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Thus, the risotto is not ready to serve.</text>
<logic>~ready_to_serve(risotto)</logic>
</step>

We derived Risotto is not ready to serve, so the conclusion Risotto is ready to serve is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Derek is known to be evidence-driven. Whenever Derek is patient, he is also meticulous. If Derek happens to be energetic, then he is not meticulous. In addition, either Derek is not evidence-driven or Derek is patient. No other default assumptions are made.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Derek is energetic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a mixture of facts, conditional rules, and a disjunction about Derek. Our goal is to see whether the statement "Derek is energetic" can be established from this information. To do that we will follow the chain of implications step by step.

This premise links patientness to meticulousness, which will be useful later when we manage to prove that Derek is patient. It tells us that patient status guarantees meticulous status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Derek is patient then Derek is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(derek) :- patient(derek)</logic>
</step>

This premise connects energetic behavior to the lack of meticulousness. It will allow us to reason about energeticness by examining whether Derek is meticulous, using a contrapositive argument.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Derek is energetic then Derek is not meticulous.</text>
<logic>~meticulous(derek) :- energetic(derek)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities: either Derek is not evidence-driven or he is patient. Combined with the fact that Derek is evidence-driven, it forces the second alternative to hold, giving us a route to patientness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The statement provides a disjunction: either Derek is not evidence-driven or Derek is patient.</text>
<logic>~evidence_driven(derek) v patient(derek)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Derek is evidence-driven is essential because it interacts with the disjunction to eliminate the "not evidence-driven" option, paving the way for the derivation of patientness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Derek is evidence-driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(derek)</logic>
</step>

Since we know Derek is evidence-driven, the disjunct "not evidence-driven" cannot be true. In a disjunction, if one option is false, the other must be true, so we conclude Derek is patient. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because Derek is evidence-driven and the disjunction says either not evidence-driven or patient, we can eliminate the first option and infer that Derek must be patient.</text>
<logic>patient(derek)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Derek is patient, we can now apply the conditional from PREMISE_1. The rule says patient implies meticulous, so by modus ponens we infer that Derek is meticulous. This gives us a concrete property to work with in the next step.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Derek being patient and the rule that patient implies meticulous, we infer that Derek is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(derek)</logic>
</step>

We now have Derek meticulous, but PREMISE_2 tells us that energetic would lead to not meticulous. Taking the contrapositive (modus tollens), if Derek were energetic, he would not be meticulous, yet we have the opposite. Therefore Derek cannot be energetic, and we derive the negation of the query.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since we now have Derek meticulous and the rule that energetic implies not meticulous, the contrapositive tells us Derek cannot be energetic, so we derive that Derek is not energetic.</text>
<logic>~energetic(derek)</logic>
</step>

We derived Derek is not energetic, so the conclusion Derek is energetic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The basalt_layer, the karst_cave, and the rocky_outcrop are all pressure_sensitive. Whenever something is pressure_sensitive, it may satisfy certain conditional properties. In particular, any pressure_sensitive karst_cave triggers a seismic_alert. Any fracture_resistant karst_cave is also mineral_rich. Whenever a karst_cave has a seismic_alert, it becomes gas_emitting. Being gas_emitting guarantees that the karst_cave is fracture_resistant. If the karst_cave is fissure_connected, then it is not mineral_rich. Moreover, the karst_cave is either fissure_connected or erosion_control. No other default rules are assumed.
examples.question: Is the karst_cave proactive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the karst_cave possesses the proactive property. Since proactive does not appear in any of the stated facts or rules, we must examine how the available information might indirectly inform this query.

I need to determine whether Karst_cave is proactive can be derived from the given premises.

The rule linking pressure_sensitive to seismic_alert is relevant because we already know the karst_cave is pressure_sensitive. Recognizing this connection lets us potentially derive further properties of the karst_cave.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any pressure_sensitive karst_cave triggers a seismic_alert.</text>
<logic>seismic_alert(karst_cave) :- pressure_sensitive(karst_cave)</logic>
</step>

The implication from fracture_resistant to mineral_rich matters because later rules cycle between fracture_resistant and gas_emitting, which could eventually affect mineral_rich status. We keep this in mind as we trace the chain of consequences.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a karst_cave is fracture_resistant, then it is mineral_rich.</text>
<logic>mineral_rich(karst_cave) :- fracture_resistant(karst_cave)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that a seismic_alert leads to gas_emitting is crucial; if we can establish a seismic_alert for the karst_cave, we will obtain gas_emitting and then, by another rule, fracture_resistant. This builds a loop of derivations.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Whenever a karst_cave has a seismic_alert, it becomes gas_emitting.</text>
<logic>gas_emitting(karst_cave) :- seismic_alert(karst_cave)</logic>
</step>

The reverse implication—gas_emitting guarantees fracture_resistant—closes the loop, allowing us to move from an alert to a resistant state and back again. This reciprocity shows how the properties reinforce each other.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Being gas_emitting implies the karst_cave is fracture_resistant.</text>
<logic>fracture_resistant(karst_cave) :- gas_emitting(karst_cave)</logic>
</step>

The rule producing a negated mineral_rich when the karst_cave is fissure_connected introduces a potential conflict with the earlier rule that fracture_resistant leads to mineral_rich. Knowing this helps us see that the disjunction about fissure_connected versus erosion_control could affect the overall picture.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the karst_cave is fissure_connected, then it is not mineral_rich.</text>
<logic>~mineral_rich(karst_cave) :- fissure_connected(karst_cave)</logic>
</step>

Stating that the basalt_layer is pressure_sensitive adds a factual anchor but does not directly influence the karst_cave's proactive status. It simply fulfills the requirement that all entities mentioned have the pressure_sensitive attribute.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The basalt_layer is pressure_sensitive.</text>
<logic>pressure_sensitive(basalt_layer)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that the karst_cave is pressure_sensitive activates PREMISE_1, allowing us to infer a seismic_alert for the karst_cave. This is the first concrete derivation we can make from the given data.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The karst_cave is pressure_sensitive.</text>
<logic>pressure_sensitive(karst_cave)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, noting that the rocky_outcrop is pressure_sensitive satisfies the universal description of the domain but does not further our goal concerning the karst_cave. It ensures completeness of the factual landscape.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The rocky_outcrop is pressure_sensitive.</text>
<logic>pressure_sensitive(rocky_outcrop)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Karst_cave is proactive: no rule or disjunction has "proactive" as a consequent.</text>

</step>

<step n="10" type="derived">

<logic>unreachable(~proactive(karst_cave))</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="derived">
<text>Neither Karst_cave is proactive nor Karst_cave is not proactive can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(proactive(karst_cave))</logic>
</step>

</think>

examples.context: In this game design scenario, Anna, Everest, and Onyx are three characters. Everest is courageous. Onyx is fit, and Anna is punctual. The design specifies several conditional relationships: if Anna is supportive then Anna is calm; if Onyx is determined then Onyx is logical; if Everest is courageous then Everest is respectful; if Everest is meticulous then Everest is strategic; and if Anna is punctual then Anna is vigorous. Additionally, the following alternatives are noted: either Onyx is not fit or Onyx is determined; either Everest is not respectful or Everest is meticulous; either Anna is not vigorous or Anna is supportive.
examples.question: Is it true that Onyx is not logical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to evaluate whether Onyx lacks the property of being logical. To answer this, we must use the given facts, conditional rules, and the disjunctive statements that relate fit and determination for Onyx, and then see if logical follows.

The rule linking determination to logical is crucial because it tells us exactly how logical can be derived: any time Onyx is determined, we can immediately conclude that Onyx is logical. Keeping this in mind will allow us to turn a determination fact into a logical one later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Onyx is determined then Onyx is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(onyx) :- determined(onyx)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities concerning Onyx: either Onyx is not fit, or Onyx is determined. This statement will become useful once we consider the concrete fact about Onyx's fitness, enabling us to eliminate one of the alternatives.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Onyx is not fit or Onyx is determined.</text>
<logic>~fit(onyx) v determined(onyx)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Everest is courageous is included in the context, although it does not play a direct role in the chain leading to Onyx's logical status. It simply satisfies one of the background facts provided.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Everest is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(everest)</logic>
</step>

Anna being punctual is another background fact; it activates the rule that punctual Anna is vigorous, but that rule is not needed for the current query about Onyx. It is recorded here to fulfill the requirement of stating all premises.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Anna is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(anna)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that Onyx is fit directly contradicts the first part of the disjunction "~fit(onyx)". By having this fact, we can use disjunctive syllogism to resolve the disjunction and obtain a stronger statement about Onyx.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Onyx is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(onyx)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Onyx is fit, the alternative "~fit(onyx)" in the disjunction is false. Disjunctive syllogism then forces the remaining option to be true, giving us that Onyx is determined. This step converts the vague choice in the disjunction into a concrete property.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From the fact that Onyx is fit and the disjunction that either not fit or determined, we can infer that Onyx must be determined.</text>
<logic>determined(onyx)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Onyx is determined, we can apply the earlier rule (Premise 1) which says that determination guarantees logicality. By modus ponens, the determined status directly yields that Onyx is logical.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Since Onyx is determined, the rule linking determined to logical lets us conclude that Onyx is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(onyx)</logic>
</step>

We derived Onyx is logical, so the conclusion Onyx is not logical is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the workshop, the apprentice's blade is sharp. Whenever an apprentice's blade is sharp, the apprentice is considered knowledgeable. Moreover, any apprentice who is knowledgeable is also concentrated in their work. Additionally, it is known that either the apprentice is not concentrated, or the apprentice is steady‑handed.
examples.question: Is it true that the apprentice is not steady‑handed?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the apprentice lacks the quality of being steady‑handed. To answer, we will trace the information given about the apprentice's blade, knowledge, and focus, and see how they interact with the available disjunction.

The first rule tells us that a sharp blade guarantees the apprentice is knowledgeable. This connection is crucial because it lets us move from a concrete fact about the blade to an abstract trait of the apprentice, which will be used later in the chain of reasoning.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the apprentice's blade is sharp, then the apprentice is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(apprentice) :- sharp_blade(apprentice)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links knowledge to concentration, stating that any knowledgeable apprentice is also concentrated. By establishing this, the proof can later combine the derived knowledge with this rule to obtain a statement about focus.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the apprentice is knowledgeable, then the apprentice is concentrated.</text>
<logic>concentrated(apprentice) :- knowledgeable(apprentice)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either the apprentice is not concentrated, or the apprentice is steady‑handed. This will become important once we have a definite claim about the apprentice's concentration, allowing us to eliminate the first alternative.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the apprentice is not concentrated, or the apprentice is steady‑handed.</text>
<logic>~concentrated(apprentice) v steady_handed(apprentice)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the apprentice's blade is sharp gives us a starting point for the deduction. With this concrete observation, we can activate the rule that turns sharpness into knowledge, beginning the logical cascade.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The apprentice's blade is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp_blade(apprentice)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, we combine the fact that the blade is sharp with the rule that sharpness implies knowledge, concluding that the apprentice is knowledgeable. This step translates a physical attribute into a mental attribute, setting up the next inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the blade being sharp and the rule linking sharpness to knowledge, we infer that the apprentice is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(apprentice)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the apprentice is knowledgeable, we again use modus ponens with the second rule, which states that knowledge leads to concentration. Consequently, we can assert that the apprentice is concentrated, giving us a definite truth about focus.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since the apprentice is knowledgeable and knowledgeable leads to concentration, we conclude the apprentice is concentrated.</text>
<logic>concentrated(apprentice)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the apprentice is concentrated, we turn to the disjunction. Since the first option ('not concentrated') is contradicted by our derived fact, the only remaining possibility is that the apprentice is steady‑handed. This use of disjunctive syllogism lets us infer steadiness.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given the apprentice is concentrated and the disjunction that either not concentrated or steady‑handed, we deduce the apprentice must be steady‑handed.</text>

We derived Apprentice is steady handed, so the conclusion Apprentice is not steady handed is False.

Answer: False

examples.context: In the dojo, Sensei Chen is known to be strategic. Within this training hall, any practitioner who is strategic is also disciplined. Moreover, every disciplined practitioner is compassionate. Conversely, any practitioner who is keen is not compassionate. These statements together capture all the facts and conditional relationships we will use.
examples.question: Is Sensei Chen not keen?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Sensei Chen lacks the quality of keenness. To answer this, we must follow the chain of given properties and conditional rules that connect strategic, disciplined, compassionate, and keen attributes.

The first conditional tells us that strategic individuals are also disciplined. This link is essential because it allows us to move from a known fact about strategic status to a new fact about discipline.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any strategic person is also disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(sensei_chen) :- strategic(sensei_chen)</logic>
</step>

The second conditional states that disciplined individuals are compassionate. Once we have established discipline, this rule will let us derive compassion for Sensei Chen.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any disciplined person is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(sensei_chen) :- disciplined(sensei_chen)</logic>
</step>

The third conditional gives a negative relationship: if someone is keen, then they are not compassionate. This will be used later to show that being compassionate rules out keenness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any keen person is not compassionate.</text>
<logic>~compassionate(sensei_chen) :- keen(sensei_chen)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that Sensei Chen is strategic. This fact is the starting point for applying the first rule and beginning our inference chain.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Fact that Sensei Chen is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(sensei_chen)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Sensei Chen is strategic and the rule that strategic implies disciplined, we can confidently assert that he is disciplined. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens: the antecedent holds, so the consequent follows.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From Sensei Chen being strategic and the rule that strategic implies disciplined, we infer that Sensei Chen is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(sensei_chen)</logic>
</step>

With the newly obtained disciplined status and the rule that disciplined leads to compassion, we deduce that Sensei Chen is compassionate. Again, modus ponens lets us move from the premise to the conclusion.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From Sensei Chen being disciplined and the rule that disciplined implies compassionate, we infer that Sensei Chen is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(sensei_chen)</logic>
</step>

We now have that Sensei Chen is compassionate. The rule about keenness says that a keen individual would be not compassionate. Since compassion holds, the contrary (keenness) would make the rule false, so by modus tollens we infer that Sensei Chen cannot be keen; thus he is not keen.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since Sensei Chen is compassionate, but a keen person would be not compassionate, we conclude that Sensei Chen cannot be keen, i.e., he is not keen.</text>
<logic>~keen(sensei_chen)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sensei_chen is not keen, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

<think>

I need to determine whether Herb_patch is proactive can be derived from the given premises.

The first rule tells us that being well_watered would make the herb patch blooming. This is relevant because if we could show the herb patch is well_watered, we would gain a new property, though it still would not directly give proactivity.

examples.context: The angel is humble, and the lake is polite. Whenever something is humble, it is also brilliant, so the angel is brilliant because it is humble. examples.question: Is the lake precise?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the lake has the property of being precise. The goal is to see if the available facts and rules allow us to assert precise(lake) or its negation.

The rule linking decisiveness to knowledge will later let us turn a decision about the lake into a statement about its knowledgeability. This connection is essential for the chain of inference we will build.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the lake is decisive, then the lake is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(lake) :- decisive(lake)</logic>
</step>

The rule that politeness implies responsibility lets us move from the known fact that the lake is polite to a new fact about responsibility. This step opens the way to use the disjunction involving responsibility.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the lake is polite, then the lake is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(lake) :- polite(lake)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that precise leads to not knowledgeable provides the crucial contrapositive we will need. If we later establish the lake is knowledgeable, this rule will let us conclude the lake cannot be precise.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the lake is precise, then the lake is not knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>~knowledgeable(lake) :- precise(lake)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of "not responsible" or "decisive" holds for the lake. Once we know the lake is responsible, the first option is ruled out, forcing the second.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the lake is not responsible or the lake is decisive.</text>
<logic>~responsible(lake) v decisive(lake)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the angel is humble is part of the background knowledge, though it does not directly affect the lake's precision. It is included to satisfy the requirement that all given facts appear in the context.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The angel is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(angel)</logic>
</step>

The lake being polite is a concrete fact we can immediately use with PREMISE_2 to derive responsibility. This factual anchor is the starting point for the forward reasoning chain.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The lake is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(lake)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that the lake is polite and the rule that politeness guarantees responsibility, we infer the lake is responsible. This new fact will allow us to apply the disjunction about responsibility.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since the lake is polite, we can infer that the lake is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(lake)</logic>
</step>

We now have responsible(lake). The disjunction says either not responsible or decisive. Since responsible is true, the "not responsible" alternative is impossible, so we must accept that the lake is decisive.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given that the lake is responsible, the disjunction forces the lake to be decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(lake)</logic>
</step>

Having established decisive(lake), we can invoke the rule from PREMISE_1 that decisiveness implies knowledgeability. Thus the lake is knowledgeable, giving us the positive knowledge fact we need for the next step.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Because the lake is decisive, we conclude that the lake is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(lake)</logic>
</step>

We possess knowledgeable(lake) and the conditional from PREMISE_3 that precise would make the lake not knowledgeable. By modus tollens, because the lake is indeed knowledgeable, it cannot be precise; therefore ~precise(lake) holds.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Knowing the lake is knowledgeable and that being precise would make it not knowledgeable, we deduce the lake is not precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(lake)</logic>
</step>

We derived Lake is not precise, so the conclusion Lake is precise is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Arden is calm. Whenever someone is judicious, that person is also diligent. If a person is calm, then they are experienced. Being studious guarantees that the person is reliable. If a person is healthy, then they are not experienced. Regarding Arden, either they are healthy or they are judicious. Also, it is the case that either Arden is not diligent or Arden is studious.
examples.question: Is Arden reliable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Arden can be said to be reliable. The goal is to build a chain of deductions from the given facts and rules that leads to the reliability of Arden.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any individual who is judicious is also diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(arden) :- judicious(arden)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If someone is calm, then they are experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(arden) :- calm(arden)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If someone is studious, then they are reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(arden) :- studious(arden)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If someone is healthy, then they are not experienced.</text>
<logic>~experienced(arden) :- healthy(arden)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Arden is either healthy or judicious.</text>
<logic>healthy(arden) v judicious(arden)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Arden is not diligent or Arden is studious.</text>
<logic>~diligent(arden) v studious(arden)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Arden is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(arden)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that Arden is calm. The rule "If someone is calm, then they are experienced" tells us that calmness guarantees experience. By applying this rule to Arden's calmness, we can safely assert that Arden is experienced.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Arden being calm and the rule that calm implies experienced, we infer that Arden is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(arden)</logic>
</step>

Next we consider the rule "If someone is healthy, then they are not experienced". We already have that Arden is experienced, which contradicts the conclusion that would follow from Arden being healthy. Using modus tollens, the only way to keep consistency is to conclude that Arden cannot be healthy; thus we have ~healthy(Arden).
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since Arden is experienced and healthy would make someone not experienced, we conclude that Arden cannot be healthy.</text>
<logic>~healthy(arden)</logic>
</step>

We also know from the disjunction that Arden is either healthy or judicious. Since we have just derived that Arden is not healthy, the healthy option is eliminated. By disjunctive syllogism, the remaining alternative must hold, so Arden is judicious.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Given that Arden is not healthy and that Arden is either healthy or judicious, we deduce that Arden must be judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(arden)</logic>
</step>

Having established Arden's judiciousness, we can invoke the rule that any judicious person is diligent. This direct implication allows us to state that Arden is diligent.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Because Arden is judicious and judicious implies diligent, we obtain that Arden is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(arden)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "Either Arden is not diligent or Arden is studious" now comes into play. Because we have just shown that Arden is diligent, the first disjunct (~diligent) is false. Therefore, the second disjunct must be true, giving us that Arden is studious.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>With Arden being diligent and the disjunction that either Arden is not diligent or Arden is studious, we infer that Arden is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(arden)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule "If someone is studious, then they are reliable" converts Arden's studiousness into reliability. Applying this rule yields the desired conclusion that Arden is reliable.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Since Arden is studious and studious leads to being reliable, we conclude that Arden is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(arden)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Arden is reliable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Unit_beta is calibrated. Unit_alpha is mission_focused. Whenever a unit has sensor_acuity, it is power_on. Any unit that is adaptive is low_profile. If a unit is low_profile, then it is logical. Whenever a unit is truthful, it is not precise. If a unit is functional, then it is not auxiliary. Functional(unit_alpha) or adaptive(unit_alpha) holds. Not mission_focused(unit_alpha) or auxiliary(unit_alpha) holds. Not unbiased(unit_beta) or sensor_acuity(unit_beta) holds. Not logical(unit_alpha) or precise(unit_alpha) holds. Also, because unit_beta is calibrated, it is unbiased. And because unit_alpha is mission_focused, the above facts and rules are all in effect.
examples.question: Is it true that unit_alpha is not truthful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We start with the information that unit_alpha is mission_focused and unit_beta is calibrated, along with a collection of conditional rules and exclusive alternatives. Our goal is to determine whether unit_alpha can be said to be not truthful.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any unit that is adaptive is low_profile.</text>
<logic>low_profile(unit_alpha) :- adaptive(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a unit is low_profile, then it is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(unit_alpha) :- low_profile(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Whenever a unit is truthful, it is not precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(unit_alpha) :- truthful(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a unit is functional, then it is not auxiliary.</text>
<logic>~auxiliary(unit_alpha) :- functional(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Functional(unit_alpha) or adaptive(unit_alpha) is true.</text>
<logic>functional(unit_alpha) v adaptive(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Not mission_focused(unit_alpha) or auxiliary(unit_alpha) is true.</text>
<logic>~mission_focused(unit_alpha) v auxiliary(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Not logical(unit_alpha) or precise(unit_alpha) is true.</text>
<logic>~logical(unit_alpha) v precise(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Unit_beta is calibrated.</text>
<logic>calibrated(unit_beta)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Unit_alpha is mission_focused.</text>
<logic>mission_focused(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

Since the disjunction states that either unit_alpha is not mission_focused or it is auxiliary, and we already know unit_alpha is mission_focused, the first option is impossible. Thus the only way for the disjunction to hold is for unit_alpha to be auxiliary. This uses the principle of eliminating the false disjunct.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>From the fact that unit_alpha is mission_focused and the disjunction not mission_focused(unit_alpha) or auxiliary(unit_alpha), we infer auxiliary(unit_alpha).</text>
<logic>auxiliary(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The rule tells us that if a unit is functional then it cannot be auxiliary. We have just derived that unit_alpha is auxiliary, so the antecedent of the rule (functional) must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Consequently we conclude that unit_alpha is not functional.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Using the rule that functional implies not auxiliary and the derived auxiliary(unit_alpha), we conclude that unit_alpha is not functional.</text>
<logic>~functional(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction functional(unit_alpha) or adaptive(unit_alpha) must be true. Because we have established that unit_alpha is not functional, the only remaining way for the disjunction to be satisfied is for unit_alpha to be adaptive. This deduction follows from disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Given that unit_alpha is not functional and the disjunction functional(unit_alpha) or adaptive(unit_alpha), we deduce adaptive(unit_alpha).</text>
<logic>adaptive(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

One of the given rules says that any adaptive unit is low_profile. Having obtained adaptive(unit_alpha) in the previous step, we can immediately infer low_profile(unit_alpha) by applying the conditional directly.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>From adaptive(unit_alpha) and the rule that adaptive implies low_profile, we obtain low_profile(unit_alpha).</text>
<logic>low_profile(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

Another rule links low_profile to logical: any low_profile unit is logical. Since we now know unit_alpha is low_profile, we can assert logical(unit_alpha) using modus ponens on that rule.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>Low_profile(unit_alpha) together with the rule low_profile implies logical yields logical(unit_alpha).</text>
<logic>logical(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction not logical(unit_alpha) or precise(unit_alpha) must hold. Because we have just derived logical(unit_alpha), the first part of the disjunction is false, forcing the second part to be true. Therefore precise(unit_alpha) follows.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,7">
<text>Logical(unit_alpha) combined with the disjunction not logical(unit_alpha) or precise(unit_alpha) gives precise(unit_alpha).</text>
<logic>precise(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

We have a rule that says if a unit is truthful then it cannot be precise. We have just shown that unit_alpha is precise, which contradicts the consequent of the rule if unit_alpha were truthful. To avoid contradiction, we must conclude that unit_alpha is not truthful, applying modus tollens.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,3">
<text>Precise(unit_alpha) and the rule that truthful leads to not precise allow us to infer that unit_alpha is not truthful.</text>
<logic>~truthful(unit_alpha)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Unit_alpha is not truthful, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In this philosophical vignette, Cora is described as principled. Whenever Cora is eloquent, she is also content. On the other hand, if Cora is clever then she is not content. Furthermore, it is asserted that either Cora is not principled or Cora is eloquent. These statements together constitute all the information we have about Cora.
examples.question: Is Cora clever?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Cora can be considered clever based on the supplied philosophical premises. The goal is to see if the assumption of cleverness can be sustained without violating any of the given facts or rules.

The first rule connects eloquence to content, telling us that eloquence guarantees content. This connection will become useful once we establish Cora's eloquence, allowing us to infer her contentment later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Cora is eloquent, then she is content.</text>
<logic>content(cora) :- eloquent(cora)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links cleverness to the negation of content, stating that cleverness would force Cora not to be content. This rule is crucial because it creates a tension with any derivation that makes Cora content, which we will later obtain.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Cora is clever, then she is not content.</text>
<logic>~content(cora) :- clever(cora)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either Cora lacks the principled quality or she is eloquent. Since we already know she is principled, this disjunction will help us eliminate the first alternative and pin down the second.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Cora is not principled or Cora is eloquent.</text>
<logic>~principled(cora) v eloquent(cora)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Cora is principled directly contradicts the first part of the disjunction. Knowing she is principled allows us to discard the possibility that she is not principled, thereby forcing the other option—Cora must be eloquent.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact tells us that Cora is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(cora)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that Cora is principled with the disjunction, we see that the ‘not principled’ option is impossible, so the only remaining way for the disjunction to hold is for Cora to be eloquent. Hence we derive that Cora is eloquent.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>From Cora being principled and the disjunction, we infer that Cora must be eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(cora)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Cora's eloquence, we can apply the first rule: eloquence implies content. By substituting Cora into this rule, we infer that Cora is indeed content.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Given that Cora is eloquent and the rule linking eloquence to content, we conclude that Cora is content.</text>
<logic>content(cora)</logic>
</step>

With Cora confirmed as content, we turn to the second rule, which says cleverness would make her not content. Since she is already content, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to reject the antecedent of that rule, concluding that Cora cannot be clever; thus, she is not clever.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since Cora is content, and cleverness would make her not content, we deduce that Cora cannot be clever, i.e., she is not clever.</text>
<logic>~clever(cora)</logic>
</step>

We derived Cora is not clever, so the conclusion Cora is clever is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the pottery studio, Arden, Fern, and Sage are all known for being calm. Moreover, any analytical Arden is automatically scholarly. Finally, there is a certainty about Arden: either Arden is not scholarly or Arden is intuitive. No other defaults apply.
examples.question: Is it true that Arden is not clear-headed?

examples.context: Consider a clay vase that adheres to the standards. If a piece adheres to the standards, then it follows the tradition. Any piece that follows the tradition is also durable. The vase is either not durable or it is seasoned.
examples.question: Is the clay vase seasoned?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given information about a particular clay vase and its qualities. Our aim is to determine whether the vase can be considered seasoned.

The first rule tells us that any vase that follows tradition also possesses durability. This link will let us move from traditional status to a durable assessment later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any traditional piece is also durable.</text>
<logic>durable(clay_vase) :- traditional(clay_vase)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that adherence to standards guarantees that a vase follows tradition. This provides a bridge from being standardized to being traditional.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a piece adheres to the standards, it follows the tradition.</text>
<logic>traditional(clay_vase) :- standardized(clay_vase)</logic>
</step>

We also know that the vase is either not durable or it is seasoned. This disjunction will be crucial for applying disjunctive reasoning once we establish durability.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the clay vase lacks durability, or it is seasoned.</text>
<logic>~durable(clay_vase) v seasoned(clay_vase)</logic>
</step>

It is explicitly mentioned that the vase adheres to the standards. This fact activates the second rule, allowing us to infer tradition.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The clay vase adheres to the standards.</text>
<logic>standardized(clay_vase)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens to the fact that the vase is standardized and the rule that standards imply tradition, we conclude the vase is traditional. This step converts a given property into another usable property.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Thus, the clay vase is traditional.</text>
<logic>traditional(clay_vase)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have the vase marked as traditional, we can use the first rule, which says tradition leads to durability. By modus ponens again, we deduce the vase is durable.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Consequently, the vase is durable.</text>
<logic>durable(clay_vase)</logic>
</step>

We have established that the vase is durable, which contradicts the left side of the earlier disjunction that claimed the vase might be not durable. Since that alternative is false, the only remaining possibility is that the vase is seasoned.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since the vase is durable, the alternative that it lacks durability is false, so it must be seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(clay_vase)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Clay_vase is seasoned, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Lance is attentive, and Craig is vigorous. Whenever Craig is eloquent, Craig is friendly. If Lance is attentive, then Lance is methodical. examples.question: Is it the case that Craig is not friendly?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement that Craig is not friendly holds. The goal is to see if we can prove Craig is friendly, which would make the negation false.

The first rule tells us that friendliness follows directly from eloquence for Craig. This will be useful later if we can establish that Craig is eloquent.

<logic>friendly(craig) :- eloquent(craig)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links decisiveness to a lack of vigor. Knowing Craig's vigor status will allow us to reason about his decisiveness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Craig is decisive, then Craig is not vigorous.</text>
<logic>~vigorous(craig) :- decisive(craig)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a choice: Craig is either decisive or eloquent (or possibly both). This will let us eliminate one option and force the other.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Craig is decisive or Craig is eloquent (or both).</text>
<logic>decisive(craig) v eloquent(craig)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Lance is attentive is recorded, though it does not directly affect Craig; it satisfies the premises of other rules about Lance.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Lance is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(lance)</logic>
</step>

Craig being vigorous is a concrete fact. Combined with the rule about decisiveness leading to non‑vigour, it sets up a contradiction if Craig were decisive.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Craig is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(craig)</logic>
</step>

Since Craig is vigorous, the condition that would make him not vigorous (being decisive) cannot be true. By contraposing the rule, we conclude Craig is not decisive. This eliminates one side of the earlier disjunction.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From Craig being vigorous and the rule that decisiveness would make him not vigorous, we infer that Craig is not decisive.</text>
<logic>~decisive(craig)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Craig is not decisive, the disjunction "decisive or eloquent" can only be satisfied by the remaining alternative, namely that Craig is eloquent. Thus we infer eloquence for Craig.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since Craig is not decisive and we know that either he is decisive or eloquent, we can conclude that Craig is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(craig)</logic>

Having established Craig's eloquence, we can invoke the first rule: eloquence guarantees friendliness. Therefore Craig must be friendly.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Because Craig is eloquent and eloquence guarantees friendliness, Craig is friendly.</text>
<logic>friendly(craig)</logic>
</step>

We derived Craig is friendly, so the conclusion Craig is not friendly is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Engineer Micah works in the automotive lab. He is test_based, meaning his decisions rely on concrete data. In this setting, any test_based engineer is also systematic, so being test_based guarantees systematic reasoning. Moreover, any systematic engineer is precise, so systematic reasoning leads to precision. Additionally, it is known that either Engineer Micah is not precise or he is diagnostic; at least one of these alternatives must hold.
examples.question: Is Engineer Micah diagnostic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Engineer Micah possesses the diagnostic quality. The goal is to show that the property diagnostic holds for Micah based on the given facts and logical relationships.

The second premise connects systematic reasoning to precision. This link will allow us to later combine Micah’s systematic status with the disjunction that involves precision.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any systematic engineer is precise: if someone is systematic then they are precise.</text>
<logic>precise(engineer_micah) :- systematic(engineer_micah)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possible ways to satisfy the statement: either Micah is not precise or he is diagnostic. Keeping both alternatives in mind is essential for applying disjunctive reasoning later.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating that Engineer Micah is either not precise or diagnostic.</text>
<logic>~precise(engineer_micah) v diagnostic(engineer_micah)</logic>

We are explicitly told that Engineer Micah is test_based. This fact serves as the starting point for the chain of inferences that follow.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Engineer Micah is test_based.</text>
<logic>test_based(engineer_micah)</logic>
</step>

Because Micah is test_based and the rule tells us that test_based guarantees systematic, we can directly infer that Micah is systematic. This step bridges the factual premise to the next rule in the chain.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From Micah being test_based and the rule that test_based implies systematic, we infer that Micah is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(engineer_micah)</logic>

Now that we have established Micah’s systematic nature, the second rule lets us conclude that he is precise. This deduction is necessary to engage the earlier disjunction that mentions precision.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Using Micah’s systematic status and the rule that systematic implies precise, we conclude Micah is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(engineer_micah)</logic>
</step>

With Micah confirmed as precise, the disjunction "not precise or diagnostic" can no longer be satisfied by the first option, so the only remaining way for the statement to hold is that Micah is diagnostic. This use of disjunctive syllogism yields the desired property.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given Micah is precise and the disjunction that Micah is not precise or diagnostic, we eliminate the first alternative and deduce Micah is diagnostic.</text>

Therefore Engineer_micah is diagnostic, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: In the renowned Vineyard Royal, the wine is known to be refined. Whenever the vineyard is balanced, it is not refined. Moreover, the vineyard is either balanced or aged. These statements capture all the information we have about the vineyard.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude that Vineyard Royal is not aged?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the information about Vineyard Royal allows us to assert that it is not aged. The goal is to see if the statement "the vineyard is not aged" can be logically derived from the given facts and rules.

The conditional rule connects the property of being balanced to the absence of refinement. Recognizing this link is crucial because it lets us use the known refinement of the vineyard to reason about its balance.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the vineyard is balanced, then it cannot be refined.</text>
<logic>~refined(vineyard_royal) :- balanced(vineyard_royal)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us the vineyard must satisfy at least one of two conditions: being balanced or being aged. This alternative will become important once we have information about one of the options.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>We are told that the vineyard is either balanced or aged (or possibly both).</text>
<logic>balanced(vineyard_royal) v aged(vineyard_royal)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the vineyard is refined provides a concrete starting point for our deduction. Knowing the vineyard possesses this property allows us to apply the conditional rule from Premise 1.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that the vineyard is refined.</text>
<logic>refined(vineyard_royal)</logic>
</step>

Because the rule says a balanced vineyard cannot be refined, and we already know the vineyard is refined, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that the vineyard is not balanced. This is a classic use of modus tollens: the consequent is false, so the antecedent must also be false.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From the fact that the vineyard is refined and the rule linking balance to not being refined, we infer that the vineyard is not balanced.</text>
<logic>~balanced(vineyard_royal)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the vineyard is not balanced, we return to the disjunction that it is either balanced or aged. Since the balanced option is ruled out, the aged alternative must hold. This is an application of disjunctive syllogism: eliminating one disjunct leaves the other as true.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Given that the vineyard is not balanced and that it must be either balanced or aged, we deduce that the vineyard is aged.</text>
<logic>aged(vineyard_royal)</logic>
</step>
