examples.context: The project manager is an expert. Whenever the project manager is innovative, they are forward‑looking. If the project manager has a flexible design approach, then they are not an expert. The project manager either has a flexible design approach or is innovative (or possibly both).
examples.question: Can we conclude that the project manager is not forward‑looking?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several pieces of information about the project manager's abilities and the logical relationships among them. Our goal is to determine whether the manager can be shown to lack the forward‑looking quality.

The rule linking innovation to forward‑looking behavior is important because it lets us turn a statement about the manager being innovative into a statement about them being forward‑looking later in the proof. We keep this conditional in mind for future derivations.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any innovative project manager is also forward‑looking.</text>
<logic>forward_looking(project_manager) :- innovative(project_manager)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that a flexible design implies not being an expert will be used together with the known fact that the manager is an expert. This contrast will allow us to eliminate the flexible‑design possibility.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a project manager has a flexible design approach, then they are not an expert.</text>
<logic>~expert(project_manager) :- flexible_design(project_manager)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The project manager either has a flexible design approach or is innovative.</text>
<logic>flexible_design(project_manager) v innovative(project_manager)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The project manager is an expert.</text>
<logic>expert(project_manager)</logic>
</step>

We know the manager is an expert, and the rule says that if they had a flexible design they would not be an expert. Since the conclusion of that rule contradicts the known fact, the only way to preserve consistency is to conclude the antecedent is false; thus the manager does not have a flexible design. This is a classic application of modus tollens.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since the project manager is an expert and a flexible design would make them not an expert, we infer that they cannot have a flexible design.</text>
<logic>~flexible_design(project_manager)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the manager is not flexible‑design, we return to the disjunction that the manager is either flexible‑design or innovative. Because the flexible‑design option is ruled out, the remaining alternative must hold, so the manager is innovative. This follows the pattern of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Given that the project manager does not have a flexible design, and they must either be flexible or innovative, we conclude they are innovative.</text>
<logic>innovative(project_manager)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have shown the manager is innovative, we can invoke the earlier rule that any innovative manager is forward‑looking. By applying modus ponens, the manager inherits the forward‑looking property from their innovation.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Because the project manager is innovative and any innovative manager is forward‑looking, we deduce the manager is forward‑looking.</text>
<logic>forward_looking(project_manager)</logic>
</step>

We derived Project_manager is forward looking, so the conclusion Project_manager is not forward looking is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Craig is eloquent. Whenever Craig is eloquent, he is cooperative. If Craig is generous, then Craig is perceptive. If Craig is perceptive, then Craig is principled. If Craig is principled, then Craig is responsible. If Craig is responsible, then Craig is skillful. Either Craig is not cooperative or Craig is generous.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Craig is not skillful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Craig can be shown to be not skillful. The goal is to see if the premises force Craig to be skillful, which would make the negation false. To do that we will follow the chain of implications provided and also use the disjunction about cooperativeness and generosity. Each step will build on the previous ones toward the final conclusion.

The first rule links eloquence to cooperativeness for Craig. Knowing this relationship is essential because we already have Craig's eloquence as a fact, which will let us start the forward reasoning chain. This rule will later allow us to derive Craig's cooperativeness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Craig is eloquent, then Craig is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(craig) :- eloquent(craig)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects generosity with perceptiveness. It will become useful after we establish that Craig is generous, enabling us to move further along the chain toward skillfulness. This conditional sets up the next inference step.
<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>perceptive(craig) :- generous(craig)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that perceptiveness implies principledness. Once we have perceptiveness, this rule will let us upgrade Craig's status to principled, continuing the chain toward responsibility and skillfulness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Craig is perceptive, then Craig is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(craig) :- perceptive(craig)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule states that being principled leads to being responsible. This will be invoked after we have shown Craig is principled, pushing the derivation one step closer to the target property of skillfulness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Craig is principled, then Craig is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(craig) :- principled(craig)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule asserts that responsibility guarantees skillfulness. This is the final link in the chain; once we have responsibility, we can directly conclude skillfulness, which will contradict the query.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Craig is responsible, then Craig is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(craig) :- responsible(craig)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either Craig is not cooperative or Craig is generous. This will be used together with the cooperative fact to eliminate the first alternative and force the second, giving us generosity.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Craig is not cooperative or Craig is generous.</text>
<logic>~cooperative(craig) v generous(craig)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Craig is eloquent gives us a solid starting point. Because eloquence is the antecedent of the first rule, this fact will let us derive cooperativeness right away.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that Craig is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(craig)</logic>

Applying modus ponens to the eloquent fact and the first rule yields that Craig is cooperative. This step is straightforward: the rule says eloquence guarantees cooperativeness, and we have eloquence, so cooperativeness follows. This new fact will be crucial for using the disjunction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Craig being eloquent and the first rule, we infer that Craig is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(craig)</logic>
</step>

With Craig now known to be cooperative, the disjunction "not cooperative or generous" can no longer hold via the first option, so the only way for it to be true is that Craig is generous. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism: one disjunct is false, therefore the other must be true. Thus we obtain generosity.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>Using the cooperative fact together with the disjunction, we deduce that Craig must be generous.</text>
<logic>generous(craig)</logic>
</step>

Now that Craig is generous, the second rule allows us to infer perceptiveness by modus ponens. The rule says generosity leads to perceptiveness, and we have generosity, so perceptiveness is derived. This moves us further along the chain toward skillfulness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Given Craig is generous and the second rule, we conclude Craig is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(craig)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Craig is perceptive, we can use the third rule to deduce that Craig is principled. Again, modus ponens applies: perceptiveness guarantees principledness, so we add principled to Craig's profile.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>From Craig being perceptive and the third rule, we obtain that Craig is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(craig)</logic>
</step>

From Craig's principled status, the fourth rule gives us responsibility. The rule directly links principledness to responsibility, and since we have principledness, responsibility follows by modus ponens.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>With Craig principled and the fourth rule, we infer Craig is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(craig)</logic>
</step>

Finally, with responsibility in hand, the fifth rule lets us conclude that Craig is skillful. This is the last application of modus ponens: responsibility ensures skillfulness, so we derive skillfulness for Craig.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,5">
<text>Finally, from Craig being responsible and the fifth rule, we derive that Craig is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(craig)</logic>
</step>

We derived Craig is skillful, so the conclusion Craig is not skillful is False.
</think>

examples.context: Wade is fit. Whenever someone is supportive, they are also curious. If Wade is not active, then Wade is supportive. Moreover, if Wade were active, he would not be fit. These statements capture all of the given facts and conditional relationships.
examples.question: Is Wade curious?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Wade can be classified as curious based on the information provided. The goal is to trace the logical connections from the known facts to the desired conclusion.

The first rule tells us that supportiveness leads directly to curiosity. Recognizing this link is essential because if we can establish Wade's supportiveness later, this rule will immediately give us his curiosity.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any supportive person is also curious, so if Wade is supportive then Wade is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(wade) :- supportive(wade)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that a lack of activity guarantees supportiveness. This conditional will be useful once we figure out Wade's activity status, allowing us to move toward establishing his supportiveness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Wade is not active, then he becomes supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(wade) :- ~active(wade)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a contrapositive relationship: if Wade were active, he could not be fit. This will help us evaluate Wade's activity by comparing it with the known fact about his fitness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Wade were active, that would make him not fit.</text>
<logic>~fit(wade) :- active(wade)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Wade is fit. This factual piece will be the anchor for applying the third rule in reverse, enabling us to infer something about his activity.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Wade is known to be fit.</text>
<logic>fit(wade)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Wade is fit together with the rule that activity would make him not fit, we see that the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Wade is not active. This is an application of modus tollens: if the consequence (not fit) is false, the antecedent (active) must also be false.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Since Wade is fit and being active would contradict that by making him not fit, we can infer that Wade is not active.</text>
<logic>~active(wade)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Wade is not active, we invoke the second rule which says that non‑activity implies supportiveness. By modus ponens, the truth of the antecedent (~active) directly yields the truth of the consequent (supportive).
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Given that Wade is not active and that lack of activity guarantees supportiveness, Wade must be supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(wade)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Wade is supportive, we can apply the first rule. Since supportive individuals are always curious, modus ponens gives us that Wade is curious.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Because Wade is supportive and every supportive person is curious, Wade is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(wade)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Wade is curious, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

Because Master Sterling’s enduring spirit is true, the rule that a creative technique would make the spirit false cannot have its antecedent satisfied. In other words, if having a creative technique would negate the spirit, and the spirit remains, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that the creative technique is absent. Thus we derive that he does not possess a creative technique.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>From Master Sterling’s enduring spirit and the rule that a creative technique would remove it, we infer that he does not possess a creative technique.</text>
<logic>~creative_technique(master_sterling)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Master Sterling does not possess a creative technique, we can now invoke the first rule, which says that the lack of a creative technique guarantees a forceful strike. By applying this conditional directly, we conclude that Master Sterling delivers a forceful strike.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since Master Sterling does not possess a creative technique, the earlier rule gives that he delivers a forceful strike.</text>
<logic>forceful_strike(master_sterling)</logic>
</step>

We derived Master_sterling is forceful strike, so the conclusion Master_sterling is not forceful strike is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the cellar, the cabernet is aged. The merlot is dry. Whenever a wine is aged, it becomes fragrant. Whenever a wine is fragrant, it becomes fermented. If a wine is balanced, then it is nutritious, and if a wine is dry, then it is refined. Either the cabernet is not fermented or it is tannic, and either the merlot is not refined or it is balanced.
examples.question: Is it true that the cabernet is not tannic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the cabernet fails to be tannic. The query translates to checking the truth of the statement that the cabernet is not tannic.

The first rule tells us that fragrance guarantees fermentation. This will let us move from a fragrant property to a fermented one later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a wine is fragrant, then it is fermented.</text>
<logic>fermented(cabernet) :- fragrant(cabernet)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links aging to fragrance, so any aged wine automatically becomes fragrant. This connection is crucial for establishing fermentation for the cabernet.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a wine is aged, then it is fragrant.</text>
<logic>fragrant(cabernet) :- aged(cabernet)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a logical alternative: either the cabernet is not fermented or it possesses tannic character. This will be used to derive tannicness once we know the cabernet is fermented.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the cabernet is not fermented, or it is tannic.</text>
<logic>~fermented(cabernet) v tannic(cabernet)</logic>
</step>

We are told that the merlot is dry, which will later allow us to infer that it is refined. Although this fact is not directly used for the cabernet, it mirrors the structure of the knowledge base.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The merlot is dry.</text>
<logic>dry(merlot)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that the cabernet is aged provides the starting point for applying the aging‑to‑fragrance rule. It anchors the chain of reasoning for the cabernet's properties.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The cabernet is aged.</text>
<logic>aged(cabernet)</logic>
</step>

Because the cabernet is aged and the rule states that aged wines become fragrant, we can safely conclude the cabernet is fragrant. This step bridges the factual aging information to the fragrant condition needed for the next inference.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From the fact that the cabernet is aged and the rule that aged wines are fragrant, we infer that the cabernet is fragrant.</text>
<logic>fragrant(cabernet)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the cabernet is fragrant, we now apply the rule that fragrance leads to fermentation. Consequently, the cabernet is fermented, satisfying the left side of the earlier disjunction.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Since the cabernet is fragrant and fragrant wines are fermented, we conclude that the cabernet is fermented.</text>
<logic>fermented(cabernet)</logic>
</step>

With the cabernet confirmed as fermented, the disjunction "not fermented or tannic" forces the second alternative to hold, giving us that the cabernet is tannic. This logical move is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Given that the cabernet is fermented and that either it is not fermented or it is tannic, we deduce that the cabernet is tannic.</text>
<logic>tannic(cabernet)</logic>
</step>

We derived Cabernet is tannic, so the conclusion Cabernet is not tannic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the theater troupe, cypress is known to be evidence driven. Whenever a member is evidence driven, that member is also fit. Moreover, cypress is either motivated or insightful, and it is also the case that either cypress is not inventive or cypress is persistent. No other facts are given.
examples.question: Is cypress strong?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether cypress possesses the quality of being strong. The goal will be reached by chaining the given facts, rules, and disjunctions together in a logical sequence.

The rule linking evidence driven to fit is relevant because we already have a fact that cypress is evidence driven. This connection will let us produce a positive fit statement later on.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any evidence‑driven individual is also fit, so it applies to cypress.</text>
<logic>fit(cypress) :- evidence_driven(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The insight‑to‑inventive rule matters for later steps, as establishing that cypress is insightful will allow us to infer inventiveness. Inventiveness will then interact with another disjunction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If someone is insightful, the rule says they must be inventive, and this concerns cypress.</text>
<logic>inventive(cypress) :- insightful(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The persistent‑to‑strong rule is the final bridge we need to reach the target property. If we can show cypress is persistent, we can immediately claim cypress is strong.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>There is a rule that any persistent person is strong, which will be used for cypress.</text>
<logic>strong(cypress) :- persistent(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The rule that motivated implies not fit will be used to eliminate the possibility that cypress is motivated, once we have a positive fit fact. This elimination is crucial for applying the disjunction about motivation versus insight.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that a motivated person cannot be fit, linking motivation to a negative fit for cypress.</text>
<logic>~fit(cypress) :- motivated(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "motivated or insightful" gives us two alternatives for cypress. After we rule out motivation, the only way the statement can hold is that cypress must be insightful.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>We are told that cypress is either motivated or insightful, giving a disjunctive choice.</text>
<logic>motivated(cypress) v insightful(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction "not inventive or persistent" will become useful after we know whether cypress is inventive. If cypress turns out to be inventive, the disjunction forces persistence.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Another disjunction says that either cypress is not inventive or cypress is persistent.</text>
<logic>~inventive(cypress) v persistent(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The fact that cypress is evidence driven is the starting point of the proof. It directly activates the rule from PREMISE_1, allowing us to derive a fit status.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that cypress is evidence driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(cypress)</logic>
</step>

Combining the evidence‑driven fact with the rule that evidence driven leads to fit, we conclude that cypress is fit. This derived fit will later help us contradict the possibility of motivation.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From the fact that cypress is evidence driven and the rule linking evidence driven to fit, we infer that cypress is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(cypress)</logic>
</step>

We have established that cypress is fit. The rule stating that a motivated person would be not fit therefore cannot be satisfied, so cypress must not be motivated. This negative conclusion is essential for resolving the motivational disjunction.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since cypress is fit and a motivated person would be not fit, we conclude that cypress cannot be motivated.</text>
<logic>~motivated(cypress)</logic>
</step>

Since cypress is not motivated, the disjunction "motivated or insightful" can only be true if cypress is insightful. Hence, we infer that cypress indeed possesses insight, which will be needed for the inventive rule.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Given that cypress is not motivated and the disjunction that cypress is motivated or insightful, the only remaining possibility is that cypress is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(cypress)</logic>
</step>

With cypress now known to be insightful, the rule that insight implies inventiveness lets us infer that cypress is inventive. This new fact sets up the second disjunction for the next step.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because cypress is insightful and the rule that insightful implies inventive, we deduce that cypress is inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "not inventive or persistent" cannot be satisfied by the first option because we have just shown cypress is inventive. Therefore, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that cypress is persistent.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>With cypress being inventive, the disjunction stating that either not inventive or persistent forces cypress to be persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(cypress)</logic>
</step>

Having proven that cypress is persistent, we can finally apply the rule that persistence guarantees strength. Consequently, cypress is strong, achieving the proof's objective.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Finally, knowing that cypress is persistent and using the rule that persistent leads to strong, we derive that cypress is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(cypress)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Cypress is strong, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The fact that the economic forecast is investor_confident does not directly affect activity, but it establishes the broader context of the forecast's characteristics. Including it satisfies the requirement to mention all given facts.

The fact that the economic forecast is budget_prepared is crucial because it activates the implication from Premise 1. Without this fact we could not derive policy_confirmed, which is needed for the default rule.

<logic>budget_prepared(economic_forecast)</logic>
</step>

Applying the implication from Premise 1 to the known budget_prepared status yields policy_confirmed for the economic forecast. This step follows the standard modus ponens pattern: the antecedent holds, so the consequent must hold as well.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the fact that the economic forecast is budget_prepared and the rule linking budget_prepared to policy_confirmed, we infer that the economic forecast is policy_confirmed.</text>
<logic>policy_confirmed(economic_forecast)</logic>

Now that we have policy_confirmed and we know there is no evidence of an abnormal activity condition, the default rule from Premise 2 applies. Because both required conditions are satisfied, we can safely infer that the economic forecast is market_active.

<logic>market_active(economic_forecast)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Economic_forecast is market active, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Nadia is confident, and Diana is knowledgeable. Whenever Diana is creative, she is also consistent. Finally, either Diana is not diligent or Diana is creative; at least one of those alternatives is true.
examples.question: Is it true that Nadia is not scholarly?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Nadia is not scholarly. The knowledge base gives us several facts about Nadia and Diana, plus conditional rules and two‑way alternatives. Our goal is to see if we can derive scholarly(nadia); if we can, the negation will be false.

This premise introduces a conditional link between being well prepared and being open minded for Nadia. It will be useful later when we manage to establish that Nadia is well prepared.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Nadia is well prepared, then she is open minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(nadia) :- well_prepared(nadia)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that cheerfulness guarantees scholarly status for Nadia. If we can show Nadia is cheerful, we will immediately obtain scholarly(nadia).
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Nadia is cheerful, then she is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(nadia) :- cheerful(nadia)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that experiencing something forces a person to lose confidence. It will allow us to turn a conflict between confidence and lack of confidence into information about experience.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Nadia is experienced, then she is not confident.</text>
<logic>~confident(nadia) :- experienced(nadia)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule creates a tension: if Nadia were energetic, she could not be open minded. This will help us eliminate the energetic alternative later.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Nadia is energetic, then she is not open minded.</text>
<logic>~open_minded(nadia) :- energetic(nadia)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of energetic or cheerful holds for Nadia. It gives us a fork that we can later resolve once we rule out one side.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Nadia is energetic or Nadia is cheerful.</text>
<logic>energetic(nadia) v cheerful(nadia)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, this disjunction says Nadia is either experienced or well prepared. It will become decisive after we learn something about experience.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Nadia is experienced or Nadia is well prepared.</text>
<logic>experienced(nadia) v well_prepared(nadia)</logic>
</step>

A simple fact: Diana possesses knowledge. Although not directly about Nadia, it activates the rule that knowledgeable people are diligent, which may be relevant for other parts of the puzzle.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Diana is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(diana)</logic>
</step>

Another basic fact: Nadia is confident. Combined with the rule linking experience to not‑confidence, this creates a contradiction that we can exploit.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Nadia is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(nadia)</logic>
</step>

Since Nadia is confident, the rule that experienced implies not confident tells us that if Nadia were experienced, she would have to be not confident, which contradicts the fact that she is confident. Therefore Nadia cannot be experienced, giving us ~experienced(nadia).
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Because Nadia is confident and the rule that experienced implies not confident, we can infer that Nadia cannot be experienced.</text>
<logic>~experienced(nadia)</logic>
</step>

We now have ~experienced(nadia) and the disjunction experienced(nadia) v well_prepared(nadia). Because the experienced alternative is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Nadia to be well prepared. Hence we derive well_prepared(nadia).
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>Since Nadia is not experienced and we know that either she is experienced or well prepared, we conclude that Nadia must be well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(nadia)</logic>
</step>

Having established well_prepared(nadia) and knowing the rule that well prepared leads to open minded, we can directly infer open_minded(nadia). This gives us a positive property that will later clash with the energetic‑to‑not‑open‑minded rule.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>From Nadia being well prepared and the rule linking well prepared to open minded, we deduce that Nadia is open minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(nadia)</logic>
</step>

The rule says energetic(nadia) leads to ~open_minded(nadia). Since we already have open_minded(nadia), the antecedent (energetic) must be false; otherwise we would have both open_minded and not open_minded. Thus we conclude ~energetic(nadia).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>Given Nadia is open minded and the rule that energetic leads to not open minded, we infer that Nadia cannot be energetic.</text>
<logic>~energetic(nadia)</logic>
</step>

With ~energetic(nadia) and the earlier disjunction energetic(nadia) v cheerful(nadia), the energetic side is eliminated, leaving cheerful(nadia) as the only remaining possibility. Therefore Nadia is cheerful.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,5">
<text>Because Nadia is not energetic and we have the disjunction that she is either energetic or cheerful, we must conclude that Nadia is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(nadia)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Nadia is cheerful, the rule cheerful → scholarly allows us to infer scholarly(nadia). This directly contradicts the query ~scholarly(nadia).
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>With Nadia being cheerful and the rule that cheerful implies scholarly, we derive that Nadia is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(nadia)</logic>
</step>

We derived Nadia is scholarly, so the conclusion Nadia is not scholarly is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is the rook not thoughtful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the rook lacks the property of being thoughtful. To answer this, we must use the facts about the rook's aggressiveness and the conditional rules linking aggressiveness, insight, and structure, as well as the rule that a thoughtful rook would contradict its structure. By chaining these implications we can see if the assumption of thoughtfulness leads to a contradiction.

The rule that an insightful rook is structured is crucial because it connects the property we will later derive (insight) to a concrete attribute (structured). Recognizing this link lets us later test whether the opposite condition (thoughtful) can coexist with structure.

<logic>structured(rook) :- insightful(rook)</logic>
</step>

The rule stating that an aggressive rook is insightful gives us a bridge from the known fact about the rook's aggressiveness to the insight property. This connection is necessary to start the chain of reasoning that will eventually produce the structured attribute.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a rook is aggressive, then it is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(rook) :- aggressive(rook)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that a thoughtful rook would be not structured provides the contradictory counterpart we need. If we can show the rook is structured, this rule will let us infer that the rook cannot be thoughtful.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a rook is thoughtful, then it is not structured.</text>
<logic>~structured(rook) :- thoughtful(rook)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the bishop is tactical is included for completeness of the knowledge base, though it does not directly affect the current query about the rook. It shows the broader context of the training camp.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The bishop is tactical.</text>
<logic>tactical(bishop)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the knight being clever is a factual detail that supports other rules in the domain but is not directly used in establishing the rook's thoughtfulness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The knight is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(knight)</logic>
</step>

The explicit statement that the rook is aggressive is the starting point for our derivation. It gives us a concrete premise from which we can apply the aggressive‑to‑insight rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rook is aggressive.</text>
<logic>aggressive(rook)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the rook is aggressive together with the rule that aggression yields insight, we can confidently assert that the rook is insightful. This step translates the conditional relationship into a concrete property for the rook.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">

Now that we have established the rook is insightful, we apply the insight‑to‑structure rule. Because an insightful rook is structured, we can conclude the rook possesses the structured attribute.

</step>

With the rook proven to be structured, we turn to the rule that a thoughtful rook would be not structured. Since the rook is already structured, the only way to satisfy both statements is for the rook not to be thoughtful. Hence we derive that the rook is not thoughtful.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">

Therefore Rook is not thoughtful, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: Player_arden is spry. Any spry character is skilled. Any skilled character is neutral. Whenever a character is neutral, it is also strict. If a character is efficient, then it is not strict. The character is either efficient or kindhearted.
examples.question: Is player_arden kindhearted?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether player_arden possesses the quality of being kindhearted. To answer this, we will trace the chain of properties that connect the known facts about player_arden to the desired conclusion.

The first premise tells us how strictness arises: any character that is neutral must also be strict. This conditional will later allow us to move from neutrality to strictness if we can establish neutrality for player_arden.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a character is neutral, then it is strict.</text>
<logic>strict(player_arden) :- neutral(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links skilledness to neutrality, stating that every skilled character is neutral. This gives us a route from skilled to neutral, which we will need after we prove player_arden is skilled.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that being skilled implies being neutral.</text>
<logic>neutral(player_arden) :- skilled(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

The third premise provides the bridge from being spry to being skilled. Since we already know player_arden is spry, this rule will let us infer skilledness directly.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule indicates that being spry leads to being skilled.</text>
<logic>skilled(player_arden) :- spry(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise offers a negative relationship: if a character is efficient, then it cannot be strict. This will be crucial for eliminating the possibility that player_arden is efficient later on.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that if a character is efficient, then it cannot be strict.</text>
<logic>~strict(player_arden) :- efficient(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise is a disjunction asserting that player_arden is either efficient or kindhearted. Knowing that one of these must hold will let us conclude kindheartedness once we rule out efficiency.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the character is either efficient or kindhearted.</text>
<logic>efficient(player_arden) v kindhearted(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise gives us the concrete fact that player_arden is spry. This is the starting point for the forward chain of implications that will eventually lead to the target property.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>We know that player_arden is spry.</text>
<logic>spry(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that player_arden is spry together with the rule that spry implies skilled, we can deduce that player_arden is skilled. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens, moving one step forward in the implication chain.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>From player_arden being spry and the rule that spry implies skilled, we infer that player_arden is skilled.</text>
<logic>skilled(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established player_arden is skilled, we apply the rule that skilled characters are neutral. By modus ponens again, we infer that player_arden is neutral, bringing us closer to strictness.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Since player_arden is skilled and skilled implies neutral, we conclude that player_arden is neutral.</text>
<logic>neutral(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

With player_arden confirmed as neutral, the earlier premise that neutral leads to strict allows us to conclude that player_arden is strict. This completes the forward direction from the initial fact to strictness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Given that player_arden is neutral and neutral implies strict, we deduce that player_arden is strict.</text>
<logic>strict(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

We have player_arden as strict, and we also know that if someone were efficient, they could not be strict. Since player_arned is strict, the condition "if efficient then not strict" forces the contrapositive: player_arden cannot be efficient. This is an instance of modus tollens.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Because player_arden is strict and the rule that efficient implies not strict, we can infer that player_arden cannot be efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that player_arden is not efficient, we return to the disjunction that player_arden is either efficient or kindhearted. Because the efficient option is ruled out, the only remaining possibility is that player_arden is kindhearted. This follows by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Having established that player_arden is not efficient and knowing that the character is either efficient or kindhearted, we determine that player_arden must be kindhearted.</text>
<logic>kindhearted(player_arden)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Player_arden is kindhearted, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Cedar, Dawn, and Eva are three engineers on a construction site. Cedar is cautious, and both Dawn and Eva are cautious as well. Dawn and Eva are also judicious. Whenever something is cautious, it is relaxed, so Cedar being cautious makes Cedar relaxed. If something is dedicated, then it is judicious; thus any dedicated Cedar would be judicious. If something is judicious, then it is astute, so any judicious Cedar would be astute. Being eloquent guarantees that Cedar is not astute. Whenever Cedar is not eloquent, Cedar is observant. Cedar is either not relaxed or dedicated. All these statements together constitute the knowledge base.
examples.question: Is Cedar observant?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Cedar can be classified as observant based on the information provided. The goal is to trace a chain of logical consequences that leads from the known facts to the desired property.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Cedar is judicious, then Cedar is astute.</text>
<logic>astute(cedar) :- judicious(cedar)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Cedar is dedicated, then Cedar is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(cedar) :- dedicated(cedar)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Cedar is not eloquent, Cedar is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(cedar) :- ~eloquent(cedar)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Cedar is cautious, then Cedar is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(cedar) :- cautious(cedar)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Cedar is eloquent, then Cedar is not astute.</text>
<logic>~astute(cedar) :- eloquent(cedar)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Cedar is either not relaxed or dedicated.</text>
<logic>~relaxed(cedar) v dedicated(cedar)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Dawn is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(dawn)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Eva is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(eva)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Dawn is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(dawn)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Eva is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(eva)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Cedar is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(cedar)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Cedar is cautious is explicitly given. The rule stating that any cautious entity is relaxed allows us to apply universal instantiation and infer that Cedar must be relaxed as well. This establishes the first link in the chain toward the target property.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>From Cedar being cautious and the rule that cautious implies relaxed, we infer that Cedar is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(cedar)</logic>
</step>

The knowledge base also contains a disjunction: Cedar is either not relaxed or dedicated. Since we have just established that Cedar is relaxed, the first alternative of the disjunction is false. By disjunctive syllogism, the only remaining possibility is that Cedar is dedicated, giving us a new fact to work with.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,6">
<text>Because Cedar is relaxed, the disjunction ‘Cedar is not relaxed or Cedar is dedicated’ forces Cedar to be dedicated.</text>
<logic>dedicated(cedar)</logic>
</step>

Having derived that Cedar is dedicated, we can employ the rule that dedicated entities are judicious. By modus ponens, the dedicated status of Cedar immediately yields that Cedar is judicious, moving us closer to the astute condition needed later.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>Given that Cedar is dedicated and the rule that dedicated implies judicious, we conclude Cedar is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(cedar)</logic>
</step>

The rule linking judiciousness to astuteness tells us that any judicious entity is also astute. Since we now know Cedar is judicious, we can infer that Cedar is astute. This fact will later allow us to reason about the negation of eloquence.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,1">
<text>Since Cedar is judicious and judicious implies astute, Cedar is astute.</text>
<logic>astute(cedar)</logic>
</step>

We also have a rule stating that if Cedar were eloquent, then Cedar would not be astute. Because we have just shown Cedar is astute, the antecedent of that conditional must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Using modus tollens, we conclude that Cedar cannot be eloquent, i.e., Cedar is not eloquent.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,5">
<text>The rule ‘If Cedar is eloquent then Cedar is not astute’ together with Cedar being astute tells us that Cedar cannot be eloquent; therefore Cedar is not eloquent.</text>
<logic>~eloquent(cedar)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule that a non‑eloquent Cedar is observant directly applies. Since we have established Cedar is not eloquent, we can immediately infer that Cedar is observant. This completes the logical chain needed to answer the query.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,3">
<text>With Cedar not being eloquent and the rule that non‑eloquent implies observant, we derive that Cedar is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(cedar)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Cedar is observant, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Dallas is known to be generous. Whenever someone is generous, that person is also brave, so Dallas’s generosity makes him brave. If a person is rational, then they cannot be honest, establishing a conflict between rationality and honesty. These statements together provide the full set of facts, rules, and the disjunction that will be used to determine whether Dallas is logical.

Bravery leads to patience, creating a forward path from the generous‑to‑brave link to the patient‑to‑honest rule. It ensures that once we have bravery, we can continue the chain toward honesty.

The rational‑to‑not‑honest rule provides a way to contradict honesty if rationality were true. It will be used together with the honesty derived earlier to show that rationality must be false.

The disjunction gives us two possible ways to proceed: either Dallas is rational or Dallas is articulate. By later disproving rationality, we will be forced to accept that Dallas is articulate.
<step n="7" type="premise">

<logic>rational(dallas) v articulate(dallas)</logic>

The fact that Dallas is generous is our starting point. All subsequent deductions will stem from this concrete piece of information.

Applying modus ponens to the generous fact and the generous‑to‑brave rule yields bravery for Dallas. This is the first derived property and unlocks the next rule in the chain.

With bravery now established, we use the brave‑to‑patient rule, again via modus ponens, to infer that Dallas is patient. This moves us closer to the honesty premise.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">

Since Dallas is patient, the patient‑to‑honest rule gives us honesty. At this point we have both honesty and a rule that ties rationality to dishonesty, setting up a conflict.

We have honesty and a rule that says a rational Dallas would not be honest. By modus tollens, if Dallas were rational, he would be not honest, but we know he is honest, so we conclude Dallas cannot be rational. This negates one side of the disjunction.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">

The disjunction states Dallas is rational or articulate. Because we have just shown Dallas is not rational, the only remaining option is that Dallas must be articulate. This uses disjunctive syllogism to obtain articulation.

The rule linking spectral precision to orbital stability is essential because it allows us to translate the known property of the nebula into another useful property. Recognizing this connection will let us use the disjunction that involves orbital stability.

<logic>orbital_stable(nebula) :- spectral_precise(nebula)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a choice: either the nebula lacks orbital stability or it possesses luminescence. This statement will become decisive once we know whether the nebula is orbital stable.
<step n="2" type="premise">

</step>

Since the nebula is spectrally precise and any spectrally precise object is orbital stable, we can infer that the nebula is orbital stable. This follows the standard modus ponens pattern, moving from a condition and its antecedent to the consequent.

Now that we have established the nebula is orbital stable, the left side of the disjunction (~orbital_stable) is false, so the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the right side (luminescent) to be true. Hence, the nebula must be luminescent, obtained by disjunctive syllogism.

examples.context: In the psychological assessment, Remy is known to be wise. The evaluator notes that whenever Remy is diplomatic, he also behaves in a meticulous way. Conversely, if Remy happens to be eloquent, this guarantees that he is not wise. Moreover, the report says that Remy must be either eloquent or diplomatic, though it does not specify which one holds. These are the only pieces of information available for the reasoning task.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Remy is not meticulous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We start with a collection of traits and conditional statements about Remy. The goal is to see whether the information forces the conclusion that Remy is not meticulous.

The first rule tells us that diplomatic behavior guarantees meticulousness. This will become useful later when we learn whether Remy is diplomatic.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Remy is diplomatic, then Remy is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(remy) :- diplomatic(remy)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links eloquence to the negation of wisdom. It will help us eliminate the eloquent alternative once we compare it with the fact that Remy is wise.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Remy is eloquent, then Remy is not wise.</text>
<logic>~wise(remy) :- eloquent(remy)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction informs us that Remy must possess at least one of the two traits: eloquent or diplomatic. This creates a logical fork that we will resolve using other premises.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Remy is either eloquent or diplomatic.</text>
<logic>eloquent(remy) v diplomatic(remy)</logic>
</step>

We are also given the concrete fact that Remy is wise, which will interact with the rule about eloquence to narrow down the possibilities.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Remy is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(remy)</logic>
</step>

Since we know Remy is wise, the conditional that an eloquent Remy would be not wise cannot hold. Therefore, the only way to keep the premises consistent is to assert that Remy is not eloquent. This eliminates one side of the disjunction.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Because Remy is wise and the rule says an eloquent Remy would be not wise, we infer that Remy cannot be eloquent.</text>
<logic>~eloquent(remy)</logic>
</step>

With the eloquent option ruled out, the disjunction that Remy is either eloquent or diplomatic forces the remaining choice: Remy must be diplomatic. This step directly follows from the principle of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Since Remy is not eloquent and we know he must be either eloquent or diplomatic, the only remaining option is that Remy is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(remy)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Remy is diplomatic, we can apply the first rule, which says diplomatic implies meticulous. Consequently, we derive that Remy is meticulous.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Given that Remy is diplomatic and diplomatic implies meticulous, we deduce that Remy is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(remy)</logic>
</step>

We derived Remy is meticulous, so the conclusion Remy is not meticulous is False.
</think>
Answer: False

The fact that Skyler is rational is the key factual trigger for the first rule. Knowing Skyler's rationality enables us to activate the anxiety rule and start the chain toward confidence.

We derived Skyler is confident, so the conclusion Skyler is not confident is False.

examples.completion:

The first premise gives us a conditional relationship: if Tess is prepared, then she is thorough. This rule will allow us to derive thoroughness once we confirm preparation. It is directly relevant because we already have a fact about preparation.

<text>The rule that if Tess is prepared then she is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(tess) :- prepared(tess)</logic>
</step>

The second premise presents a disjunction: either Tess is not thorough or she is dedicated. This will become useful after we know whether Tess is thorough, because the truth of one side eliminates the other, potentially giving us dedication.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating that either Tess is not thorough or Tess is dedicated.</text>
<logic>~thorough(tess) v dedicated(tess)</logic>
</step>

The third premise states the concrete fact that Tess is prepared. This is the starting point for the deduction, as it activates the conditional rule from Premise 1.
<step n="3" type="premise">

<logic>prepared(tess)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, we combine the fact that Tess is prepared (Premise 3) with the rule that preparation implies thoroughness (Premise 1). Since the antecedent holds, we can conclude that Tess is thorough. This new fact will let us evaluate the disjunction in the next step.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From Tess being prepared and the rule, we infer that Tess is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(tess)</logic>
</step>

Now we have the statement that Tess is thorough. Looking back at the disjunction (Premise 2), one of its components is "Tess is not thorough". Because we have established that Tess is indeed thorough, the negated component is false, so the only way for the disjunction to remain true is for the other component, "Tess is dedicated", to be true. Thus we infer that Tess is dedicated.

<text>Because Tess is thorough, the disjunction forces the other option, so Tess must be dedicated.</text>

Therefore Tess is dedicated, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Is Rory curious?

This conditional links evidence driven status to responsibility for Rory. Once we know Rory is evidence driven, we can infer his responsibility.

<logic>responsible(rory) :- evidence_driven(rory)</logic>

This rule states that punctuality would contradict responsibility for Rory. It will help us eliminate the punctual alternative later in the proof.
<step n="4" type="premise">

<logic>~responsible(rory) :- punctual(rory)</logic>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities for Rory: either he is punctual or he is curious. By ruling out punctuality we will be forced to accept curiosity.

<logic>punctual(rory) v curious(rory)</logic>

The fact that Indie is clear headed is part of the background knowledge but does not directly affect the chain leading to Rory's curiosity.
<step n="6" type="premise">

<logic>clear_headed(indie)</logic>
</step>

Ralph being decisive is another background fact that does not play a role in the current derivation about Rory.
<step n="7" type="premise">

<logic>decisive(ralph)</logic>
</step>

Rory being scholarly is the crucial factual starting point for the chain of implications that will eventually lead to his curiosity.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Rory is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(rory)</logic>
</step>

Using PREMISE_2 together with the fact that Rory is scholarly, we apply modus ponens to obtain that Rory is insightful. This gives us the first link needed for the later steps.

Now that we have established Rory's insight, PREMISE_1 allows us to infer that Rory is evidence driven by another application of modus ponens.

With Rory's evidence driven status in hand, PREMISE_3 lets us conclude that Rory is responsible, again via modus ponens.

<logic>responsible(rory)</logic>
</step>

We now have Rory as responsible, but PREMISE_4 says that if Rory were punctual, he would be not responsible. Since both responsible and not responsible cannot hold simultaneously, we use modus tollens to deduce that Rory cannot be punctual.

Phoenix being resilient is a foundational fact. Combined with Premise 1, it enables us to derive adaptability for phoenix.

Using Modus Ponens, we combine the fact that phoenix is resilient (Premise 5) with the rule that resilience implies adaptability (Premise 1). This yields the new conclusion that phoenix is adaptable, which moves us one step closer to establishing brilliance.

examples.context: Master Jane is marked as ab_verified. Master Jane is respectful. Student Riley is respectful. If a person is focused and there is no indication that they are ab_dedicated, then that person is committed.
examples.question: Is student Riley committed?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether student Riley is committed. The goal is to derive the committed property for the student using the given facts and default rules.

The rule linking certification to focus will later allow us to turn a certified status into a focused one. Recognizing this connection is essential for the chain of inference.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever the student is certified, they are considered focused.</text>
<logic>focused(student_riley) :- certified(student_riley)</logic>
</step>

This default rule tells us that focus together with the absence of an abnormality leads to commitment. It will be applied once we have established that the student is focused.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the student is focused and there is no indication they are ab_dedicated, then they are committed.</text>
<logic>committed(student_riley) :- focused(student_riley), not ab_committed(student_riley)</logic>
</step>

The default rule for certification is crucial because it provides a way to obtain the certified status from the respectful fact, provided no ab_verified evidence exists. This will be the first step in the reasoning.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the student is respectful and there is no sign they are ab_verified, then they become certified.</text>
<logic>certified(student_riley) :- respectful(student_riley), not ab_certified(student_riley)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Master Jane is ab_verified is included for completeness, but it does not affect the student directly. It shows that the abnormality predicate can be true for some individuals.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Master Jane is marked as ab_verified.</text>
<logic>ab_certified(master_jane)</logic>
</step>

Master Jane being respectful is another background fact that does not directly involve the student, yet it mirrors the pattern used for the student’s respectful status.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Master Jane is respectful.</text>
<logic>respectful(master_jane)</logic>
</step>

Student Riley being respectful gives us the antecedent needed for the certification default rule. This fact is the starting point for deriving the certified property.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Student Riley is respectful.</text>
<logic>respectful(student_riley)</logic>
</step>

From the respectful fact about the student and the lack of any ab_verified fact concerning the student, the certification default rule applies, granting student Riley the certified status. This step uses the default reasoning mechanism to open the chain.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Because the student is respectful and there is no evidence they are ab_verified, we can infer that they are certified.</text>
<logic>certified(student_riley)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the student is certified, we invoke the rule that certification implies focus. By modus ponens, the certified condition yields the focused property for the student.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since the student is certified, the rule tells us they are focused.</text>
<logic>focused(student_riley)</logic>
</step>

Now that the student is known to be focused, we can apply the commitment default rule. Because there is no ab_dedicated fact about the student, the rule concludes that the student is committed.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>With the student being focused and lacking any ab_dedicated indication, we conclude they are committed.</text>
<logic>committed(student_riley)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Student_riley is committed, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the mythic realm, Lia is known to be brave. The phoenix is described as cautious, and Luna is celebrated for being strong. Moreover, either the phoenix is not articulate or the phoenix is eloquent. Either Luna is not healthy or Luna is loyal. Finally, either Lia is not strategic or Lia is agile.
examples.question: Is it true that the phoenix is not confident?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the phoenix lacks confidence. To answer this, we must see if the knowledge base forces the phoenix to be confident, which would contradict the negation.

This premise connects the phoenix's cautious nature to being articulate. It will become useful once we establish that the phoenix is indeed cautious.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the phoenix is cautious, then the phoenix is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(phoenix) :- cautious(phoenix)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a conditional linking eloquence to confidence for the phoenix. If we can show the phoenix is eloquent, we will immediately obtain confidence.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the phoenix is eloquent, then the phoenix is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(phoenix) :- eloquent(phoenix)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either the phoenix is not articulate, or it is eloquent. It will allow us to move from knowledge about articulation to a statement about eloquence.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either the phoenix is not articulate or the phoenix is eloquent.</text>
<logic>~articulate(phoenix) v eloquent(phoenix)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Lia is brave is recorded, but it does not directly affect the phoenix's confidence. It is included to satisfy the requirement that all facts be mentioned.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Lia is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(lia)</logic>
</step>

Luna's strength is noted as a fact. Like Lia's bravery, it does not play a role in the current chain of reasoning about the phoenix.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Luna is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(luna)</logic>
</step>

The phoenix being cautious is a key fact because it activates the rule from Premise 1, enabling us to derive articulation.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that the phoenix is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(phoenix)</logic>
</step>

Because we know the phoenix is cautious (Premise 6) and the rule says cautious implies articulate (Premise 1), we can confidently assert that the phoenix is articulate. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From the fact that the phoenix is cautious and the rule linking cautious to articulate, we infer that the phoenix is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(phoenix)</logic>
</step>

Now we have the phoenix articulated (Step 7). The disjunction from Premise 3 tells us that either the phoenix is not articulate or it is eloquent. Since we have established that the phoenix is indeed articulate, the first alternative of the disjunction is false, forcing the second alternative to be true. Hence the phoenix must be eloquent.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Using the disjunction that the phoenix is either not articulate or eloquent, together with the newly derived fact that the phoenix is articulate, we conclude that the phoenix must be eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(phoenix)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained eloquence for the phoenix (Step 8) and knowing the rule that eloquence leads to confidence (Premise 2), we can apply modus ponens again to infer that the phoenix is confident.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given that the phoenix is eloquent and the rule that eloquence leads to confidence, we deduce that the phoenix is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(phoenix)</logic>
</step>

We derived Phoenix is confident, so the conclusion Phoenix is not confident is False.
</think>

The seventh premise tells us that productivity forces a lack of courage. It will allow us to eliminate the possibility that Cleo is productive once we have established her courage.

examples.question: Is Olive strategic?
examples.completion:

We need to decide whether Olive possesses the strategic property. The goal is to trace a chain of logical consequences from the given facts and rules that leads to strategic(olive).

The first rule links determination to anxiety, which will be useful because we already know Olive is determined. Establishing anxious(olive) creates a bridge to later rules involving anxiety.

The third rule tells us that gratitude guarantees strategic status. This is the crucial link we need to reach the final query, so we must eventually derive grateful(olive).

<logic>strategic(olive) :- grateful(olive)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that being perceptive forces non‑anxiety. This contrapositive relationship will allow us to eliminate the perceptive alternative later in the proof.

<text>The rule says that any perceptive entity is not anxious.</text>

The disjunction states Olive is either perceptive or focused. Knowing one of these must hold will let us use the previous rules to move forward once we eliminate one option.

<logic>perceptive(olive) v focused(olive)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction offers a choice between not‑scholarly and grateful. If we can show Olive is scholarly, the only way to satisfy the disjunction is that Olive must be grateful, which is exactly what we need.

<text>It is asserted that either Olive is not scholarly or Olive is grateful.</text>

</step>

The fact that Olive is determined gives us a solid starting point. It directly activates the first rule, allowing us to produce an anxious conclusion.

<logic>determined(olive)</logic>

Applying Modus Ponens to the fact that Olive is determined and the rule that determined implies anxious, we obtain anxious(olive). This step converts the initial fact into a new property that will interact with other rules.

<logic>anxious(olive)</logic>
</step>

We now have anxious(olive) and the rule that perceptive implies not anxious. By Modus Tollens, if Olive were perceptive she would not be anxious, contradicting our derived anxious(olive). Therefore Olive cannot be perceptive, giving us ~perceptive(olive).
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since Olive is anxious and the fourth rule tells us that perceptiveness would preclude anxiety, we conclude Olive is not perceptive.</text>

Since we have established that Olive is not perceptive and we know from the disjunction that Olive is either perceptive or focused, the only remaining possibility is that Olive is focused. Thus we infer focused(olive) by Disjunctive Syllogism.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">

<logic>focused(olive)</logic>
</step>

With focused(olive) in hand, we use the second rule (focused → scholarly) via Modus Ponens to deduce scholarly(olive). This moves us closer to the gratitude alternative in the second disjunction.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">

<logic>scholarly(olive)</logic>

Now we possess scholarly(olive). The disjunction ~scholarly(olive) v grateful(olive) requires at least one of its literals to be true. Because scholarly(olive) makes ~scholarly(olive) false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that grateful(olive) must be true. Hence we derive grateful(olive).

examples.question: Does Chef Kira's preparation end up sizzling?

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">

This conditional states that responsibility would force Zara to be not proactive. By later establishing that Zara is indeed proactive, we will be able to argue that she cannot be responsible.

<logic>~proactive(zara) :- responsible(zara)</logic>

Ada being strategic is a factual statement that, while not directly about Zara, illustrates the troupe's strategic nature. It confirms the relevance of the strategic‑to‑proficient rule across members.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Ada is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(ada)</logic>
</step>

Emma's strategic status similarly reinforces the universal applicability of the strategic‑to‑proficient rule. It helps us trust that the rule holds for Zara as well.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Emma is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(emma)</logic>
</step>

Zane being strategic adds another instance confirming the rule's consistency within the group. It supports the logical environment in which Zara's properties are evaluated.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Zane is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(zane)</logic>
</step>

The key fact is that Zara herself is strategic. This directly activates the rule linking strategic to proficient, giving us the first piece of the chain we need.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Zara is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(zara)</logic>
</step>

By applying the strategic‑to‑proficient rule to Zara's strategic fact, we infer that Zara is proficient. This step bridges the given fact to the next conditional premise.

<logic>proactive(zara)</logic>

Since we have shown Zara is proactive, and the responsibility rule says a responsible Zara would be not proactive, we can deduce that Zara cannot be responsible. This uses the contrapositive reasoning of the rule, yielding a negated responsibility claim.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">

We derived Zara is not responsible, so the conclusion Zara is responsible is False.

examples.context: Within the protected reserve known as Eden, the monitoring team reports that Eden is currently being actively monitored. The conservation guidelines state that any creature that maintains a low profile is also considered to have its habitat assessed. Furthermore, whenever a creature's habitat has been assessed, it is regarded as stress‑free. The rules also specify that if a creature acts on instinct, then it cannot be under active monitoring. Finally, the observations note that Eden is either instinctive or maintains a low profile (or possibly both).
examples.question: Is it true that Eden is not stress‑free?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Eden is not stress‑free. To answer this, we must use the given facts, rules, and the disjunction about Eden's properties, and see if we can prove that Eden is stress‑free. If we can derive stress_free(Eden), then the negation will be false.

The first premise gives us a conditional link: being low_profile guarantees being habitat_assessed. This will be useful later when we manage to show Eden is low_profile, allowing us to infer habitat assessment.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Eden is low_profile, then Eden is habitat_assessed.</text>
<logic>habitat_assessed(eden) :- low_profile(eden)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that any entity whose habitat has been assessed is automatically stress_free. This creates a chain from low_profile to stress_free once we establish the intermediate step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Eden is habitat_assessed, then Eden is stress_free.</text>
<logic>stress_free(eden) :- habitat_assessed(eden)</logic>
</step>

The third premise states that instinctive behavior prevents active monitoring. This relationship will let us eliminate the possibility of Eden being instinctive once we know Eden is actively monitored.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Eden is instinctive, then Eden is not monitoring_active.</text>
<logic>~monitoring_active(eden) :- instinctive(eden)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise presents a disjunction: Eden is either instinctive or low_profile (or both). This gives us two possible ways to proceed, and we will later narrow down which one actually holds.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Eden is instinctive or Eden is low_profile (or both).</text>
<logic>instinctive(eden) v low_profile(eden)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise is a concrete fact: Eden is currently being actively monitored, i.e., monitoring_active(Eden) is true. This fact will clash with the third premise if Eden were instinctive.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Eden is monitoring_active.</text>
<logic>monitoring_active(eden)</logic>
</step>

Because Eden is monitoring_active, the third premise implies that Eden cannot be instinctive—otherwise we would have both monitoring_active and not monitoring_active, a contradiction. examples.context: Phoebe is brilliant. Jules is clear headed. Ralph is well prepared. Whenever something is clear headed, it is also competitive, so Jules being clear headed makes Jules competitive. Confidence leads to strength, so confident Ralph implies strong Ralph. Athletic Ralph would cause Ralph not to be strong, expressed as athletic Ralph implies not strong Ralph. One of the following must hold: either Jules is not competitive or Jules is motivated. Either Phoebe is not inventive or Phoebe is judicious. Either Ralph is not relaxed or Ralph is confident.
examples.question: Is Ralph athletic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Ralph possesses the property athletic. The goal is to see if the premises force Ralph to be athletic or to rule it out. By chaining the given rules and facts we can evaluate Ralph's status.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Ralph is strategic, then Ralph is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(ralph) :- strategic(ralph)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Ralph is well prepared, then Ralph is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(ralph) :- well_prepared(ralph)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Ralph is confident, then Ralph is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(ralph) :- confident(ralph)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Ralph is athletic, then Ralph is not strong.</text>
<logic>~strong(ralph) :- athletic(ralph)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says either Ralph is not relaxed or Ralph is confident.</text>
<logic>~relaxed(ralph) v confident(ralph)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Phoebe is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(phoebe)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Jules is clear headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Ralph is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(ralph)</logic>
</step>

We start with the fact that Ralph is well prepared. The rule "well prepared implies strategic" allows us to apply modus ponens, giving us that Ralph is strategic. This establishes the first link in the chain toward the property we care about.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>From Ralph being well prepared and the rule that well prepared implies strategic, we infer that Ralph is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(ralph)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Ralph strategic, we can use the rule "strategic implies relaxed". Applying modus ponens again yields Ralph relaxed. This step is crucial because the disjunction we will use later mentions "not relaxed".
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Since Ralph is strategic and the rule that strategic implies relaxed, we conclude Ralph is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(ralph)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says either Ralph is not relaxed or Ralph is confident. Since we have just shown Ralph is relaxed, the first alternative is false, forcing the second alternative to be true. Therefore we can conclude Ralph is confident, moving us closer to the strength condition.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Given the disjunction that either Ralph is not relaxed or Ralph is confident, and knowing Ralph is relaxed, we must accept the second alternative, so Ralph is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(ralph)</logic>
</step>

With Ralph confident established, we turn to the rule that confidence leads to strength. Using modus ponens, we infer that Ralph is strong. This gives us a positive statement that will later be contrasted with the rule linking athleticism to non‑strength.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Because Ralph is confident and confidence leads to strength, we derive that Ralph is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(ralph)</logic>
</step>

We now have Ralph strong, and we also have the rule that if Ralph were athletic, then Ralph would not be strong. This is a classic modus tollens situation: the consequent (not strong) is contradicted by the known fact (strong), so the antecedent (athletic) must be false. Hence we derive that Ralph is not athletic.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Ralph is strong and the rule that athletic would make him not strong lets us use modus tollens to infer that Ralph is not athletic.</text>
<logic>~athletic(ralph)</logic>
</step>

We derived Ralph is not athletic, so the conclusion Ralph is athletic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

Applying the empathy‑to‑responsibility rule to the known fact that Arden is empathetic yields that Arden must be responsible. This is a straightforward use of Modus Ponens: the antecedent holds, so the consequent follows.

Noel's competitiveness is the critical fact that activates Premise 2, letting us start the chain of derivations toward rationality. It is the anchor for the subsequent steps.

Having established Noel's industriousness, we turn to Premise 1: industrious implies ambitious. Consequently, Noel is ambitious. This moves us one step closer to obtaining fair‑mindedness.
