<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that being focused leads to being prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(clover) :- focused(clover)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that cleverness forces the opposite of being active.</text>
<logic>~active(clover) :- clever(clover)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Clover is not logical or Clover is observant.</text>
<logic>~logical(clover) v observant(clover)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Clover is not prepared or Clover is strategic.</text>
<logic>~prepared(clover) v strategic(clover)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Mark is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(mark)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Arden is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(arden)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Noel is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(noel)</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="premise">
<text>Mark is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(mark)</logic>
</step>

<step n="13" type="premise">
<text>Mark is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(mark)</logic>
</step>

<step n="14" type="premise">
<text>Clover is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(clover)</logic>
</step>

Given the fact that Clover is vigorous, we can apply the rule linking vigor to focus. This directly yields that Clover is focused, establishing the first link in the chain toward preparation. The inference is straightforward because the rule is universal for any vigorous entity.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,3">
<text>From Clover's vigor we infer that Clover is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(clover)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Clover is focused, we turn to the rule that every focused entity is prepared. By modus ponens, the focused status of Clover allows us to conclude that Clover is prepared. This moves us one step closer to the strategic condition needed later.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,5">
<text>Because Clover is focused, we can conclude that Clover is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(clover)</logic>
</step>

Now we face a disjunction that says either Clover is not prepared or Clover is strategic. Since we have just proven that Clover is indeed prepared, the left side of the disjunction is false, forcing the right side to be true. Consequently, we can assert that Clover is strategic.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,8">
<text>Since Clover is prepared, the disjunction forces Clover to be strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(clover)</logic>
</step>

The rule that strategic entities are active applies to Clover now that we know she is strategic. By applying this implication, we derive that Clover is active. This active status will be crucial for the next step involving cleverness.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,1">
<text>Clover's strategic nature lets us deduce that Clover is active.</text>
<logic>active(clover)</logic>
</step>

We have the rule stating that cleverness leads to the negation of activity. Its contrapositive lets us infer that if an entity is active, then it cannot be clever. Since Clover is active, we deduce that Clover is not clever. This not‑clever condition will activate the brave rule.
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,6">
<text>Active Clover together with the rule about cleverness yields that Clover is not clever.</text>
<logic>~clever(clover)</logic>
</step>

The brave rule activates whenever an entity is not clever. With the newly obtained fact that Clover is not clever, we can immediately infer that Clover is brave. This bravery sets up the subsequent logical rule.
<step n="20" type="derived" from="19,2">
<text>Not cleverness activates the brave rule, so Clover becomes brave.</text>
<logic>brave(clover)</logic>
</step>

The logical rule tells us that any brave entity is also logical. Because we have just shown that Clover is brave, we can conclude that Clover is logical. This logical status is the final piece needed for the observant disjunction.
<step n="21" type="derived" from="20,4">
<text>Brave Clover triggers the logical rule, giving us logical Clover.</text>
<logic>logical(clover)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction concerning logical and observant states says either Clover is not logical or Clover is observant. Since we have established that Clover is logical, the first alternative is false, which forces the second alternative to be true. Therefore, we can safely conclude that Clover is observant.
<step n="22" type="derived" from="21,7">
<text>Logical Clover combined with the logical‑or‑observant disjunction yields observant Clover.</text>
<logic>observant(clover)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Clover is observant, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Craig is relaxed. Ivan is relaxed. Sofia is relaxed. Whenever Sofia is relaxed, she is also calm. If Sofia is calm, then she is well prepared. If Sofia is strategic, then she is not well prepared. No other rules or defaults apply.
examples.question: Is it true that Sofia is not strategic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given information about three carpenters and several conditional statements that link their mental states. The goal is to determine whether Sofia can be considered strategic, i.e., to prove ~strategic(sofia).

The first rule connects being relaxed to being calm for Sofia. This link will be needed because we already know Sofia is relaxed, so it gives us a way to obtain the calm property, which is a prerequisite for later steps.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that Sofia being relaxed guarantees she is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(sofia) :- relaxed(sofia)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that calmness leads to being well prepared. Once we have established Sofia's calmness, this rule will allow us to derive that she is well prepared, an essential fact for the final inference.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Sofia is calm, then she is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(sofia) :- calm(sofia)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a contrapositive relationship: if Sofia were strategic, she could not be well prepared. This negative consequence will be crucial when we later have a positive statement about her being well prepared.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that if Sofia is strategic, then she is not well prepared.</text>
<logic>~well_prepared(sofia) :- strategic(sofia)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Craig is relaxed is part of the background information, but it does not directly affect the chain concerning Sofia. It shows that relaxation is a common state among the carpenters.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Craig is a relaxed person.</text>
<logic>relaxed(craig)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, Ivan being relaxed adds to the overall picture of the group, yet it does not play a role in the logical path toward the query about Sofia.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Ivan is also relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(ivan)</logic>
</step>

Sofia being relaxed is the key factual premise that activates the first rule. Without this fact, we could not start the chain that leads to her calmness and subsequent properties.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Sofia is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(sofia)</logic>
</step>

Applying universal instantiation to the relaxed‑to‑calm rule with Sofia as the specific individual, and using the fact that Sofia is relaxed, we can infer that Sofia is calm. This step bridges the known fact to a new property needed for the next deduction.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From Sofia's relaxed state and the relaxed‑to‑calm rule we infer that Sofia is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(sofia)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Sofia's calmness, modus ponens allows us to employ the calm‑to‑well‑prepared rule. Consequently, we deduce that Sofia is well prepared, giving us a positive statement that will clash with the strategic‑to‑not‑well‑prepared rule.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Because Sofia is calm, the calm‑to‑well‑prepared rule lets us conclude that Sofia is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(sofia)</logic>
</step>

We possess the statement that Sofia is well prepared, and we also have the conditional that strategic implies not well prepared. Using modus tollens, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Sofia is not strategic. This flips the antecedent of the conditional to a negative conclusion.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since Sofia is well prepared, the conditional that strategic implies not well prepared forces the conclusion that Sofia is not strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(sofia)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sofia is not strategic, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

Gamma bot's calibration is explicitly stated, which will allow us to use the disjunction in Premise 2 to infer high power by ruling out the negated alternative.

Because we have Gamma bot calibrated (Premise 6) and the disjunction that Gamma bot is either not calibrated or high power (Premise 2), the calibrated fact eliminates the 'not calibrated' side. Thus, by disjunctive syllogism, Gamma bot must be high power.

examples.context: Cora is eloquent, and Casey is rational. Whenever someone is anxious, they are enthusiastic, and whenever someone is anxious, they become enthusiastic; likewise, any person who is eloquent is automatically studious. If someone is rational, then they are observant, and any observant individual is vigorous. Moreover, if a person is diplomatic, then they are not vigorous. Additionally, either Cora is not studious or Cora is anxious. These statements capture all the given facts, rules, and the single disjunction.
examples.question: Based on the information provided, can we conclude that Casey is not diplomatic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Casey can be classified as diplomatic given a collection of nutritional‑style logical statements. The goal is to see if the premises force the negation of diplomatic for Casey.

The first premise tells us that rationality leads to observance for Casey. This connection will be useful because we already know Casey is rational, allowing us to later obtain observance.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Casey is rational, then Casey is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(casey) :- rational(casey)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links observance to vigor, meaning anyone who is observant must also be vigorous. Once we have Casey's observance, this rule will let us derive vigor.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any observant person, such as Casey, must be vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(casey) :- observant(casey)</logic>
</step>

The third premise provides a conditional that if Casey were diplomatic, then Casey would be not vigorous. This creates a tension with any proof that Casey is vigorous, which we can exploit to reject the diplomatic possibility.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that if Casey were diplomatic, then Casey would not be vigorous.</text>
<logic>~vigorous(casey) :- diplomatic(casey)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise simply records that Cora possesses the eloquent property. Although not directly used in the target query, it satisfies the requirement to list all facts in the context.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that Cora is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(cora)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise records that Casey is rational. This fact activates the rational‑to‑observant rule in PREMISE_1, setting the chain of inference in motion.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that Casey is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(casey)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that Casey is rational (PREMISE_5) with the rule that rationality implies observance (PREMISE_1), we can assert Casey is observant. This is a straightforward application of the conditional rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Casey being rational and the rational‑to‑observant rule, we infer that Casey is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(casey)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Casey's observance, we apply the observant‑to‑vigorous rule (PREMISE_2). Because an observant individual must be vigorous, Casey inherits the vigorous property.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since Casey is observant and observant implies vigorous, we conclude that Casey is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(casey)</logic>

We have shown Casey is vigorous (STEP_7). The rule in PREMISE_3 says that if Casey were diplomatic, then Casey would be not vigorous. Since the consequent of that conditional is false (Casey is vigorous), the only way to keep the conditional true is for its antecedent, diplomatic, to be false. Thus we infer Casey is not diplomatic.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Because Casey is vigorous while diplomatic would force Casey to be not vigorous, we deduce that Casey cannot be diplomatic.</text>
<logic>~diplomatic(casey)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Casey is not diplomatic, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Chef Quinn is steady. If someone is steady, then they are on duty. Being data-focused guarantees being ambitious. Whenever someone is ambitious, they are also thoughtful. All thoughtful chefs are skilled. If a chef is well-versed, then they are not on duty. If a chef is inventive, then they are not skilled. Chef Quinn is either well-versed or data-focused.
examples.question: Is Chef Quinn inventive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Chef Quinn possesses the inventive quality. The goal is to see if the premises force the opposite, making him non‑inventive.

The first rule connects steadiness to being on duty, which will let us move from the known fact of steadiness toward duty. This link is essential for later contradictions.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Chef Quinn is steady, then he is on duty.</text>
<logic>on_duty(chef_quinn) :- steady(chef_quinn)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that data focus implies ambition, setting up a chain that will later produce thoughtful and skilled attributes. It will become useful once we know whether Quinn is data‑focused.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being data-focused guarantees being ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(chef_quinn) :- data_focused(chef_quinn)</logic>
</step>

The third rule turns ambition into thoughtfulness, extending the chain of properties. This will allow us to reach the skilled predicate.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Any ambitious chef is also thoughtful.</text>
<logic>thoughtful(chef_quinn) :- ambitious(chef_quinn)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that every thoughtful chef is skilled, giving us a final positive attribute to work with. Skilled will later clash with the inventive‑to‑not‑skilled rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>All thoughtful chefs are skilled.</text>
<logic>skilled(chef_quinn) :- thoughtful(chef_quinn)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule provides a way to negate duty if Quinn were well‑versed, which will later help us eliminate the well‑versed alternative in the disjunction.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If a chef is well-versed, then he is not on duty.</text>
<logic>~on_duty(chef_quinn) :- well_versed(chef_quinn)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule creates a direct conflict: being inventive forces a chef not to be skilled. This is the key rule that will let us infer non‑inventiveness once we have skilled.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If a chef is inventive, then he is not skilled.</text>
<logic>~skilled(chef_quinn) :- inventive(chef_quinn)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that Quinn must be either well‑versed or data‑focused. This gives us two possible routes, and we will see which one survives the reasoning.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Chef Quinn is either well-versed or data-focused.</text>
<logic>well_versed(chef_quinn) v data_focused(chef_quinn)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Quinn is steady is our starting point. It activates the first rule and initiates the chain of deductions.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Chef Quinn is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(chef_quinn)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Quinn is steady together with the rule that steadiness implies duty, we can assert that Quinn is on duty. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From Chef Quinn being steady we infer that he is on duty.</text>
<logic>on_duty(chef_quinn)</logic>
</step>

examples.context: In the bustling pottery studio, the artisan Hayden is known for being wise. The master potters say that any wise potter is also ambitious, so Hayden's wisdom guarantees ambition. They also note that whenever a potter is not diplomatic, that potter works industriously on the wheel, so a lack of diplomatic skill makes one industrious. Conversely, if a potter is diplomatic, they tend to lose precision in their shaping, meaning diplomatic potters are not precise. Finally, the studio lore holds that Hayden is either not ambitious or precise; one of those two possibilities must be true.
examples.question: Given the information above, is Hayden industrious?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the potter Hayden works industriously, based on a set of facts and conditional statements about his character traits. The goal is to trace a logical chain from the given premises to the target property industrious(Hayden).

This premise tells us that wisdom implies ambition. It will become useful later because we already know Hayden is wise, allowing us to infer his ambition. Establishing ambition is a stepping stone toward applying the disjunction later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any wise potter is also ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(hayden) :- wise(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a rule linking the absence of diplomatic skill to industriousness. If we can show that Hayden is not diplomatic, this rule will directly give us the desired conclusion. Thus, we keep this premise in mind for later deduction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that a potter who is not diplomatic works industriously.</text>
<logic>industrious(hayden) :- ~diplomatic(hayden)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that being diplomatic forces a potter to lack precision. This relationship will help us work backwards: if we ever obtain precision for Hayden, we can infer that he cannot be diplomatic, which in turn activates the previous rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if a potter is diplomatic, then they are not precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(hayden) :- diplomatic(hayden)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction presents two mutually exclusive possibilities for Hayden: either he is not ambitious, or he is precise. Since we will later prove that he is ambitious, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for him to be precise. This will trigger the earlier rule about diplomacy.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The statement that Hayden is either not ambitious or precise.</text>
<logic>~ambitious(hayden) v precise(hayden)</logic>
</step>

The fact establishes Hayden's wisdom, which is the foundation for the first inference. Knowing he is wise lets us use the wisdom‑to‑ambition rule immediately.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Hayden is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that Hayden is wise with the rule that wisdom entails ambition, we can confidently assert that Hayden is ambitious. This new fact will clash with the disjunction, forcing the other alternative.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Hayden's wisdom and the rule linking wisdom to ambition, we infer that Hayden is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(hayden)</logic>
</step>

We now have ambitious(Hayden) together with the statement that either not ambitious or precise must be true. Since ambitious(Hayden) contradicts the "not ambitious" side, the only remaining way for the disjunction to be satisfied is for precise(Hayden) to hold. Hence we deduce that Hayden is precise.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Using Hayden's ambition together with the disjunction that he is either not ambitious or precise, we conclude that he must be precise.</text>
<logic>precise(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Having established precise(Hayden), we look back at the rule linking diplomacy to non‑precision. That rule says diplomatic(Hayden) would make Hayden not precise, which is incompatible with what we just derived. Therefore, the assumption of diplomatic(Hayden) must be false, and we conclude ~diplomatic(Hayden).
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Because precise Hayden contradicts the rule that diplomatic implies not precise, we deduce that Hayden cannot be diplomatic.</text>
<logic>~diplomatic(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Hayden is not diplomatic, the second rule becomes applicable: non‑diplomatic potters are industrious. By modus ponens on ~diplomatic(Hayden) and the rule, we infer industrious(Hayden).
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since Hayden is not diplomatic and the rule states that non‑diplomatic potters are industrious, we derive that Hayden is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hayden is industrious, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Max is an unbiased forensic analyst. In this investigation, any analyst who follows the standard procedure is considered courteous. </step>

Similarly, the second default rule says that a courteous analyst is deemed certified unless there is evidence of abnormal unqualification. This will allow us to move from Max being courteous to Max being certified, provided no abnormality is known.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>By default, a courteous analyst is certified unless there is evidence that they are abnormally unqualified.</text>
<logic>certified(max) :- courteous(max), not ab_certified(max)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Max is unbiased gives us the starting point for the default reasoning. It directly satisfies the condition of the first default rule, enabling us to infer further properties about Max.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Max is unbiased.</text>
<logic>unbiased(max)</logic>
</step>

Applying the first default rule to Max's unbiased status, and noting that we have no abnormality information, we can conclude that Max follows the standard procedure. This step bridges the factual premise to the procedural property needed for the next inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From Max being unbiased and the default rule, we infer that Max follows the standard procedure.</text>
<logic>standard_procedure(max)</logic>
</step>

With Max now known to follow the standard procedure, we invoke the rule that standard procedure leads to courteous behavior. Consequently, Max is courteous, which sets up the condition required for the certification default.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since Max follows the standard procedure, the rule that standard procedure implies courteous gives us that Max is courteous.</text>
<logic>courteous(max)</logic>
</step>

Given that Max is courteous and we lack any abnormal unqualification evidence, the second default rule allows us to infer that Max is certified. This step completes the chain from the original fact to the desired conclusion.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">

<logic>certified(max)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Max is certified, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: The blueprint is praised for its aesthetic quality. An aesthetic blueprint is reinforced, meaning that any blueprint that is aesthetic becomes reinforced. Being reinforced ensures the blueprint is ventilated, so every reinforced blueprint is also ventilated. A ventilated blueprint is structural, therefore any blueprint that is ventilated becomes structural. If a blueprint is foundational, then it is load‑bearing; likewise, any load‑bearing blueprint is considered sustainable. A symmetrical blueprint cannot be structural, which captures the rule that if a blueprint is symmetrical, it is not structural. The blueprint is either symmetrical or foundational. All of these statements together capture the entire set of facts, conditionals, and the exclusive alternative given for the blueprint.
examples.question: Can we conclude that the blueprint is not sustainable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the blueprint fails to be sustainable. The goal is to see if the premises force the blueprint to be sustainable, which would make the negation false.

The first rule tells us that a foundational blueprint automatically becomes load‑bearing. This connection will be useful later when we establish the blueprint's foundational status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the blueprint is foundational, then it is load‑bearing.</text>
<logic>load_bearing(blueprint) :- foundational(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links ventilation to structural integrity, so any ventilated blueprint will also be structural. This will allow us to move from ventilation to structural later on.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Whenever the blueprint is ventilated, it is also structural.</text>
<logic>structural(blueprint) :- ventilated(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that reinforcement guarantees ventilation. Once we have reinforcement, we can immediately claim ventilation.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being reinforced guarantees that the blueprint is ventilated.</text>
<logic>ventilated(blueprint) :- reinforced(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule provides the bridge from aesthetic quality to reinforcement. Since we know the blueprint is aesthetic, this rule will start the chain of deductions.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>An aesthetic blueprint is automatically reinforced.</text>
<logic>reinforced(blueprint) :- aesthetic(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule connects load‑bearing status to sustainability. If we can prove the blueprint is load‑bearing, we will have a direct path to sustainability.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the blueprint is load‑bearing, then it is sustainable.</text>
<logic>sustainable(blueprint) :- load_bearing(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule states that symmetry prevents structural status. This negative relationship will later help us eliminate the symmetry alternative.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>A symmetrical blueprint cannot be structural.</text>
<logic>~structural(blueprint) :- symmetrical(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise gives a disjunction: the blueprint must be either symmetrical or foundational. This exclusive choice will be resolved once we rule out one option.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The blueprint is either symmetrical or foundational.</text>
<logic>symmetrical(blueprint) v foundational(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The eighth premise is a concrete fact: the blueprint is aesthetic. This fact is the starting point for all forward reasoning.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The blueprint possesses aesthetic quality.</text>
<logic>aesthetic(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

From the aesthetic fact and the rule that aesthetic implies reinforcement, we can assert that the blueprint is reinforced. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>From the aesthetic fact and the rule that aesthetic implies reinforced, we infer that the blueprint is reinforced.</text>
<logic>reinforced(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Having established reinforcement, we invoke the rule that reinforcement leads to ventilation, again via modus ponens, to conclude the blueprint is ventilated.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Using the reinforced status and the rule that reinforced leads to ventilated, we derive that the blueprint is ventilated.</text>
<logic>ventilated(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

With ventilation established, the rule linking ventilation to structural allows us to deduce that the blueprint is structural, again using modus ponens.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Given that the blueprint is ventilated, the rule that ventilated implies structural lets us conclude the blueprint is structural.</text>
<logic>structural(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Now we have a structural blueprint. The rule that a symmetrical blueprint cannot be structural tells us that if the blueprint were symmetrical, it would contradict its structural nature. Therefore, the blueprint must not be symmetrical, which we obtain by modus tollens.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Because the blueprint is structural and the rule that symmetrical prevents structural, we deduce that the blueprint cannot be symmetrical.</text>
<logic>~symmetrical(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Since the blueprint cannot be symmetrical and we know from the disjunction that it must be either symmetrical or foundational, the only remaining possibility is that it is foundational. This follows by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Having shown the blueprint is not symmetrical and knowing it must be either symmetrical or foundational, we infer that it is foundational.</text>
<logic>foundational(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

From the newly derived foundational status, we apply the rule that foundational implies load‑bearing, giving us a load‑bearing blueprint via modus ponens.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,1">
<text>Since the blueprint is foundational, the rule linking foundational to load‑bearing yields that the blueprint is load‑bearing.</text>
<logic>load_bearing(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule that load‑bearing leads to sustainability lets us infer that the blueprint is sustainable. This is another direct application of modus ponens.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>From the load‑bearing condition and the implication that load‑bearing leads to sustainability, we obtain that the blueprint is sustainable.</text>
<logic>sustainable(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

We derived Blueprint is sustainable, so the conclusion Blueprint is not sustainable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the garden, ash is known to be proficient. Whenever ash is active, ash becomes strategic. Conversely, if ash is resourceful, then ash is not strategic. Moreover, at least one of the following holds: ash is not proficient or ash is active.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that ash is not resourceful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether ash lacks the property of being resourceful. The goal is to derive ~resourceful(ash) using the supplied facts, rules, and the disjunction.

This premise connects activity to strategy: if we ever establish that ash is active, we will be able to infer that ash is strategic. It will become useful later when we obtain the activity fact.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if ash is active, then ash is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(ash) :- active(ash)</logic>
</step>

This premise tells us that being resourceful forces ash to be not strategic. It creates a contrapositive relationship that will allow us to rule out resourcefulness once we know ash is strategic.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if ash is resourceful, then ash is not strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(ash) :- resourceful(ash)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities: either ash is not proficient or ash is active. Since we already have information about ash's proficiency, this statement will let us eliminate one option and secure the other.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>We are told that either ash is not proficient or ash is active.</text>
<logic>~proficient(ash) v active(ash)</logic>
</step>

The fact that ash is proficient directly contradicts the first part of the disjunction (~proficient). By negating that alternative, we are forced to accept the second alternative, namely that ash is active.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that ash is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(ash)</logic>
</step>

Combining the disjunction with the fact that ash is proficient, we see that the "ash is not proficient" side cannot hold. Therefore, the only way the disjunction remains true is for ash to be active, giving us active(ash).
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>From the disjunction and the fact that ash is proficient, we infer that ash must be active.</text>
<logic>active(ash)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have active(ash), we can apply the first rule (PREMISE_1). The rule says any active ash must also be strategic, so we derive strategic(ash).
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since ash is active, the first rule lets us conclude that ash is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(ash)</logic>
</step>

We have strategic(ash) and the second rule (PREMISE_2) which says that if ash were resourceful, ash would be not strategic. Since ash is already strategic, the antecedent of that rule cannot be true; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Hence ash cannot be resourceful, giving us ~resourceful(ash).
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Because ash is strategic and the second rule says a resourceful ash would be not strategic, we deduce that ash cannot be resourceful.</text>
<logic>~resourceful(ash)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Ash is not resourceful, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Alpha_bot has a knowledge base. Whenever a robot is in low power mode, it is also high precision. If a robot has a knowledge base, then it operates in an energy‑saving manner. Any robot that is user‑friendly functions as a heavy lifter. Being a heavy lifter guarantees that the robot switches to low power mode. Whenever a robot is energy‑saving, it enters learning mode. If a robot is in learning mode, then it is secure. If a robot performs self‑diagnostics, then it is not secure. Either alpha_bot performs self‑diagnostics or it is user‑friendly.
examples.question: Is alpha_bot safety compliant?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether alpha_bot satisfies the safety_compliant property. To answer, we must see if any chain of the given facts and conditional rules can produce that property.

I need to determine whether Alpha_bot is safety compliant can be derived from the given premises.

The first rule tells us that low_power_mode leads to high_precision. This is relevant because several later rules use low_power_mode as a premise, so establishing it could start a cascade toward other attributes.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the robot is in low power mode, then it is high precision.</text>
<logic>high_precision(alpha_bot) :- low_power_mode(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links the knowledge_base fact to the energy_saving attribute. Since we already know alpha_bot has a knowledge base, this rule will allow us to derive that it is energy_saving, which is needed for later steps.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the robot has a knowledge base, then it operates in an energy‑saving manner.</text>
<logic>energy_saving(alpha_bot) :- knowledge_base(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that being user_friendly implies being a heavy_lifter. Although we do not yet know whether alpha_bot is user_friendly, the disjunction in the context gives us a possible path: either self_diagnostic or user_friendly holds, so this rule could become useful.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the robot is user‑friendly, then it functions as a heavy lifter.</text>
<logic>heavy_lifter(alpha_bot) :- user_friendly(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

According to the fourth rule, if a robot is a heavy_lifter it will be in low_power_mode. This creates a bridge from the user_friendly/heavy_lifter line to the low_power_mode line, potentially activating the first rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the robot is a heavy lifter, then it switches to low power mode.</text>
<logic>low_power_mode(alpha_bot) :- heavy_lifter(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule says that an energy_saving robot becomes learning_mode. Once we have derived energy_saving from the knowledge_base, we can obtain learning_mode, which is a stepping stone toward the secure attribute.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the robot is energy‑saving, then it enters learning mode.</text>
<logic>learning_mode(alpha_bot) :- energy_saving(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule connects learning_mode to the secure property. If we manage to get learning_mode, this rule will give us that alpha_bot is secure, an important predicate for evaluating trustworthiness.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If the robot is in learning mode, then it is secure.</text>
<logic>secure(alpha_bot) :- learning_mode(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

The seventh rule provides a contrary effect: if the robot performs self_diagnostic, then it is not secure. Because the context offers a choice between self_diagnostic and user_friendly, this rule creates a conflict that must be considered when assessing whether alpha_bot can be deemed secure.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If the robot performs self‑diagnostics, then it is not secure.</text>
<logic>~secure(alpha_bot) :- self_diagnostic(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

The fact that alpha_bot possesses a knowledge base is the starting point for the chain that yields energy_saving, then learning_mode, and eventually secure. It anchors the forward reasoning process.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The robot possesses a knowledge base.</text>
<logic>knowledge_base(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Alpha_bot is safety compliant: no rule or disjunction has "safety_compliant" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(safety_compliant(alpha_bot))</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Alpha_bot is not safety compliant.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~safety_compliant(alpha_bot))</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="derived">
<text>Neither Alpha_bot is safety compliant nor Alpha_bot is not safety compliant can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(safety_compliant(alpha_bot))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: The star Cassiopeia_star is luminescent. Whenever a star is aged, it is radiant. Whenever a star is stable, it is gravitationally_bound. Whenever a star is massive, it is aged. Whenever a star is luminescent, it is massive. If a star is consistent, then it is not radiant. Finally, Cassiopeia_star is either consistent or gravitationally_bound.
examples.question: Is Cassiopeia_star not stable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Cassiopeia_star fails to be stable. To answer this we must trace the chain of properties starting from the known fact that the star is luminescent and use the conditional rules provided. If we can derive that the star is indeed stable, then the negation will be false.

The rule that every aged star is radiant will later allow us to turn an age property into a radiant one, which is needed to test the consistency condition. It sets up a forward link from age to radiance.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any aged star is radiant.</text>
<logic>radiant(cassiopeia_star) :- aged(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

The rule linking stability to being gravitationally_bound gives us a way to infer stability once we have established the gravitationally_bound status. This is the crucial bridge to the query.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Any stable star is gravitationally_bound.</text>
<logic>stable(cassiopeia_star) :- gravitationally_bound(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

Stating that every massive star is aged lets us move from a mass property to age, and subsequently to radiance via PREMISE_1. It connects the later derived massive fact to the chain of reasoning.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Any massive star is aged.</text>
<logic>aged(cassiopeia_star) :- massive(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that any luminescent star is massive is the starting point for the forward chain. Since we know the star is luminescent, this rule will let us claim it is massive.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Any luminescent star is massive.</text>
<logic>massive(cassiopeia_star) :- luminescent(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

The rule that a consistent star would be non‑radiant creates a contradiction if we ever find the star both consistent and radiant. This will help us eliminate the consistent alternative later on.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If a star is consistent then it is not radiant.</text>
<logic>~radiant(cassiopeia_star) :- consistent(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction stating the star is either consistent or gravitationally_bound gives us a choice that will be resolved once we rule out one of the options. It is essential for deriving the gravitationally_bound status.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Cassiopeia_star is either consistent or gravitationally_bound.</text>
<logic>consistent(cassiopeia_star) v gravitationally_bound(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

We are given directly that Cassiopeia_star is luminescent, which activates PREMISE_4 and starts the cascade of deductions leading to stability.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Cassiopeia_star is luminescent.</text>
<logic>luminescent(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens to the luminescent fact together with the rule that luminescent implies massive, we can firmly assert that Cassiopeia_star is massive. This new fact is the foundation for the next step.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>From the luminescent property we infer that Cassiopeia_star is massive.</text>
<logic>massive(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

With the massive property established, we use the rule that massive entails aged to infer that the star is aged. This moves us one rung higher in the implication ladder toward radiance.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since the star is massive, we can conclude that it is aged.</text>
<logic>aged(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

Now that the star is aged, PREMISE_1 tells us that an aged star is radiant, so we conclude that Cassiopeia_star is radiant. This fact will later clash with the consistency rule.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Because the star is aged, it follows that Cassiopeia_star is radiant.</text>
<logic>radiant(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

We have both that the star is radiant and that a consistent star would have to be non‑radiant (PREMISE_5). Since the star is radiant, it cannot satisfy the antecedent of that rule, allowing us to infer that the star is not consistent via modus tollens.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Knowing that Cassiopeia_star is radiant and that a consistent star would be non‑radiant, we infer that Cassiopeia_star cannot be consistent.</text>
<logic>~consistent(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

Having shown the star is not consistent, the disjunction from PREMISE_6 forces the other alternative to be true. Therefore, Cassiopeia_star must be gravitationally_bound.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Given that the star is not consistent and that it must be either consistent or gravitationally_bound, we deduce that it is gravitationally_bound.</text>
<logic>gravitationally_bound(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using PREMISE_2, a gravitationally_bound star is stable, so we deduce that Cassiopeia_star is stable. This directly contradicts the queried negation.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>From the gravitationally_bound status we infer that Cassiopeia_star is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(cassiopeia_star)</logic>
</step>

We derived Cassiopeia_star is stable, so the conclusion Cassiopeia_star is not stable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: River Elliot has a high albedo. Whenever River Elliot is not temperature‑sensitive, it is a balanced ecosystem. If River Elliot has a high albedo, then it is nutrient‑rich. If River Elliot is temperature‑sensitive, then it is not clear water. Either River Elliot is not nutrient‑rich or it has clear water.
examples.question: Does River Elliot have a balanced ecosystem?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether River Elliot enjoys a balanced ecosystem. To answer this, we must use the environmental facts and conditional relationships provided about its albedo, nutrient status, water clarity, and temperature sensitivity.

The first rule connects the absence of temperature sensitivity to a balanced ecosystem. This conditional will be crucial once we can establish that River Elliot is not temperature‑sensitive.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if River Elliot is not temperature‑sensitive then it has a balanced ecosystem.</text>
<logic>balanced_ecosystem(river_elliot) :- ~temperature_sensitive(river_elliot)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that high albedo guarantees nutrient richness. Since we already know River Elliot has high albedo, this rule will let us derive a new property about the river.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if River Elliot has a high albedo then it is nutrient‑rich.</text>
<logic>nutrient_rich(river_elliot) :- high_albedo(river_elliot)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that if River Elliot were temperature‑sensitive, it would not have clear water. This provides a way to infer the negation of temperature sensitivity from information about water clarity later on.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if River Elliot is temperature‑sensitive then it is not clear water.</text>
<logic>~clear_water(river_elliot) :- temperature_sensitive(river_elliot)</logic>
</step>

The disjunctive statement offers two alternatives: either River Elliot lacks nutrient richness or it possesses clear water. This will let us eliminate one option once we learn something about its nutrient status.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either River Elliot is not nutrient‑rich or it has clear water.</text>
<logic>~nutrient_rich(river_elliot) v clear_water(river_elliot)</logic>
</step>

The fact that River Elliot has a high albedo is the starting point for our chain of reasoning. It directly activates the rule that links albedo to nutrient richness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>River Elliot has a high albedo.</text>
<logic>high_albedo(river_elliot)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from Premise 2 to the fact in Premise 5, we conclude that River Elliot is nutrient‑rich. The high albedo condition is satisfied, so the consequent—nutrient richness—must hold.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From River Elliot having a high albedo and the rule linking high albedo to nutrient richness, we infer that River Elliot is nutrient‑rich.</text>
<logic>nutrient_rich(river_elliot)</logic>
</step>

Now we have River Elliot being nutrient‑rich, which makes the left side of the disjunction in Premise 4 false. Since a disjunction is true when at least one part is true, the only way for the statement to remain true is for the right side—clear water—to be true. Hence, River Elliot has clear water.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Since River Elliot is nutrient‑rich and we know that either River Elliot is not nutrient‑rich or it has clear water, we can conclude that River Elliot has clear water.</text>
<logic>clear_water(river_elliot)</logic>
</step>

We have just established that River Elliot has clear water. Premise 3 tells us that if it were temperature‑sensitive, it would be not clear water. Because we observe clear water, the antecedent (temperature sensitivity) cannot hold; therefore River Elliot is not temperature‑sensitive.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Given that River Elliot has clear water and the rule that temperature‑sensitivity would make it not clear water, we deduce that River Elliot cannot be temperature‑sensitive.</text>
<logic>~temperature_sensitive(river_elliot)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that River Elliot is not temperature‑sensitive, we can finally use the first rule from Premise 1. That rule says a lack of temperature sensitivity guarantees a balanced ecosystem, so we infer that River Elliot indeed has a balanced ecosystem.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Because River Elliot is not temperature‑sensitive and the rule that lack of temperature‑sensitivity leads to a balanced ecosystem, we infer that River Elliot has a balanced ecosystem.</text>
<logic>balanced_ecosystem(river_elliot)</logic>
</step>

Therefore River_elliot is balanced ecosystem, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the ancient chronicles, Mount Everest is described as wise. The texts also state that any being that is just is automatically robust. Conversely, the records claim that whenever something is valiant, it cannot be robust. Moreover, the scrolls present a dilemma: either Mount Everest is not wise, or it is just. These are the only pieces of information we have about the mountain.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Mount Everest is not valiant?
examples.completion:
<think>
We start with a collection of ancient statements about Mount Everest, including a fact about its wisdom and several conditional claims. Our goal is to determine whether the mountain can be described as not valiant.

The rule linking just to robust is relevant because it will let us move from a property we may later derive (just) to another (robust) that participates in a later inference. Keeping this conditional in mind prepares us for a forward chain of reasoning.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The first rule tells us that being just guarantees being robust.</text>
<logic>robust(mount_everest) :- just(mount_everest)</logic>
</step>

The second conditional connects valiant with the negation of robust, which is crucial for a contrapositive argument later. If we can show the mountain is robust, this rule will let us deny its valiance.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The second rule says that if something is valiant, then it must not be robust.</text>
<logic>~robust(mount_everest) :- valiant(mount_everest)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possible ways to describe the mountain: either it lacks wisdom or it possesses justice. Since we already have a fact about wisdom, this premise will help us eliminate one option and secure the other.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The third premise offers a disjunction: either Mount Everest is not wise, or it is just.</text>
<logic>~wise(mount_everest) v just(mount_everest)</logic>
</step>

The explicit statement that Mount Everest is wise supplies the concrete fact needed to resolve the disjunction. Knowing the mountain is wise means the first disjunct (~wise) is false, forcing the second disjunct to be true.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>We are explicitly told that Mount Everest is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(mount_everest)</logic>
</step>

Applying disjunctive syllogism, we combine the fact that Mount Everest is wise with the disjunction "not wise or just". Because the "not wise" part cannot hold, the only remaining possibility is that the mountain is just. This gives us the property we need for the next step.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because we know Mount Everest is wise, the disjunction forces the alternative that it is just.</text>
<logic>just(mount_everest)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Mount Everest is just, we can invoke the first rule: any just entity is robust. By modus ponens, the justification that the mountain is just directly yields that it is robust.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Having established that Mount Everest is just, the first rule lets us infer that it is robust.</text>
<logic>robust(mount_everest)</logic>
</step>

With the robustness of Mount Everest in hand, we turn to the second rule, which says a valiant thing cannot be robust. Using modus tollens, the presence of robustness forces the negation of valiance, so we conclude the mountain is not valiant.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since Mount Everest is robust, the second rule's contrapositive tells us that it cannot be valiant.</text>
<logic>~valiant(mount_everest)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Mount_everest is not valiant, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Detective Jones is a quick thinker. Whenever Detective Jones is a sharp observer, he is also detail‑oriented. Detective Jones is trustworthy whenever he is not methodical. If Detective Jones is a quick thinker, then he is accurate. Being methodical guarantees that Detective Jones is not detail‑oriented. Either Detective Jones is not accurate, or he is a sharp observer.
examples.question: Is it the case that Detective Jones is not trustworthy?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Detective Jones lacks trustworthiness. To answer, we will chain together the given facts and conditionals until we can see if the property ‘trustworthy’ holds or not.

This premise tells us how the property of being a sharp observer leads to being detail‑oriented. It will become useful once we know whether Detective Jones is a sharp observer.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Detective Jones were a sharp observer, then he would be detail‑oriented.</text>
<logic>detail_oriented(detective_jones) :- sharp_observer(detective_jones)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that the absence of the methodical trait guarantees trustworthiness. It will allow us to conclude about trustworthiness once we can show that Detective Jones is not methodical.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Detective Jones becomes trustworthy whenever he is not methodical.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(detective_jones) :- ~methodical(detective_jones)</logic>
</step>

This rule links quick thinking to accuracy. Since we already have a fact that Detective Jones is a quick thinker, it will let us derive his accuracy.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being a quick thinker ensures that Detective Jones is accurate.</text>
<logic>accurate(detective_jones) :- quick_thinker(detective_jones)</logic>
</step>

The conditional states that being methodical forces a lack of detail‑orientation. It will be needed later to turn a positive detail‑orientation into a negation of the methodical trait.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Detective Jones is methodical, then he is not detail‑oriented.</text>
<logic>~detail_oriented(detective_jones) :- methodical(detective_jones)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction presents two alternatives: either Detective Jones is not accurate, or he is a sharp observer. Once we settle the truth of one side, we can infer the other.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Detective Jones is not accurate, or he is a sharp observer.</text>
<logic>~accurate(detective_jones) v sharp_observer(detective_jones)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that Detective Jones is a quick thinker is the starting point for several of our deductions, especially for gaining accuracy.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Detective Jones is a quick thinker.</text>
<logic>quick_thinker(detective_jones)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Detective Jones is a quick thinker together with the rule that quick thinkers are accurate, we apply modus ponens to obtain his accuracy. This gives us a concrete truth value for the predicate ‘accurate’.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>From the fact that Detective Jones is a quick thinker and the rule that quick thinkers are accurate, we infer that he is accurate.</text>
<logic>accurate(detective_jones)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Detective Jones is accurate, the disjunction “not accurate or sharp observer” cannot be satisfied by the first option, so the second option must hold. By disjunctive syllogism we therefore conclude that he is a sharp observer.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Since we now know Detective Jones is accurate, the disjunction “not accurate or sharp observer” forces the other option, so we conclude that he is a sharp observer.</text>
<logic>sharp_observer(detective_jones)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Detective Jones is a sharp observer, we can invoke the conditional linking sharp observers to detail‑orientation. Modus ponens yields that he is detail‑oriented.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Because he is a sharp observer, the conditional linking sharp observers to detail‑orientation lets us deduce that Detective Jones is detail‑oriented.</text>
<logic>detail_oriented(detective_jones)</logic>
</step>

The rule that methodical people are not detail‑oriented gives us the implication ‘methodical → not detail‑oriented’. Since we have the opposite, that Detective Jones is detail‑oriented, the contrapositive (detail‑oriented → not methodical) lets us deduce that he is not methodical.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>The rule stating that methodical people are not detail‑oriented, combined with the fact that Detective Jones is detail‑oriented, lets us infer that he cannot be methodical.</text>
<logic>~methodical(detective_jones)</logic>
</step>

With the conclusion that Detective Jones is not methodical, we can now apply the rule that non‑methodical individuals are trustworthy. Modus ponens provides the final derived fact that he is trustworthy.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">

examples.context: In the forensic laboratory, the analyst Joy is described as open_minded. Any analyst who is productive is also ambitious. Whenever an analyst is ambitious, she becomes articulate. examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Joy is not prepared?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Joy is not prepared, given a collection of forensic‑team traits and logical dependencies. The goal is to follow the chain of implications from the known fact that Joy is open_minded and see what it tells us about her preparedness.

</step>

</step>

<logic>empathetic(joy) :- open_minded(joy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Joy is articulate, then she is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(joy) :- articulate(joy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Joy is resilient, then she is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(joy) :- resilient(joy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Joy is not empathetic or Joy is resilient.</text>
<logic>~empathetic(joy) v resilient(joy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Joy is open_minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(joy)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Joy is open_minded is directly supplied, and the rule linking open_minded to empathetic tells us that any open_minded analyst must also be empathetic. Applying this conditional to Joy lets us conclude she is empathetic, establishing the first link in the chain.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Because Joy is open_minded and open_minded implies empathetic, we infer that Joy is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(joy)</logic>
</step>

We now have Joy as empathetic, but the disjunction states that either Joy is not empathetic or she is resilient. Since the former alternative is contradicted by our derived empathetic fact, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Joy must be resilient. This uses disjunctive syllogism to obtain resilience.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>Given Joy is empathetic and the statement that either she is not empathetic or she is resilient, we deduce that Joy must be resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(joy)</logic>
</step>

Having established Joy's resilience, we invoke the rule that resilience implies productivity. The conditional guarantees that any resilient individual becomes productive, so Joy is now known to be productive.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Since Joy is resilient and resilience guarantees productivity, Joy is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(joy)</logic>
</step>

The productivity-to-ambition rule says that every productive analyst is ambitious. Because Joy is productive, we can immediately infer that she is ambitious, moving us further toward the preparation property.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Productivity leads to ambition, so Joy is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(joy)</logic>
</step>

Ambition, in turn, triggers articulateness according to the second rule. Since Joy is ambitious, the rule allows us to deduce that she is articulate.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Ambition leads to articulateness, therefore Joy is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(joy)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule linking articulateness to being prepared tells us that any articulate analyst is prepared for case work. Joy's articulateness therefore yields the conclusion that she is prepared.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Articulateness leads to being prepared, so Joy is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(joy)</logic>

We derived Joy is prepared, so the conclusion Joy is not prepared is False.

examples.context: Drew is astute. Whenever Drew is vigorous, he is also sharp. If Drew is astute, then Drew is vigorous. If Drew is focused, then Drew is wise. If Drew is friendly, then Drew is not wise. Either Drew is not sharp, or he is focused.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Drew is not friendly?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Drew is not friendly based on the information given. The goal is to chain the available facts and rules to reach a conclusion about Drew's friendliness.

The first premise tells us how sharpness follows from vigor for Drew. Keeping this in mind will let us later turn a statement about vigor into a statement about sharpness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Drew is vigorous then he is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(drew) :- vigorous(drew)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links astuteness to vigor, which is useful because we already know Drew is astute. This connection will let us move from the known fact to vigor.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Drew is astute then he is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(drew) :- astute(drew)</logic>
</step>

The third premise explains that focus leads to wisdom. It will become relevant once we have established that Drew is focused, allowing us to infer his wisdom.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Drew is focused then he is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(drew) :- focused(drew)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise says that if Drew were friendly, he would necessarily be not wise. This contrapositive relationship will be the key to showing that Drew cannot be friendly once we have proven his wisdom.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Drew is friendly then he is not wise.</text>
<logic>~wise(drew) :- friendly(drew)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise is a disjunction: either Drew is not sharp or he is focused. This provides an either‑or bridge that we can exploit once we know something about his sharpness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The statement that either Drew is not sharp or he is focused.</text>
<logic>~sharp(drew) v focused(drew)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise gives us a concrete fact: Drew is astute. This is the starting point for our forward reasoning chain.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Drew is astute.</text>
<logic>astute(drew)</logic>
</step>

Because Drew is astute and we have a rule that astuteness guarantees vigor, we can deduce that Drew is vigorous. This step converts the known fact into a new property that will feed into the next rule.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Drew being astute and the rule linking astuteness to vigor, we infer that Drew is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(drew)</logic>
</step>

With Drew now known to be vigorous, the rule that vigor implies sharpness allows us to conclude that Drew is sharp. We have moved from vigor to sharpness, setting up the conditions needed for the disjunction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Using Drew's newly derived vigor and the rule that vigor implies sharpness, we infer that Drew is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(drew)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that either Drew is not sharp or he is focused. Since we have just established that Drew is sharp, the first option is false, leaving us with the only viable alternative: Drew must be focused. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Given that Drew is sharp and the disjunction that either he is not sharp or he is focused, we deduce that Drew must be focused.</text>
<logic>focused(drew)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Drew is focused, we can apply the rule that focus leads to wisdom, thereby concluding that Drew is wise. This bridges the gap between the earlier focus conclusion and the later friendliness rule.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Since Drew is focused and focus leads to wisdom, we conclude that Drew is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(drew)</logic>
</step>

We now know Drew is wise, but the rule about friendliness says that if Drew were friendly, he would be not wise. Since he is wise, the antecedent of that rule (being friendly) cannot hold; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Thus, we infer that Drew is not friendly.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Because Drew is wise and the rule that friendliness would make him not wise, we infer that Drew cannot be friendly.</text>
<logic>~friendly(drew)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Drew is not friendly, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

Similarly, Quinn being relaxed is a stand‑alone fact. It does not play a role in the chain concerning Sasha, yet it satisfies the requirement to list all given facts.
<step n="6" type="premise">

Sasha is explicitly stated to be relaxed. This is the starting point for applying the rule that a relaxed person is talented.
<step n="7" type="premise">

<logic>relaxed(sasha)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Sasha is relaxed together with the rule that relaxed implies talented, we can conclude Sasha is talented. This is a straightforward application of the conditional premise.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">

<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">

We possess Sasha witty, and the disjunction says either not witty or logical. Because Sasha is indeed witty, the "not witty" side cannot hold, so the only way the disjunction remains true is that Sasha is logical. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">

examples.question: Is Rhea precise?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Rhea possesses the property of being precise. To answer this, we will follow the chain of given facts and conditional rules until we can either confirm or refute Rhea's precision.

The rule linking perceptiveness to motivation tells us that any entity that is perceptive must also be motivated. This will be useful later when we establish Rhea's perceptiveness, allowing us to derive her motivation status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Rhea is perceptive, then Rhea is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(rhea) :- perceptive(rhea)</logic>
</step>

The humility‑perceptive rule asserts that humility guarantees perceptiveness. Since we already know Rhea is humble, this rule provides the bridge we need to move from a known fact to a new property.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If someone is humble, then they are perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(rhea) :- humble(rhea)</logic>
</step>

The precision‑negated‑motivation rule says that if someone were precise, they could not be motivated. This conditional will let us turn a motivation fact into information about precision by contraposition.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If someone is precise, then they are not motivated.</text>
<logic>~motivated(rhea) :- precise(rhea)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Tate is principled is recorded for completeness; although it does not directly involve Rhea, it appears in the overall knowledge base and supports other rules about Tate.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Tate is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(tate)</logic>
</step>

Rhea's humility is an explicit fact. It is the starting point for applying the humility‑to‑perceptive rule, which begins our inference chain toward her motivation.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Rhea is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(rhea)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Rhea is humble together with the rule that humility implies perceptiveness, we can directly infer that Rhea is perceptive. This step converts a known attribute into another that will be needed for the next inference.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From Rhea's humility and the rule that humility implies perceptiveness, we infer that Rhea is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(rhea)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Rhea's perceptiveness, the rule stating that perceptiveness leads to motivation lets us conclude that Rhea is motivated. This links the earlier derived property to a new one, setting up the condition needed for the final contradiction.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Since Rhea is perceptive and perceptiveness leads to motivation, we conclude that Rhea is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(rhea)</logic>
</step>

We have Rhea motivated, but the rule about precision says that being precise would make someone not motivated. Since Rhea is indeed motivated, the only way to satisfy the rule is to assert that Rhea cannot be precise. Thus we infer ¬precise(Rhea).
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
