</step>

We derived Rhea is not precise, so the conclusion Rhea is precise is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Bruce is known to be cheerful, and Hope is recognized as clever. Faith holds the principle of being principled. Whenever someone is polite, that person is capable; thus Faith being polite would make Faith capable. If a person is principled, then that person is motivated, so Faith’s principled nature guarantees Faith is motivated. The reverse also holds: any motivated individual is automatically polite, giving Faith a two‑way link between politeness and motivation. Rationality follows from friendliness, meaning that any friendly individual is rational. A vigorous person cannot be capable, expressed as if Faith is vigorous then Faith is not capable. Moreover, either Faith is vigorous or Faith is friendly – at least one of these holds. In a separate matter, either Hope is not disciplined or Hope is trustworthy, and either Bruce is not intuitive or Bruce is dedicated. No other default rules are present.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Faith is not rational?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Faith can be shown to be not rational. To answer this, we must trace the chain of implications from the facts about Faith and the logical connections provided, ultimately checking if a rational conclusion about Faith can be reached. If we end up proving Faith is rational, the negation will be shown false.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Faith is polite, then Faith is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(faith) :- polite(faith)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Faith is principled, then Faith is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(faith) :- principled(faith)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Faith is motivated, then Faith is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(faith) :- motivated(faith)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Faith is friendly, then Faith is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(faith) :- friendly(faith)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Faith is vigorous, then Faith is not capable.</text>
<logic>~capable(faith) :- vigorous(faith)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Faith is vigorous or Faith is friendly.</text>
<logic>vigorous(faith) v friendly(faith)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Bruce is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(bruce)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Hope is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(hope)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Faith is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(faith)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that Faith is principled. The rule that a principled individual is motivated lets us move from that fact to the new conclusion that Faith is motivated. This step is straightforward application of the conditional relationship given in the premises.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>From Faith being principled and the rule that principled implies motivated, we infer that Faith is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(faith)</logic>
</step>

Having established Faith's motivated status, we can use the rule linking motivation back to politeness. Since motivated persons are automatically polite, Faith inherits politeness from the motivated property we just derived. This reinforces the two‑way connection between motivation and politeness.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Because Faith is now known to be motivated and motivated implies polite, we deduce that Faith is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(faith)</logic>
</step>

Now that Faith is polite, the rule stating that politeness entails capability applies. By invoking this rule, we can assert that Faith is capable. This brings the capable predicate into our reasoning, which will later interact with the vigorous‑related rule.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>With Faith being polite and the rule that polite implies capable, Faith is established as capable.</text>
<logic>capable(faith)</logic>
</step>

We know Faith is capable, and we also have a rule that says if Faith were vigorous, Faith would not be capable. Because we have already proven Faith is capable, the only way to keep both statements consistent is to reject the possibility that Faith is vigorous. This uses modus tollens to eliminate the vigorous alternative.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,5">
<text>Since Faith is capable and the rule says a vigorous Faith would make Faith not capable, we conclude Faith cannot be vigorous.</text>
<logic>~vigorous(faith)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that Faith must be either vigorous or friendly. Since we have just ruled out vigorous, the remaining viable option is that Faith is friendly. This step employs disjunctive syllogism to narrow down the choice to friendliness.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">
<text>Given that Faith is not vigorous and the disjunction that Faith is either vigorous or friendly, the only remaining option is that Faith is friendly.</text>
<logic>friendly(faith)</logic>
</step>

With Faith now identified as friendly, we can apply the rule that friendliness leads to rationality. Consequently, Faith must be rational. This final derivation gives us the positive rational status of Faith, directly contradicting the query's negation.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>Because Faith is friendly and friendliness leads to rationality, we conclude Faith is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(faith)</logic>
</step>

We derived Faith is rational, so the conclusion Faith is not rational is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Captain Kate is seaworthy, meaning she can easily adjust to changing seas. Firstmate Maria is quick‑witted, showing she has a sharp sense of humor. Whenever someone is sturdy, they are also a navigational guru, so any crew member who is sturdy becomes a navigational guru. Whenever someone is quick‑witted, they become race‑ready, so Maria being quick‑witted makes her race‑ready. If a sailor is not resourceful, then they are steadfast; thus any crew member who lacks resourcefulness is steadfast. Any sailor who is seaworthy is also enduring, so Captain Kate’s seaworthiness guarantees she is enduring. If a sailor is resourceful, they cannot be jolly; being resourceful leads to not being jolly. Either Firstmate Maria is not race‑ready or she is sturdy; this disjunction tells us at least one of those holds. Either Captain Kate is not enduring or she is jolly; this gives another alternative about Kate’s endurance and mood.
examples.question: Is Captain Kate steadfast?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Captain Kate possesses the quality of steadfastness. The goal is to trace a chain of logical implications from the facts and rules provided in the sailing scenario.

The first premise introduces a conditional link between lacking resourcefulness and being steadfast. This will be crucial later when we establish whether Kate is not resourceful.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is not resourceful, then they are steadfast.</text>
<logic>steadfast(captain_kate) :- ~resourceful(captain_kate)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that being seaworthy automatically makes a sailor enduring. Since we already know Kate is seaworthy, this rule will let us derive her endurance.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that seaworthiness guarantees endurance.</text>
<logic>enduring(captain_kate) :- seaworthy(captain_kate)</logic>
</step>

The third premise gives a relationship where resourcefulness forces a sailor not to be jolly. This relationship will be used in a contrapositive fashion once we know Kate’s mood.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that being resourceful guarantees not being jolly.</text>
<logic>~jolly(captain_kate) :- resourceful(captain_kate)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise provides a disjunction about Kate: either she is not enduring or she is jolly. This alternative will be resolved once we have a concrete fact about her endurance.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction claims that either Captain Kate is not enduring or she is jolly.</text>
<logic>~enduring(captain_kate) v jolly(captain_kate)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise simply records that Firstmate Maria is quick‑witted, a fact that is not directly needed for Kate’s proof but forms part of the overall knowledge base.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Firstmate Maria is quick‑witted.</text>
<logic>quick_witted(firstmate_maria)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise affirms that Captain Kate is seaworthy, giving us the starting point for applying the seaworthy‑to‑enduring rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Captain Kate is seaworthy.</text>
<logic>seaworthy(captain_kate)</logic>
</step>

By applying Modus Ponens to the fact that Kate is seaworthy and the rule that seaworthy implies enduring, we can confidently state that Kate is enduring. This new fact will interact with the disjunction in the next step.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Captain Kate’s seaworthiness and the rule linking seaworthy to enduring, we infer that she is enduring.</text>
<logic>enduring(captain_kate)</logic>
</step>

We now have the fact that Kate is enduring, which directly conflicts with the first part of the disjunction (~enduring). Since a disjunction is true when at least one part holds, the only way for it to be satisfied is for the second part, Kate being jolly, to be true. Hence we infer that Kate is jolly.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Using the derived fact that Captain Kate is enduring together with the disjunction that she is either not enduring or jolly, we conclude that she must be jolly.</text>
<logic>jolly(captain_kate)</logic>
</step>

With Kate established as jolly, we look back at the rule linking resourcefulness to not being jolly. If a sailor were resourceful, she could not be jolly; however, Kate is jolly, so by modus tollens we conclude that she cannot be resourceful. This gives us the negated resourcefulness needed for the final rule.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since Captain Kate is jolly and the rule says resourceful sailors are not jolly, we apply modus tollens to infer that she cannot be resourceful.</text>
<logic>~resourceful(captain_kate)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we combine the derived fact that Kate is not resourceful with the first rule that non‑resourceful sailors are steadfast. Modus Ponens lets us infer that Kate is steadfast, achieving the target conclusion.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Knowing that Captain Kate is not resourceful and using the rule that non‑resourceful sailors are steadfast, we deduce that she is steadfast.</text>
<logic>steadfast(captain_kate)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Captain_kate is steadfast, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

Premise 4 gives a negative calmness conclusion from sharpness. If we ever find Lance to be sharp, we can assert that he is not calm. examples.context: Ruth has been noted as experienced. Whenever Ruth is not sharp, she becomes creative. If Ruth is vigorous, then she is not creative. Should Ruth be sharp, she will not be thorough. Either Ruth is not experienced or she is relaxed. Either Ruth is not relaxed or she is thorough.
examples.question: Is it true that Ruth is not vigorous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Ruth can be vigorous. The goal is to show that Ruth is not vigorous by chaining the information given about her properties. To do this we will use the facts, conditional rules, and the two disjunctions provided.

This premise tells us how creativity arises for Ruth: if we ever manage to establish that she lacks sharpness, we can immediately infer her creativity. It will become useful later when we derive ¬sharp.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Ruth is not sharp, then she is creative.</text>
<logic>creative(ruth) :- ~sharp(ruth)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that vigor comes with a denial of creativity. Once we have proven Ruth is creative, this rule will let us flip the implication and claim that she cannot be vigorous.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Ruth is vigorous, then she is not creative.</text>
<logic>~creative(ruth) :- vigorous(ruth)</logic>
</step>

The third rule links sharpness to a lack of thoroughness. By later showing that Ruth is indeed thorough, we will be able to turn this rule around and obtain ¬sharp.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Ruth is sharp, then she is not thorough.</text>
<logic>~thorough(ruth) :- sharp(ruth)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction gives us a choice: either Ruth is not experienced or she is relaxed. Since we already know she is experienced, the only way for the statement to hold is that she must be relaxed. This sets up the next chain of reasoning.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The first disjunction states that either Ruth is not experienced or Ruth is relaxed.</text>
<logic>~experienced(ruth) v relaxed(ruth)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction offers another alternative: either Ruth is not relaxed or she is thorough. After we have concluded she is relaxed, the only way for this clause to stay true is that she must be thorough. This will later let us attack the sharpness rule.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The second disjunction states that either Ruth is not relaxed or Ruth is thorough.</text>
<logic>~relaxed(ruth) v thorough(ruth)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Ruth is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(ruth)</logic>
</step>

We combine the fact that Ruth is experienced with the disjunction "not experienced or relaxed". Because the left side of the disjunction is false, the right side must be true, giving us that Ruth is relaxed. This is a standard application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>From the fact that Ruth is experienced and the disjunction that either she is not experienced or relaxed, we conclude that Ruth must be relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(ruth)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Ruth is relaxed, we look at the second disjunction "not relaxed or thorough". The first part is false, so the second part must hold, leading us to conclude that Ruth is thorough. Again, this follows directly from disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Using Ruth’s relaxed status and the disjunction that either she is not relaxed or thorough, we infer that Ruth is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(ruth)</logic>
</step>

We have the rule "sharp → not thorough". Since we have established that Ruth is thorough, the contrapositive tells us that she cannot be sharp. Thus we derive ¬sharp, using modus tollens on the sharpness rule.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since sharpness would make Ruth not thorough, and we have established that she is thorough, we deduce that Ruth is not sharp.</text>
<logic>~sharp(ruth)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained ¬sharp, we can apply the rule from PREMISE_1 which says "¬sharp → creative". By modus ponens, Ruth must be creative. This step bridges the gap between the earlier sharpness conclusion and the creativity needed for the final argument.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Given that Ruth is not sharp and the rule linking not‑sharp to creative, we can assert that Ruth is creative.</text>
<logic>creative(ruth)</logic>
</step>

Finally we use the rule from PREMISE_2: "vigorous → not creative". We now know Ruth is creative, so the antecedent of that implication cannot hold; otherwise we would have a contradiction. By modus tollens we infer ¬vigorous, establishing the desired claim.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because being vigorous would force Ruth to be not creative, and we now know she is creative, we conclude that Ruth is not vigorous.</text>
<logic>~vigorous(ruth)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Ruth is not vigorous, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Steve is known to be witty. Whenever someone is witty, they are disciplined, so Steve being witty makes him disciplined. Any disciplined person is competitive, therefore Steve’s discipline guarantees his competitiveness. Whenever someone is competitive, they are ambitious, so Steve’s competitiveness implies ambition. If a person is resilient, then they are cautious; and any cautious individual is honest. Honesty, in turn, ensures consistency, so an honest Steve would be consistent. Moreover, if someone is brave, they cannot be ambitious; this gives a link between bravery and the negation of ambition. Finally, either Steve is brave or Steve is resilient, but we do not yet know which disjunct holds.
examples.question: Is Steve consistent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Steve possesses the property of consistency. The goal is to trace a chain of implications from the facts we know about Steve to the desired conclusion.

The rule that competitive Steve are ambitious is important because ambition appears later in a rule that blocks ambition when Steve is brave. Keeping this link in mind will help us later eliminate the brave alternative. It also connects competitiveness, which we will obtain from discipline, to ambition.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any competitive Steve is also ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(steve) :- competitive(steve)</logic>
</step>

Resilience implying caution is a crucial bridge: once we can show Steve is resilient, we will be able to move forward to honesty and then consistency. This rule sets up a pathway from a disjunctive option to the final goal.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Steve is resilient then he is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(steve) :- resilient(steve)</logic>
</step>

Discipline leading to competitiveness gives us a way to convert the initial fact that Steve is witty into a competitive status. Since ambition depends on competitiveness, this rule is a necessary step in the forward chain.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being disciplined makes Steve competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(steve) :- disciplined(steve)</logic>
</step>

The connection from honesty to consistency is the final link we need; once we have honesty we can directly assert consistency. This rule tells us that establishing honesty is sufficient for our target.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Honesty guarantees consistency for Steve.</text>
<logic>consistent(steve) :- honest(steve)</logic>
</step>

Witty Steve being disciplined is the starting point of the forward reasoning. It allows us to move from the given fact to a property that triggers subsequent rules. Without this, we would have no foothold to begin the derivations.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Witty Steve is automatically disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(steve) :- witty(steve)</logic>
</step>

Cautiousness guaranteeing honesty continues the chain after we obtain caution from resilience. This rule ensures that once we have cautious Steve, honesty—and therefore consistency—will follow.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Cautious Steve must be honest.</text>
<logic>honest(steve) :- cautious(steve)</logic>
</step>

The rule that a brave Steve cannot be ambitious will later be used in a contrapositive fashion. By showing Steve is ambitious, we will be able to infer that he cannot be brave, which will help resolve the initial disjunction.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If Steve is brave then he cannot be ambitious.</text>
<logic>~ambitious(steve) :- brave(steve)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possible ways to proceed: either Steve is brave or he is resilient. Determining which holds is essential because resilience enables the rest of the chain toward consistency.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Steve is brave or Steve is resilient.</text>
<logic>brave(steve) v resilient(steve)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Steve is witty is the concrete piece of information we start from. All subsequent forward steps rely on this fact, as it triggers discipline and cascades through the rules.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Steve is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(steve)</logic>
</step>

Since we know Steve is witty, the rule that wit implies discipline lets us conclude Steve is disciplined. This moves us from a base fact to a property that will later give us competitiveness. The inference follows directly from Modus Ponens.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>From Steve being witty we infer that Steve is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(steve)</logic>
</step>

Having established Steve's discipline, we apply the rule that discipline leads to competitiveness. This gives us a competitive Steve, which is needed for the ambition rule. The step is a straightforward application of the earlier premise.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Because Steve is disciplined, we conclude that Steve is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(steve)</logic>
</step>

With Steve now competitive, the rule linking competitiveness to ambition allows us to infer that Steve is ambitious. This is essential because ambition will later be used to eliminate the brave alternative in the disjunction.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>Steve's competitiveness lets us deduce that Steve is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(steve)</logic>
</step>

We now have Steve ambitious, and we also have the rule that a brave Steve would be not ambitious. By contraposition (or Modus Tollens), if Steve were brave, he would have to be not ambitious, which contradicts the fact that he is ambitious. Therefore Steve cannot be brave. This eliminates one side of the original disjunction.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Since Steve is ambitious, the rule linking bravery to non‑ambition tells us Steve cannot be brave.</text>
<logic>~brave(steve)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us Steve is either brave or resilient. Since we have just shown Steve cannot be brave, the only remaining possibility is that Steve is resilient. This disjunctive syllogism gives us resilience, unlocking the next segment of the chain.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>With Steve not brave and the original disjunction, we resolve that Steve must be resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(steve)</logic>
</step>

Resilience now triggers the rule that resilient entities are cautious. Hence Steve becomes cautious. This step is necessary because cautiousness is the gateway to honesty.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,2">
<text>Resilient Steve leads to Steve being cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(steve)</logic>
</step>

From cautious Steve, we invoke the rule that caution leads to honesty. Thus Steve is honest, positioning us to reach the final property of consistency. The inference follows directly via Modus Ponens.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,6">
<text>Cautious Steve then gives us that Steve is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(steve)</logic>
</step>

Finally, with Steve honest, the rule stating that honesty guarantees consistency lets us conclude Steve is consistent. This directly satisfies the query we set out to prove.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,4">
<text>Honest Steve finally yields that Steve is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(steve)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Steve is consistent, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

Either the carpenter is resourceful or the carpenter is plan based. Either the carpenter is not structurally sound or the carpenter is code compliant. examples.question: Is the carpenter methodical?

A compound that is catalytic is considered electrophilic. For acetone, either it is not reactive or it is catalytic.

examples.context: August is vigorous. Whenever August is athletic, then August is diplomatic. If August is observant, then August is responsible. If August is vigorous, then August is rested. If August is empathetic, then August is not rested. Either August is empathetic or August is observant. Either August is not responsible or August is athletic.
examples.question: Is it the case that August is not diplomatic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement that August is not diplomatic holds. To do that we will trace the chain of implications and disjunctions that involve August's attributes.

This conditional is crucial because it connects the property athletic to diplomatic. If we later establish that August is athletic, we will be able to infer his diplomatic status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if August is athletic, then he is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(august) :- athletic(august)</logic>
</step>

This rule links observant to responsible, giving us a way to move from an observation about August's attentiveness to a claim about his responsibility. It will become relevant after we determine whether August is observant.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if August is observant, then he is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(august) :- observant(august)</logic>
</step>

The implication that vigor leads to being rested provides a bridge from the known fact of vigor to a new fact of rest. This bridge will be used early in the derivation.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if August is vigorous, then he is rested.</text>
<logic>rested(august) :- vigorous(august)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a rule that being empathetic forces August to be not rested. This opposite relationship will allow us to rule out empathy once we know August is rested.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if August is empathetic, then he is not rested.</text>
<logic>~rested(august) :- empathetic(august)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possible alternatives for August: either he is empathetic or he is observant. Once one of these options is eliminated, the other must be true.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>We are told that either August is empathetic or August is observant.</text>
<logic>empathetic(august) v observant(august)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction tells us that at least one of the statements ‘August is not responsible’ or ‘August is athletic’ must hold. When we later learn about his responsibility, we can use this to infer athleticness.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>We are told that either August is not responsible or August is athletic.</text>
<logic>~responsible(august) v athletic(august)</logic>
</step>

The starting fact tells us directly that August possesses vigor. This concrete piece of information will trigger the rule that vigor implies rested.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that August is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(august)</logic>
</step>

Because we have the fact that August is vigorous and the rule that vigorous entails rested, we can apply modus ponens. Consequently, we conclude that August is rested.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>From August being vigorous and the rule that vigorous implies rested, we infer that August is rested.</text>
<logic>rested(august)</logic>
</step>

We now have August being rested, and we also have a rule stating that if he were empathetic he would be not rested. Since he is indeed rested, the antecedent (empathetic) must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Thus we derive that August is not empathetic.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since August is rested and the rule that empathetic would make him not rested, we conclude that August cannot be empathetic.</text>
<logic>~empathetic(august)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that August is not empathetic, the disjunction ‘empathetic or observant’ can no longer be satisfied by the first alternative. Therefore, the only way for the disjunction to remain true is for August to be observant.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Given that August is not empathetic and the disjunction that he is either empathetic or observant, we deduce that he must be observant.</text>
<logic>observant(august)</logic>
</step>

Having established that August is observant, we can invoke the rule that observant implies responsible. By modus ponens, this gives us the new fact that August is responsible.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because August is observant, the rule that observant implies responsible lets us conclude he is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(august)</logic>
</step>

We now possess the fact that August is responsible. The disjunction ‘not responsible or athletic’ tells us that at least one of those must be true. Since ‘not responsible’ is false, the remaining alternative, athletic, must be true.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>With August being responsible and the disjunction that either he is not responsible or athletic, we infer that he must be athletic.</text>
<logic>athletic(august)</logic>
</step>

Finally, with August confirmed as athletic, we use the first rule which states that athletic leads to diplomatic. Applying modus ponens again yields that August is diplomatic.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Finally, from August being athletic and the rule linking athletic to diplomatic, we derive that August is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(august)</logic>
</step>

We derived August is diplomatic, so the conclusion August is not diplomatic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is reference_ash archival?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the reference book reference_ash is archival. To answer, we will follow the logical chain supplied by the library's descriptive rules and the known facts about reference_ash and catalog_derek.

The first rule tells us that quietness guarantees informativeness for reference_ash. This will be useful later when we manage to establish that reference_ash is quiet.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If reference_ash is quiet, then it is informative.</text>
<logic>informative(reference_ash) :- quiet(reference_ash)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links archival status to a lack of informativeness. If we ever prove reference_ash is archival, we can immediately infer it is not informative, which will clash with other information.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If reference_ash is archival, then it is not informative.</text>
<logic>~informative(reference_ash) :- archival(reference_ash)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that either reference_ash is not well_organized or it is quiet. This gives us a way to obtain quietness once we can eliminate the first alternative.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either reference_ash is not well_organized, or it is quiet.</text>
<logic>~well_organized(reference_ash) v quiet(reference_ash)</logic>
</step>

The other disjunction offers that either reference_ash is not multidisciplinary or it is well_organized. Since we already know reference_ash is multidisciplinary, the first alternative is false, forcing the second to be true.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either reference_ash is not multidisciplinary, or it is well_organized.</text>
<logic>~multidisciplinary(reference_ash) v well_organized(reference_ash)</logic>
</step>

The fact that catalog_derek is accurate does not directly affect reference_ash, but it satisfies the requirement that all facts in the context be mentioned.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>catalog_derek is accurate.</text>
<logic>accurate(catalog_derek)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that reference_ash is multidisciplinary, which will be used with the disjunction in PREMISE_4 to derive a new property.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>reference_ash is multidisciplinary.</text>
<logic>multidisciplinary(reference_ash)</logic>
</step>

Because reference_ash is multidisciplinary and the disjunction says "either not multidisciplinary or well_organized," the multidisciplinary fact invalidates the "not multidisciplinary" side, leaving us with the conclusion that reference_ash must be well_organized. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism step.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>From reference_ash being multidisciplinary and the disjunction that either it is not multidisciplinary or it is well_organized, we infer that reference_ash is well_organized.</text>
<logic>well_organized(reference_ash)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have reference_ash as well_organized, we turn to the other disjunction: "either not well_organized or quiet." The well_organized status negates the first option, so we must accept that reference_ash is quiet. Again, disjunctive syllogism applies.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Using the well_organized status and the disjunction that either it is not well_organized or it is quiet, we deduce that reference_ash is quiet.</text>
<logic>quiet(reference_ash)</logic>
</step>

Having established that reference_ash is quiet, we can invoke the conditional from PREMISE_1: quiet implies informative. Therefore, reference_ash becomes informative via modus ponens.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Since reference_ash is quiet, the conditional rule gives us that reference_ash is informative.</text>
<logic>informative(reference_ash)</logic>
</step>

We now have both that reference_ash is informative and the rule that archival leads to not informative. By contraposition (modus tollens), if being archival would make it not informative, but we see it is informative, we must conclude that reference_ash cannot be archival, i.e., it is not archival.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Given that reference_ash is informative and the rule that archival implies not informative, we conclude that reference_ash cannot be archival.</text>
<logic>~archival(reference_ash)</logic>
</step>

We derived Reference_ash is not archival, so the conclusion Reference_ash is archival is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The bridge section is structurally sound. Whenever a bridge section is structurally sound, it is load-carrying. If a bridge section is long-lasting, then it is not load-carrying.
examples.question: Is the bridge section long-lasting?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the bridge section possesses the long-lasting property. The goal is to see if the given facts and rules force the opposite, making the claim false.

The first rule links structural soundness to the ability to carry load. Recognizing this connection is crucial because it lets us turn the known fact about soundness into information about load-carrying capability.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any bridge section that is structurally sound is also load-carrying.</text>
<logic>load_carrying(bridge_section) :- structurally_sound(bridge_section)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that being long-lasting would prevent a bridge section from being load-carrying. This conditional will later allow us to argue against the long-lasting claim once we know the section is load-carrying.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a bridge section is long-lasting, then it cannot be load-carrying.</text>
<logic>~load_carrying(bridge_section) :- long_lasting(bridge_section)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the bridge section is structurally sound provides the starting point for applying the first rule. Without this fact, we could not derive any information about load-carrying.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the bridge section is structurally sound.</text>
<logic>structurally_sound(bridge_section)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, we combine the fact of structural soundness with the rule that structural soundness implies load-carrying. This yields the new conclusion that the bridge section is indeed load-carrying.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Because the bridge section is structurally sound and structurally sound implies load-carrying, we infer that the bridge section is load-carrying.</text>
<logic>load_carrying(bridge_section)</logic>
</step>

Now we use modus tollens: we have established that the bridge section is load-carrying, but the rule says a long-lasting section would be not load-carrying. Therefore, the bridge section cannot be long-lasting, giving us the negation of that property.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since the bridge section is load-carrying and long-lasting would make it not load-carrying, we conclude that the bridge section cannot be long-lasting.</text>
<logic>~long_lasting(bridge_section)</logic>
</step>

We derived Bridge_section is not long lasting, so the conclusion Bridge_section is long lasting is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the chess scenario, the bishop is aggressive. Any piece that is positional is also strategic. Moreover, if a piece is strategic, then it is not aggressive. These statements capture all the given facts and rules for the bishop.
examples.question: Is the bishop positional?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the bishop has a positional style. To answer this, we will use the given facts about aggressiveness and the conditional relationships between positional, strategic, and aggressiveness.

The rule that any positional piece is strategic is important because it links the property we are trying to prove (positional) to another property (strategic) that appears in other rules. Keeping this connection in mind will allow us to reason backwards from what we know about the bishop.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any piece that is positional is also strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(bishop) :- positional(bishop)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that being strategic forces a piece to be non‑aggressive. Since we already know the bishop is aggressive, this rule will help us eliminate the possibility that the bishop is strategic, and thus affect the positional claim.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a piece is strategic, then it is not aggressive.</text>
<logic>~aggressive(bishop) :- strategic(bishop)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The bishop is aggressive.</text>
<logic>aggressive(bishop)</logic>
</step>

We start with the fact that the bishop is aggressive. The rule that a strategic piece must be non‑aggressive contradicts this fact if the bishop were strategic. By modus tollens, because the bishop is aggressive, it cannot be strategic, so we derive that the bishop is not strategic.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Since the bishop is aggressive, and being strategic would make it not aggressive, we can infer that the bishop cannot be strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(bishop)</logic>
</step>

Now we have that the bishop is not strategic. The earlier rule states that any positional piece must be strategic. Applying modus tollens again, the lack of strategic status means the bishop cannot be positional. Hence we infer that the bishop is not positional.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Because the bishop is not strategic, and being positional would make it strategic, we conclude the bishop is not positional.</text>
<logic>~positional(bishop)</logic>
</step>

We derived Bishop is not positional, so the conclusion Bishop is positional is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is Blake not curious?

This conditional connects scholarly status to systematic behavior. After we obtain scholarly, we can immediately infer systematic, which is needed for later disjunctive reasoning. It completes the forward progression from capable all the way to systematic.

<logic>systematic(blake) :- scholarly(blake)</logic>

This rule states that curiosity would force Blake to be not anxious. It will be crucial at the end: if we ever derive that Blake is anxious, the contrapositive reasoning will let us conclude that Blake cannot be curious.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Blake is curious then Blake is not anxious.</text>
<logic>~anxious(blake) :- curious(blake)</logic>

According to this rule, being witty would make Blake not friendly. Later, when we know Blake is friendly, we can use this to rule out the possibility that Blake is witty, narrowing down the disjunction involving witty and capable.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Blake is witty then Blake is not friendly.</text>
<logic>~friendly(blake) :- witty(blake)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction offers two alternatives: Blake is either witty or capable. By eliminating one alternative, we will be forced to accept the other. This will become the gateway to establishing capability.
<step n="6" type="premise">

<logic>witty(blake) v capable(blake)</logic>

The second disjunction says Blake is either not confident or friendly. Because we have a concrete fact that Blake is confident, the only way for this disjunction to hold is for Blake to be friendly. This will start a cascade of deductions.

<text>Either Blake is not confident or Blake is friendly.</text>
<logic>~confident(blake) v friendly(blake)</logic>

The third disjunction presents the options that Blake is either not systematic or anxious. Once we manage to prove Blake systematic, the only remaining option is that Blake is anxious, which will later trigger the curiosity rule.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Blake is not systematic or Blake is anxious.</text>
<logic>~systematic(blake) v anxious(blake)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Blake is confident is given outright. It will directly interact with the second disjunction, allowing us to deduce friendliness.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Blake is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(blake)</logic>
</step>

We combine the fact that Blake is confident with the disjunction that Blake is not confident or friendly. Since Blake is indeed confident, the 'not confident' part cannot be true, so the disjunction forces the other alternative: Blake must be friendly. This establishes the friendly status needed for the next step.

<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">

With capability established, we invoke PREMISE_1, which says capable leads to clever. By applying this conditional, we derive that Blake is clever. This moves us one step forward toward scholarly status.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Because Blake is capable and the rule that capable leads to clever, Blake is clever.</text>

Using the newly obtained cleverness and PREMISE_2, which links clever to scholarly, we conclude that Blake is scholarly. This continues the forward progression toward systematic behavior.

examples.context: The geological report states that the rock_sample is stress_prone and that it has been confirmed. Whenever a rock_sample is layered, it is unstable. If a rock_sample is stress_prone and there is no information contradicting it being layered, then we may consider it layered. If a rock_sample is unstable and there is no information contradicting it being in a protected_zone, then we may consider it in a protected_zone. No other alternatives or disjunctions are mentioned.
examples.question: Is it true that the rock_sample is not in a protected zone?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a set of geological observations and default assumptions about a particular rock_sample. Our goal is to determine whether the statement that the sample is not in a protected zone holds, i.e., to evaluate the negation of protected_zone(rock_sample).

The rule linking layered to unstable is central because it lets us turn knowledge about the sample's internal structure into a statement about its stability. Recognizing this conditional will later allow us to derive instability once we have established layering.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the sample is layered, then it is unstable.</text>
<logic>unstable(rock_sample) :- layered(rock_sample)</logic>
</step>

The default rule tells us that a stress_prone sample can be assumed layered unless there is explicit evidence against it. Since we already know the sample is stress_prone, this premise sets up the possibility of inferring layering.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Because the sample is stress_prone and we lack contrary evidence, we can assume it is layered.</text>
<logic>layered(rock_sample) :- stress_prone(rock_sample), not ab_layered(rock_sample)</logic>
</step>

This default rule connects instability to membership in a protected zone, again subject to the absence of contrary evidence. It will be the bridge that lets us move from instability to the protected_zone conclusion.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the sample is unstable and we lack contrary evidence, we can assume it lies in a protected zone.</text>
<logic>protected_zone(rock_sample) :- unstable(rock_sample), not ab_protected_zone(rock_sample)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The sample has been confirmed.</text>
<logic>confirmed(rock_sample)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The sample is stress_prone.</text>
<logic>stress_prone(rock_sample)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the sample is stress_prone together with the default rule from PREMISE_2, we can adopt the conclusion that the sample is layered. The default rule operates because there is no ab_consistent information blocking the inference, so layered(rock_sample) follows.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From the stress_prone fact and the default rule, we infer that the sample is layered.</text>
<logic>layered(rock_sample)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established layered(rock_sample), the conditional from PREMISE_1 applies directly: a layered sample must be unstable. By modus ponens, we derive unstable(rock_sample).
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Since we now know the sample is layered, the rule gives us that it is unstable.</text>
<logic>unstable(rock_sample)</logic>
</step>

examples.context: Hollis, Kurt, and Nico are all industrious bees. Specifically, Hollis is industrious, Kurt is industrious, and Nico is industrious. Whenever a bee is industrious, it is also cheerful; thus any industrious bee becomes cheerful. Whenever a bee is cheerful, it becomes active, so any cheerful bee is active as well. If a bee is active, then it becomes motivated, meaning activity guarantees motivation. Whenever a bee is motivated, it becomes courageous, so motivation leads to courage. If a bee is rested, then that bee is not courageous; restedness forces a lack of courage. No other rules or defaults apply.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Hollis is not rested?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Hollis can be shown to be not rested. The goal is to use the given facts and rules to derive the negation of rested for Hollis. To achieve this we will follow the chain of implications from industriousness all the way to courage, then apply the rule that restedness would contradict courage.

The first rule tells us that cheerfulness guarantees activity for Hollis. This will be useful later once we have established that Hollis is cheerful, enabling us to infer activity. It sets up a direct conditional link we will later activate.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Hollis is cheerful, Hollis is active.</text>
<logic>active(hollis) :- cheerful(hollis)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects industriousness to cheerfulness for Hollis. Since we already know Hollis is industrious, this rule will let us derive that Hollis is cheerful, the starting point for the chain toward activity.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Hollis is industrious, Hollis is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(hollis) :- industrious(hollis)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that if Hollis is motivated then he becomes courageous. This will be invoked after we obtain motivation, allowing us to move from motivation to courage, which is needed for the final contradiction with restedness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Hollis is motivated then Hollis is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(hollis) :- motivated(hollis)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule links activity to motivation for Hollis. Once we have activity, this rule will let us infer motivation, continuing the forward flow toward courage.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Hollis is active then Hollis is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(hollis) :- active(hollis)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule states that restedness forces a lack of courage for Hollis. This is the crucial rule for the final step: if we can prove that Hollis is courageous, then restedness would contradict that, letting us infer not rested by contrapositive reasoning.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Hollis is rested then Hollis is not courageous.</text>
<logic>~courageous(hollis) :- rested(hollis)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Kurt is industrious is recorded but not directly needed for the query about Hollis. It is included to satisfy the requirement that all facts be mentioned in the context.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Kurt is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(kurt)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the fact that Nico is industrious is part of the background information. It does not influence the derivation concerning Hollis, yet it fulfills the completeness condition.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Nico is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(nico)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Hollis himself is industrious is essential; it activates the rule linking industriousness to cheerfulness. This is the foundational premise from which the rest of the reasoning proceeds.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Hollis is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(hollis)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Hollis is industrious together with the rule that any industrious bee is cheerful, we can directly infer that Hollis is cheerful. This step applies universal instantiation of the rule to the specific individual Hollis.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>From Hollis being industrious and the rule that any industrious bee is cheerful, we infer that Hollis is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(hollis)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Hollis is cheerful, we can apply the rule that cheerfulness leads to activity. By modus ponens, the antecedent (cheerful) holds, so we conclude that Hollis is active.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Because Hollis is cheerful and the rule linking cheerfulness to activity, we deduce that Hollis is active.</text>
<logic>active(hollis)</logic>

With Hollis active, the rule stating that activity implies motivation becomes applicable. Again using modus ponens, we derive that Hollis is motivated.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">

<logic>motivated(hollis)</logic>

Having obtained motivation for Hollis, we invoke the rule that motivation yields courage. Modus ponens gives us that Hollis is courageous.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">

<logic>courageous(hollis)</logic>
</step>

We now have Hollis courageous, but the rule says that if Hollis were rested, he would not be courageous. This creates a direct conflict: courage and not-courage cannot both be true. By applying modus tollens, we infer the negation of the antecedent, concluding that Hollis is not rested.

examples.context: The courtyard is academic_style. The facade is also academic_style, but the facade is not sustainable. Whenever the courtyard is innovative, it is well_lit. examples.question: Is the courtyard experienced?

The rule linking innovation to illumination tells us that any innovative courtyard would automatically be well_lit. This connection could later help us infer other properties if we ever learn that the courtyard is innovative.

The sustainability‑to‑sturdiness rule states that a sustainable courtyard must be sturdy. Knowing whether the courtyard is sustainable will therefore give us direct access to its sturdiness, which is useful for later deductions.

<text>If the courtyard is sustainable, then it is sturdy.</text>
<logic>sturdy(courtyard) :- sustainable(courtyard)</logic>

The rule about a lack of innovation leading to symmetry provides a way to obtain the symmetrical attribute when innovation is absent. This alternative path is important because it lets us derive symmetry without assuming innovation.

From symmetry we can infer iconic status, according to the fourth rule. Thus, if we ever manage to establish that the courtyard is symmetrical, we will also know it is iconic.

<logic>iconic(courtyard) :- symmetrical(courtyard)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule says that a courtyard that is not sustainable becomes timeless. This gives us a route to the timeless attribute that depends on the negation of sustainability, which we might be able to prove later.

<text>If the courtyard is not sustainable, then it is timeless.</text>
<logic>timeless(courtyard) :- ~sustainable(courtyard)</logic>

The sixth rule creates a negative link: a well_lit courtyard cannot be academic_style. This will be handy if we ever derive well_lit, because it will contradict the explicit fact that the courtyard is academic_style.

<text>If the courtyard is well_lit, then it is not academic_style.</text>
<logic>~academic_style(courtyard) :- well_lit(courtyard)</logic>

Similarly, the seventh rule tells us that a sturdy courtyard cannot be iconic. Should we infer sturdiness, we will obtain a direct conflict with any claim that the courtyard is iconic.

<text>If the courtyard is sturdy, then it is not iconic.</text>
<logic>~iconic(courtyard) :- sturdy(courtyard)</logic>

The eighth rule provides that a functional courtyard is not timeless. This offers another way to negate timelessness, complementing the earlier rule that produces timelessness under a different condition.

<text>If the courtyard is functional, then it is not timeless.</text>
<logic>~timeless(courtyard) :- functional(courtyard)</logic>

examples.question: Is Maria not astute?

<think>

The rule states that if Maria were astute, she could not be inventive. This contrapositive relationship will be pivotal once we have an inventive fact, allowing us to infer the negation of astuteness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Maria is astute, then she is not inventive.</text>
<logic>~creative(maria) :- sharp(maria)</logic>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either Maria is not consistent or she is scholarly. When we later confirm that Maria is consistent, the only way the disjunction can hold is if Maria is scholarly, giving us a new fact.

<step n="6" type="premise">

Using the fact that Maria is rested together with PREMISE_2, we apply modus ponens to conclude that Maria is goal‑oriented. This converts the initial fact into a form that can trigger PREMISE_1.

We now have Maria as inventive, but PREMISE_4 tells us that an astute person cannot be inventive. Applying modus tollens, the presence of inventiveness forces the negation of astuteness, giving us ~astute(Maria).

We need to decide whether Bruce's urban planning approach can be described as eco‑friendly. The goal is to see if the premise that Bruce is responsible (eco‑friendly) can be supported by the given facts and rules.

The first rule connects flexibility with low traffic, which will let us derive a concrete property about Bruce's plan. Knowing this link is crucial because it lets us turn the fact about flexibility into an observable outcome.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Bruce's plan is flexible, then it results in low traffic.</text>
<logic>low_traffic(bruce) :- flexible_design(bruce)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that eco‑friendliness forces the opposite of low traffic. This conditional will be useful later to test whether Bruce can simultaneously be flexible and eco‑friendly.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Bruce's plan is eco-friendly, then it is not low traffic.</text>
<logic>~low_traffic(bruce) :- eco_friendly(bruce)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Bruce's plan is flexible gives us a starting point for applying the first rule. With this fact we can begin the chain of deductions needed to evaluate eco‑friendliness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>We are told that Bruce's plan is flexible.</text>
<logic>flexible_design(bruce)</logic>
</step>

By applying the first rule to the fact that Bruce's plan is flexible, we deduce that the plan results in low traffic. This step converts the abstract property of flexibility into a concrete traffic condition, setting up the contrast needed for the next rule.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Using Bruce's flexibility together with the first rule, we infer that his plan leads to low traffic.</text>
<logic>low_traffic(bruce)</logic>
</step>

Now we have low traffic, but the second rule says an eco‑friendly plan would make traffic not low. Since low traffic is true, the condition for eco‑friendliness would lead to a contradiction, so we infer that Bruce's plan cannot be eco‑friendly, i.e., it is not responsible.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Because we have low traffic and the second rule links eco-friendliness to not low traffic, we conclude that Bruce's plan cannot be eco-friendly.</text>
<logic>~eco_friendly(bruce)</logic>
</step>

We derived Bruce is not eco friendly, so the conclusion Bruce is eco friendly is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the ballet troupe, the dancers Cleo, Elena, and Wade are each described as adaptable performers. The company notes that whenever Elena is adaptable, she becomes observant on stage. Furthermore, the troupe follows the principle that any dancer who is observant will also feel anxious during rehearsals. Consequently, if Elena is observant, she will be anxious. These statements capture all the known facts and conditional rules about adaptability, observance, and anxiety among the three dancers.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Elena is enthusiastic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Elena can be said to be enthusiastic given the troupe's characteristics. To answer, we must examine the facts about adaptability and the conditional rules linking adaptability, observance, and anxiety, and see if any chain leads to enthusiasm.

I need to determine whether Elena is enthusiastic can be derived from the given premises.

Recognizing the first rule helps us understand how anxiety could arise for Elena. It tells us that once we know Elena is observant, we can immediately infer that she is anxious, which is a useful intermediate property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Elena is observant, then Elena is anxious.</text>
<logic>anxious(elena) :- observant(elena)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects Elena's adaptability to observance, providing a bridge from a known fact to the condition used in the first rule. If we can establish Elena's adaptability, we can then deduce her observance and subsequently her anxiety.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Elena is adaptable, then Elena is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(elena) :- adaptable(elena)</logic>
</step>

Stating that Cleo is adaptable adds a concrete fact to the knowledge base, though it does not directly involve Elena. It demonstrates that adaptability is a trait present in the troupe, reinforcing the relevance of the adaptability‑observance rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Cleo is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(cleo)</logic>
</step>

This premise confirms that Elena herself possesses the adaptable property. Because the rule in PREMISE_2 applies specifically to Elena, this fact enables us to infer that Elena is observant.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Elena is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(elena)</logic>
</step>

Mentioning Wade's adaptability further illustrates that adaptability is common among the dancers, but like Cleo’s fact, it does not affect Elena’s chain of reasoning toward enthusiasm.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Wade is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(wade)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Elena is enthusiastic: no rule or disjunction has "enthusiastic" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(enthusiastic(elena))</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Elena is not enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~enthusiastic(elena))</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">
<text>Neither Elena is enthusiastic nor Elena is not enthusiastic can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(enthusiastic(elena))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: In the workshop, the oak beam, the pine plank, the cedar shingle, and the mahogany door are all noted for their precision. Specifically, the oak beam is precise, the pine plank is precise, the cedar shingle is precise, and the mahogany door is precise. Moreover, the foreman knows that whenever the oak beam is precise, it is also careful. Furthermore, the foreman also states that if the oak beam is careful, then it is reliable.
examples.question: Is the oak beam reliable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given information about several items in a carpentry workshop and a chain of properties that link precision to reliability. Our goal is to determine whether Brock, the oak beam, can be concluded to be reliable based on the provided facts and rules.

The first rule tells us that if Brock is meticulous, then he is compassionate. This conditional will let us move from Brock’s precision to his carefulness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Brock is meticulous, then Brock is compassionate.</text>
<logic>careful(oak_beam) :- precise(oak_beam)</logic>
</step>

The second rule says that if Brock is compassionate, then he is responsible. Together with the first rule, it creates a two‑step path from meticulous to responsible.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Brock is compassionate, then Brock is responsible.</text>
<logic>reliable(oak_beam) :- careful(oak_beam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>James is meticulous.</text>
<logic>precise(pine_plank)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Steve is meticulous.</text>
<logic>precise(cedar_shingle)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Zion is meticulous.</text>
<logic>precise(mahogany_door)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Brock is meticulous.</text>
<logic>precise(oak_beam)</logic>
</step>

Since we know from Premise 6 that Brock is meticulous, and Premise 1 gives the implication from meticulous to compassionate, we can apply modus ponens to obtain that Brock is compassionate. This establishes the intermediate property needed for the next rule.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From Brock being meticulous and the first rule, we infer that Brock is compassionate.</text>
<logic>careful(oak_beam)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Brock being compassionate, Premise 2 allows us to infer that Brock is responsible by another application of modus ponens. Thus the chain of implications leads directly to the desired conclusion.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Brock being compassionate and the second rule, we infer that Brock is responsible.</text>
<logic>reliable(oak_beam)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Oak_beam is reliable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Skyler is known to be courageous. Whenever something is organized, it is also inventive, so if Skyler were organized, he would be inventive. Being studious guarantees meticulousness, meaning that if Skyler were studious, he would be meticulous. Conversely, any inventive person is automatically studious, so if Skyler were inventive, he would be studious. If Skyler happens to be well prepared, then he cannot be capable. Should Skyler be analytical, that would make him not eloquent. Moreover, Skyler is either analytical or organized. It is also the case that either Skyler is not courageous or he is eloquent. Finally, either Skyler is not meticulous or he is capable.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude whether Skyler is diligent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the property of being diligent applies to Skyler. To answer, we must examine all the facts, conditional rules, and alternative statements that describe Skyler's attributes.

I need to determine whether Skyler is diligent can be derived from the given premises.

The first rule tells us that organization would automatically make Skyler inventive. This link is important because it could start a chain leading to other traits, but we do not yet know whether Skyler is organized.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Skyler is organized, then Skyler is inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(skyler) :- organized(skyler)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that studiousness guarantees meticulousness. If we could establish that Skyler is studious, we would obtain meticulousness, yet the rule works only in that direction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Skyler is studious, then Skyler is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(skyler) :- studious(skyler)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides the converse connection: inventiveness implies studiousness. Combined with the first rule, it shows a potential loop—organized → inventive → studious—but again, we lack any concrete premise that activates the chain.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Skylet is inventive, then Skyler is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(skyler) :- inventive(skyler)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that being well prepared forces Skyler to be not capable. This negative information could interact with other disjunctions, but we have no fact about Skyler's preparation status.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Skyler is well prepared, then Skyler is not capable.</text>
<logic>~capable(skyler) :- well_prepared(skyler)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule links analyticalness to a lack of eloquence. Knowing whether Skyler is analytical would let us infer that he is not eloquent, which could affect the disjunction involving eloquence, yet the analytical status is only given as a possibility.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Skyler is analytical, then Skyler is not eloquent.</text>
<logic>~eloquent(skyler) :- analytical(skyler)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that Skyler is courageous gives us a concrete truth to work with. It directly settles the positive side of the courage‑eloquence disjunction, allowing us to infer that the alternative "not courageous" must be false, which in turn forces the other side—eloquence—to be true.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Skyler is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(skyler)</logic>

<step n="7" type="derived">

<logic>unknown(diligent(skyler))</logic>
