Answer: Unknown

examples.context: Zebra_mark is assertive. Whenever a zebra is assertive, it becomes timid. Any zebra that is equitable is also social. Being prudent guarantees that a zebra is equitable. A social zebra is well restored. Whenever a zebra is well restored, it becomes studious. Either zebra_mark is not timid, or it is prudent.
examples.question: Is zebra_mark studious?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether zebra_mark is studious. The goal is to follow the chain of conditions provided in the background to reach that conclusion.

The first rule tells us that assertiveness leads to timidity. This link will let us move from the known fact about assertiveness toward other properties.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a zebra is assertive, then it is timid.</text>
<logic>timid(zebra_mark) :- assertive(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects being equitable with being social. We will need this later once we establish that zebra_mark is equitable.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Any zebra that is equitable is also social.</text>
<logic>social(zebra_mark) :- equitable(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that prudence guarantees equity. Getting prudence will therefore give us equity, unlocking the previous rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being prudent guarantees that a zebra is equitable.</text>
<logic>equitable(zebra_mark) :- prudent(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule links social behavior to being well restored. Once we know zebra_mark is social, we can infer it is well restored.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>A social zebra is well restored.</text>
<logic>well_restored(zebra_mark) :- social(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule states that a well‑restored zebra is studious. This is the final step that will give us the answer we seek.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Whenever a zebra is well restored, it becomes studious.</text>
<logic>studious(zebra_mark) :- well_restored(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either zebra_mark is not timid or it is prudent. It will be used together with the timidity fact to force prudence.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either the zebra is not timid, or it is prudent.</text>
<logic>~timid(zebra_mark) v prudent(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

We are told directly that zebra_mark is assertive. This fact is the starting point for the whole inference chain.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Zebra_mark is assertive.</text>
<logic>assertive(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that zebra_mark is assertive and the rule that assertiveness implies timidity, we conclude that zebra_mark is timid. This establishes the left side of the disjunction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since zebra_mark is assertive, we can infer that it is timid.</text>
<logic>timid(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

Given that zebra_mark is timid, the disjunction "not timid or prudent" cannot be satisfied by the first option, so the second option must hold; therefore zebra_mark is prudent. This moves us toward equity.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>Because zebra_mark is timid, the disjunction forces that zebra_mark must be prudent.</text>
<logic>prudent(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

Since zebra_mark is prudent and prudence guarantees equity, we infer that zebra_mark is equitable. Now we can apply the rule linking equity to social behavior.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Being prudent means zebra_mark is equitable.</text>
<logic>equitable(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

Equity leads to social behavior, so zebra_mark is social. This activates the rule that a social zebra is well restored.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>An equitable zebra is social, so zebra_mark is social.</text>
<logic>social(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

Because zebra_mark is social, we infer that it is well restored. This prepares us to use the final rule about studiousness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>A social zebra is well restored, therefore zebra_mark is well restored.</text>
<logic>well_restored(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

A well‑restored zebra is studious, thus zebra_mark is studious. We have now derived the desired property.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,5">
<text>A well restored zebra is studious, so zebra_mark is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(zebra_mark)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Zebra_mark is studious, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the mythic garden, Eden is known to be ordered. Whenever something is clever, it is also inspired. Whenever something is exact, it is clever. If a being is heroic, then it cannot be faithful. If a being is divine, then it cannot be studious. Moreover, Eden either possesses divine power or is exact. Either Eden is not inspired or it is faithful. Finally, either Eden is not ordered or it is studious.
examples.question: Is Eden heroic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the mythic figure Eden possesses the quality of heroism. To answer, we must trace the logical consequences of the given facts, rules, and disjunctions until we reach a statement about Eden's heroism.

This premise tells us that cleverness is sufficient for inspiration, which will later let us turn a derived cleverness fact into an inspired one. It sets up a forward‑chaining link we will need.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any clever entity is also inspired.</text>
<logic>inspired(eden) :- clever(eden)</logic>
</step>

Here, exactness guarantees cleverness, so once we know Eden is exact we can immediately infer cleverness. This conditional will be crucial after we establish exactness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being exact guarantees that one is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(eden) :- exact(eden)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects heroism to the impossibility of being faithful; it will allow us to contradict heroism once we have shown Eden is faithful. It is the final bridge to the query.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If something is heroic, it cannot be faithful.</text>
<logic>~faithful(eden) :- heroic(eden)</logic>
</step>

This rule says that divinity rules out studiousness. When we later learn Eden is studious, we will be able to deny Eden's divinity, which will help resolve a disjunction about divinity versus exactness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If something is divine, it cannot be studious.</text>
<logic>~studious(eden) :- divine(eden)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a choice between divinity and exactness for Eden. After we eliminate one option, the other must hold, giving us a concrete property to work with.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Eden is either divine or exact.</text>
<logic>divine(eden) v exact(eden)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction links inspiration to faithfulness. Once we have either the presence or absence of inspiration, we can deduce something about faithfulness, feeding into the heroism rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Eden is not inspired or Eden is faithful.</text>
<logic>~inspired(eden) v faithful(eden)</logic>
</step>

The last disjunction relates being ordered to being studious. Combined with the fact that Eden is ordered, it will force Eden to be studious, which then triggers the divinity rule.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Eden is not ordered or Eden is studious.</text>
<logic>~ordered(eden) v studious(eden)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact states that Eden is ordered, providing the starting point for applying the disjunction in PREMISE_7.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Eden is ordered.</text>
<logic>ordered(eden)</logic>
</step>

Because Eden is ordered, the disjunction "not ordered or studious" forces the second alternative to be true; otherwise the disjunction would be false. Therefore, Eden must be studious. This step converts the ordering fact into a new property.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,7">
<text>From Eden being ordered and the disjunction that either Eden is not ordered or Eden is studious, we infer that Eden is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(eden)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Eden is studious, we invoke the rule that a divine being cannot be studious. Since Eden is indeed studious, the only way the rule can hold is if Eden is not divine. This gives us the negation of divinity.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Because Eden is studious and the rule that a divine being cannot be studious, we conclude that Eden is not divine.</text>
<logic>~divine(eden)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction from PREMISE_5 tells us Eden is either divine or exact. We have just shown Eden is not divine, so the only remaining possibility is that Eden is exact. Thus we can assert Eden's exactness.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Since Eden is not divine and the disjunction that Eden is either divine or exact, we deduce that Eden is exact.</text>
<logic>exact(eden)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Eden is exact, we apply PREMISE_2, which says exactness implies cleverness. Consequently, Eden is clever. This bridges the exactness we just derived to the cleverness needed for later steps.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>From Eden being exact and the rule that exactness implies cleverness, we obtain that Eden is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(eden)</logic>
</step>

With Eden recognized as clever, PREMISE_1 lets us turn that cleverness into inspiration. Hence Eden becomes inspired, providing the left side of the disjunction in PREMISE_6.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Given Eden's cleverness and the rule that cleverness leads to inspiration, we infer that Eden is inspired.</text>
<logic>inspired(eden)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction in PREMISE_6 states that either Eden is not inspired or Eden is faithful. Since we have just proven Eden is inspired, the first alternative is false, forcing the second alternative to be true. Therefore Eden is faithful.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">
<text>With Eden being inspired and the disjunction that either Eden is not inspired or Eden is faithful, we derive that Eden is faithful.</text>
<logic>faithful(eden)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using the rule from PREMISE_3, which says heroism precludes faithfulness, and having shown Eden is faithful, we must conclude that Eden cannot be heroic. This gives us the negation of the query.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,3">
<text>Because Eden is faithful and the rule that heroism prevents faithfulness, we infer that Eden is not heroic.</text>
<logic>~heroic(eden)</logic>
</step>

We derived Eden is not heroic, so the conclusion Eden is heroic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Within the wildlife reserve, the tiger, the elephant, the orangutan, and the gorilla are all known for their generosity toward other animals. Specifically, the tiger is generous, the elephant is generous, the orangutan is generous, and the gorilla is generous. Moreover, any elephant that is generous is also cooperative; that is, generosity guarantees cooperativeness for the elephant. In addition, if the elephant happens to be adaptable, then it would not be cooperative. No other rules or exceptions apply.
examples.question: Based on the information, is the elephant not adaptable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several facts about four animals and two conditional rules that involve the elephant. Our goal is to determine whether the information forces the conclusion that the elephant is not adaptable.

The first rule connects generosity to cooperativeness for the elephant, which will let us turn a fact about generosity into a fact about cooperation. This connection is essential because later we will need a cooperative statement about the elephant.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if the elephant is generous then the elephant is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(elephant) :- generous(elephant)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that adaptability would lead to the opposite of cooperativeness for the elephant. This creates a tension: if we can show the elephant is cooperative, then adaptability must be impossible.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if the elephant is adaptable then the elephant is not cooperative.</text>
<logic>~cooperative(elephant) :- adaptable(elephant)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The tiger is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(tiger)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The orangutan is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(orangutan)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The gorilla is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(gorilla)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The elephant is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(elephant)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the elephant is generous together with the first rule, we apply a straightforward implication: generosity guarantees cooperativeness, so the elephant must be cooperative. This derived cooperative fact is the bridge to the next step.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Because the elephant is generous and generosity implies cooperativeness for the elephant, we conclude that the elephant is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(elephant)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the elephant is cooperative, we look back at the second rule, which says that adaptability would force the elephant to be not cooperative. Since the elephant is already cooperative, the condition that would make it not cooperative cannot hold, meaning the elephant cannot be adaptable. This is a classic contrapositive reasoning.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Since the elephant is cooperative, and if it were adaptable it would have to be not cooperative, we infer that the elephant cannot be adaptable.</text>
<logic>~adaptable(elephant)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Elephant is not adaptable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Ash, Dean, and Kent are three individuals in a small firm. Ash, Dean, and Kent are all focused. In addition, Ash and Kent are vigorous. The company follows a number of logical policies: whenever someone is focused, that person is also content; any systematic person is determined; any humble person is diligent; any talented person is humble; any vigorous person is systematic; any determined person is talented; and any content person is vigorous. These policies link the various attributes together in a chain of implications. No other facts or defaults are given.
examples.question: Is it true that Dean is not diligent?

<think>
We are asked to decide whether Dean fails to be diligent. The premises give a network of conditional statements that connect focus, content, vigor, systematicity, determination, talent, humility, and diligence. Our goal is to trace a chain from the known facts about Dean to the property of diligence and see whether the negation can hold.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If someone is focused, then they are content.</text>
<logic>content(dean) :- focused(dean)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If someone is systematic, then they are determined.</text>
<logic>determined(dean) :- systematic(dean)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If someone is humble, then they are diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(dean) :- humble(dean)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If someone is talented, then they are humble.</text>
<logic>humble(dean) :- talented(dean)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If someone is vigorous, then they are systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(dean) :- vigorous(dean)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If someone is determined, then they are talented.</text>
<logic>talented(dean) :- determined(dean)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If someone is content, then they are vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(dean) :- content(dean)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Ash is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(ash)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Kent is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(kent)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Ash is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(ash)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Kent is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(kent)</logic>

<step n="12" type="premise">

examples.context: Bree's photo is vibrant. The photo of bree is either underexposed or in focus. Whenever a photo is sharp_image, it is also well_composed. If a photo is vibrant, then it has bold_contrast. If a photo is in_focus, then it has wide_aperture. If a photo has wide_aperture, then it is sharp_image. If a photo is high_iso, then it is not well_composed. If a photo is underexposed, then it is not bold_contrast.
examples.question: Can we determine whether bree's photo is consistent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide if bree's photo can be judged consistent given the photographic properties described. Consistency here means that no contradictory attributes can be derived from the information provided.

I need to determine whether Bree is consistent can be derived from the given premises.

The rule linking sharpness to good composition tells us that whenever we can prove the photo is sharp_image, we will also have well_composed. This connection is useful because it allows us to propagate a sharpness claim forward to composition, which may later clash with other rules.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Sharpness of bree's photo guarantees it is well composed.</text>
<logic>well_composed(bree) :- sharp_image(bree)</logic>
</step>

The statement that vibrancy implies bold contrast gives us a direct way to infer bold_contrast from the known fact that bree's photo is vibrant. Since bold_contrast appears in another rule about underexposure, this premise helps us explore possible contradictions.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If bree's photo is vibrant, then it has bold contrast.</text>
<logic>bold_contrast(bree) :- vibrant(bree)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that in_focus leads to wide_aperture allows us to turn any claim of focus into a claim of a wide aperture. This is important because wide_aperture, in turn, leads to sharp_image, creating a chain that could affect consistency.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If bree's photo is in focus, then it has a wide aperture.</text>
<logic>wide_aperture(bree) :- in_focus(bree)</logic>
</step>

The rule that a wide aperture guarantees sharpness closes the loop in the chain started by premise 3. If we ever establish wide_aperture, we can immediately infer sharp_image, which may then trigger the well_composed property via premise 1.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If bree's photo has a wide aperture, then it is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp_image(bree) :- wide_aperture(bree)</logic>
</step>

The negative rule about high ISO tells us that a high ISO setting prevents the photo from being well composed. This introduces a potential conflict: if we could infer both high_iso and well_composed, we would have an inconsistency.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If bree's photo uses high ISO, then it is not well composed.</text>
<logic>~well_composed(bree) :- high_iso(bree)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the rule linking underexposure to a lack of bold contrast creates a possible contradiction with premise 2. If underexposed were true while vibrancy (and thus bold_contrast) were also true, we would derive both bold_contrast and its negation.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If bree's photo is underexposed, then it lacks bold contrast.</text>
<logic>~bold_contrast(bree) :- underexposed(bree)</logic>
</step>

The fact that bree's photo is vibrant is the only positive piece of concrete information we have. It directly yields bold_contrast via premise 2, but does not by itself settle the truth of any of the other properties such as in_focus, high_iso, or underexposed.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Bree's photo is vibrant.</text>
<logic>vibrant(bree)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Bree is consistent: no rule or disjunction has "consistent" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(consistent(bree))</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Bree is not consistent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~consistent(bree))</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="derived">
<text>Neither Bree is consistent nor Bree is not consistent can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(consistent(bree))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: Eva is punctual and Oscar is resourceful. Whenever someone is resourceful, they become sharp, so Oscar is sharp. If someone is determined, then they are fair‑minded, and Oscar is determined or not sharp; the disjunction states that either Oscar is not sharp or Oscar is determined. For Eva, any proactive behavior guarantees she is not punctual, i.e., if Eva is proactive then Eva is not punctual. Eva is either proactive or responsible (or both). Moreover, if Eva is responsible then she is scholarly, and if Eva is scholarly then she is courageous. These rules together capture all the given facts, conditionals, and disjunctions.
examples.question: Is Eva courageous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a collection of facts and conditional statements about Eva and Oscar, and we need to determine whether Eva possesses the courageous property. The goal is to chain the available information so that we can derive courage for Eva.

This premise connects scholarly status directly to courage, which will be essential once we establish that Eva is scholarly. It tells us that any scholarly Eva must also be courageous.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Eva is scholarly then she is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(eva) :- scholarly(eva)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a bridge from responsibility to scholarship; if we can show Eva is responsible, we will be able to move toward scholarly status. This rule is a key link in the chain we intend to build.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Eva is responsible then she is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(eva) :- responsible(eva)</logic>
</step>

This conditional lets us use Eva’s punctual fact to reason about her proactivity. If Eva were proactive, she would contradict her known punctuality, which will help us eliminate the proactive alternative.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Eva is proactive then she is not punctual.</text>
<logic>~punctual(eva) :- proactive(eva)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possible ways Eva could satisfy the earlier conditions: either she is proactive or she is responsible. By ruling out one option, we can infer the other.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The statement that Eva is either proactive or responsible.</text>
<logic>proactive(eva) v responsible(eva)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Oscar is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(oscar)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Eva is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(eva)</logic>
</step>

Eva is known to be punctual. If she were proactive, the rule would make her not punctual, which clashes with the fact, so we must reject the possibility of her being proactive. Thus we conclude that Eva is not proactive.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>From Eva being punctual and the rule that proactive would make her not punctual, we infer that Eva cannot be proactive.</text>
<logic>~proactive(eva)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Eva is not proactive, yet the disjunction says she is either proactive or responsible. Since the proactive side is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Eva to be responsible. Therefore Eva is responsible.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Since Eva is not proactive and we know she is either proactive or responsible, we conclude that Eva must be responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(eva)</logic>
</step>

Now that Eva is responsible, the rule linking responsibility to scholarship applies, allowing us to infer that Eva is scholarly. This moves us one step closer to the target property of courage.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because Eva is responsible and the rule that responsibility leads to scholarship, we infer that Eva is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(eva)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that Eva is scholarly, we can invoke the earlier rule that any scholarly Eva is courageous. Consequently, Eva must be courageous.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Given that Eva is scholarly and the rule that scholarship leads to courage, we deduce that Eva is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(eva)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Eva is courageous, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Inside the bustling cellar, Kira is known to be courageous. The winemakers also state that if Kira happens to be well prepared, then she will be friendly. Conversely, whenever Kira is prepared, she will not be friendly. Moreover, the staff remind us that either Kira is not courageous or she is well prepared. These statements together form the complete set of information.
examples.question: Is Kira prepared?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the proposition prepared(kira) holds true based on the information provided. The goal is to see if the available facts and logical connections force Kira to be prepared or to deny it.

The first rule connects well‑preparedness to friendliness, which will be useful later when we discover Kira's preparation status. Keeping this conditional in mind allows us to move from a well prepared state to a friendly one.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that being well prepared guarantees friendliness for Kira.</text>
<logic>friendly(kira) :- well_prepared(kira)</logic>
</step>

The second rule gives a contrapositive link: if Kira were prepared, she would not be friendly. This will become crucial once we establish Kira's friendliness, enabling us to infer the opposite of preparation.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that if Kira is prepared, she cannot be friendly.</text>
<logic>~friendly(kira) :- prepared(kira)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives about Kira's courage and preparation: either she lacks courage or she is well prepared. This statement sets up a classic disjunctive syllogism once we know one of the alternatives is false.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction states that Kira is either not courageous or she is well prepared.</text>
<logic>~courageous(kira) v well_prepared(kira)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Kira is courageous, which directly contradicts the first part of the disjunction. Knowing this fact lets us eliminate the "not courageous" option.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>We are told directly that Kira is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(kira)</logic>
</step>

Because Kira is courageous, the "not courageous" side of the disjunction is false, leaving only the "well prepared" alternative as true. Hence, by disjunctive syllogism we can conclude that Kira is well prepared.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>From Kira being courageous and the disjunction, we eliminate the possibility that she is not courageous, concluding that she must be well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(kira)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have well_prepared(kira), we can apply the first rule: any entity that is well prepared is friendly. This gives us friendly(kira) through a straightforward modus ponens.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Given that Kira is well prepared, the earlier rule lets us infer that she is friendly.</text>
<logic>friendly(kira)</logic>
</step>

With friendly(kira) established, we turn to the second rule, which says a prepared individual cannot be friendly. Using modus tollens, the presence of friendliness forces the negation of the antecedent, so we infer that Kira is not prepared.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since Kira is friendly, the rule linking preparation to non‑friendliness lets us infer that she cannot be prepared.</text>
<logic>~prepared(kira)</logic>
</step>

We derived Kira is not prepared, so the conclusion Kira is prepared is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: West is a psychologist who is highly motivated. Whenever someone is motivated, that person is also driven. Moreover, any driven individual is resilient. These two conditional statements together describe how motivation leads to resilience in West's professional demeanor.
examples.question: Is West resilient?

We need to determine whether West possesses resilience. The goal is to trace a logical path from the known facts about West to the property of being resilient.

The rule that motivated individuals are driven is crucial because it connects the fact we have about West (motivation) to a new property (drive). Recognizing this conditional link lets us move forward in the argument.

<logic>driven(west) :- motivated(west)</logic>
</step>

The second rule, which makes driven individuals resilient, provides the final bridge to the desired conclusion. Without this rule, even if we derived that West is driven, we could not claim resilience.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The second rule says that any driven person is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(west) :- driven(west)</logic>
</step>

The statement that West is motivated gives us a concrete starting point. It supplies the antecedent needed for the first conditional rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>West is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(west)</logic>
</step>

Applying the first rule to the fact that West is motivated, we use modus ponens to infer that West is driven. This step translates the abstract rule into a specific fact about West.

Now that we have established West is driven, we can invoke the second rule. By again using modus ponens, we deduce that West is resilient, completing the chain of inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since West is driven and the rule linking drive to resilience holds, we infer that West is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(west)</logic>
</step>

Therefore West is resilient, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Ada is decisive, Alice is intuitive, and Sarah is logical. Whenever someone is decisive, that person is honest, so Ada’s decisiveness makes her honest. If a person is honest, then that person is articulate, which means Ada’s honesty guarantees her articulateness. Being articulate leads to being evidence driven, so any articulate individual becomes evidence driven; thus Ada’s articulateness would make her evidence driven. Conversely, if someone is anxious then they cannot be evidence driven, establishing a link from anxiety to the negation of evidence driven for Ada. Additionally, the forensic team knows that either Ada is anxious or Ada is calm; this disjunction captures the two possible emotional states for Ada. Other background rules include: if Alice were efficient she would be clever, if Sarah is logical then she is consistent, if Alice is intuitive then she is dedicated, and if Sarah is patient then she is diligent. The team also records that either Sarah is not consistent or Sarah is patient, and either Alice is not dedicated or Alice is efficient. No default rules apply in this scenario.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Ada is calm?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the statement that Ada is calm follows from the supplied forensic facts and rules. The goal is to trace a logical chain from known premises to the desired conclusion.

This premise connects honesty to articulateness for Ada, establishing a conditional that will be useful once we know Ada is honest. It sets up a bridge from a later derived fact to a subsequent one.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Ada is honest then Ada is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(ada) :- honest(ada)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a rule that turns articulateness into being evidence driven. It will allow us to move from Ada’s articulateness (once derived) to a property that later participates in a contradiction with anxiety.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Ada is articulate then Ada is evidence driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(ada) :- articulate(ada)</logic>
</step>

The rule linking decisiveness to honesty is crucial because we already know Ada is decisive. It will let us produce the honesty fact needed for the next steps.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Ada is decisive then Ada is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(ada) :- decisive(ada)</logic>
</step>

This conditional states that anxiety prevents evidence driven status. It will become the key to eliminate the possibility that Ada is anxious once we have evidence that she is evidence driven.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Ada is anxious then Ada is not evidence driven.</text>
<logic>~evidence_driven(ada) :- anxious(ada)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two exclusive possibilities for Ada’s emotional state: either she is anxious or she is calm. This will be the final piece that lets us infer calmness after we rule out anxiety.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>We are told that either Ada is anxious or Ada is calm.</text>
<logic>anxious(ada) v calm(ada)</logic>
</step>

Alice being intuitive is recorded but not directly needed for the calmness proof; it simply satisfies the requirement that all listed facts appear in the context.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Alice is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(alice)</logic>
</step>

Sarah’s logical status is another factual entry that must be mentioned in the context; it does not intervene in the current derivation.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Sarah is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(sarah)</logic>
</step>

Ada’s decisiveness is the factual anchor that triggers the chain of implications leading to honesty, articulateness, and evidence driven status.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Ada is decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(ada)</logic>
</step>

Since we have the fact that Ada is decisive (PREMISE_8) and the rule that decisiveness guarantees honesty (PREMISE_3), we can apply modus ponens to conclude that Ada is honest. This gives us the first needed intermediate property.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>From Ada being decisive and the rule that decisiveness implies honesty, we infer that Ada is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(ada)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Ada is honest from STEP_9, we can use the rule that honesty implies articulateness (PREMISE_1). By modus ponens again, we derive that Ada is articulate. This moves us one step closer to the evidence driven condition.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Using Ada’s honesty together with the rule that honesty implies articulateness, we derive that Ada is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(ada)</logic>
</step>

With Ada’s articulateness established, we invoke the rule that articulateness leads to being evidence driven (PREMISE_2). Applying modus ponens yields that Ada is evidence driven, providing the crucial positive fact that will clash with the anxiety rule.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>From Ada’s articulateness and the rule that articulateness leads to being evidence driven, we obtain that Ada is evidence driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(ada)</logic>
</step>

We have Ada being evidence driven (STEP_11) and the rule that anxiety forces the negation of evidence driven (PREMISE_4). Using modus tollens, if anxiety were true, Ada could not be evidence driven; since she is evidence driven, anxiety must be false. Hence we obtain ¬anxious(Ada).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>Because Ada is evidence driven and the rule that anxiety forces the opposite, we conclude that Ada cannot be anxious.</text>
<logic>~anxious(ada)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction from PREMISE_5 tells us that Ada is either anxious or calm. Since we have just shown that Ada cannot be anxious (STEP_12), the only remaining option is that Ada is calm. This disjunctive syllogism lets us conclude calm(Ada).
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,5">
<text>Given that Ada is not anxious and that either she is anxious or she is calm, we deduce that Ada must be calm.</text>
<logic>calm(ada)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Ada is calm, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The tomato_plant is drought_tolerant. Whenever a tomato_plant is pruned_regularly, it is also slow_growing. Any tomato_plant that is drought_tolerant is pruned_regularly. If a tomato_plant is thorny, then it cannot be slow_growing.
examples.question: Is the tomato_plant thorny?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the tomato_plant possesses thorns. The goal is to see if the property thorny can be supported by the given facts and rules.

Recognizing the conditional relationship between pruning and slow growth is important because it will allow us to propagate properties later. This premise tells us how a gardening practice translates into a growth characteristic.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the tomato_plant is pruned_regularly, then it is slow_growing.</text>
<logic>slow_growing(tomato_plant) :- pruned_regularly(tomato_plant)</logic>
</step>

The link from drought tolerance to regular pruning will let us move from a known fact to another useful attribute. Knowing that drought tolerance guarantees pruning is a key bridge in the chain of reasoning.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the tomato_plant is drought_tolerant, then it is pruned_regularly.</text>
<logic>pruned_regularly(tomato_plant) :- drought_tolerant(tomato_plant)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the tomato_plant is thorny, then it is not slow_growing.</text>
<logic>~slow_growing(tomato_plant) :- thorny(tomato_plant)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact tells us that the tomato_plant is drought_tolerant.</text>
<logic>drought_tolerant(tomato_plant)</logic>
</step>

Since we already know the tomato_plant is drought_tolerant, the rule from drought_tolerant to pruned_regularly lets us assert that the plant is indeed pruned_regularly. This step converts the initial factual information into a new attribute that can be used in the next rule.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From the fact that the tomato_plant is drought_tolerant and the rule linking drought_tolerant to pruned_regularly, we infer that the tomato_plant is pruned_regularly.</text>
<logic>pruned_regularly(tomato_plant)</logic>
</step>

With the plant now established as pruned_regularly, we can apply the rule that pruning leads to slow_growing. This gives us the slow_growing status, which is essential for evaluating the impact of thorns later on.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Using the newly obtained information that the tomato_plant is pruned_regularly together with the rule that pruned_regularly implies slow_growing, we conclude that the tomato_plant is slow_growing.</text>
<logic>slow_growing(tomato_plant)</logic>
</step>

We have the plant being slow_growing, but the rule says thorny would cause it not to be slow_growing. Because the plant is slow_growing, the antecedent 'thorny' must be false; otherwise we would have a conflict. Hence we infer that the plant is not thorny.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Because the tomato_plant is slow_growing and the rule says thorny would make it not slow_growing, we deduce that the tomato_plant cannot be thorny.</text>
<logic>~thorny(tomato_plant)</logic>
</step>

We derived Tomato_plant is not thorny, so the conclusion Tomato_plant is thorny is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Remy works industriously, and Carl is loyal. Whenever something is industrious, it is also resourceful, so Remy's industriousness guarantees his resourcefulness. If Carl behaves politely, then he becomes adaptable. Being loyal ensures that Carl is knowledgeable. Either Remy is not resourceful or Remy is talented. Either Remy is not talented or Remy is supportive. Also, either Carl is not knowledgeable or Carl is polite.
examples.question: Is Remy supportive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement supportive(remy) holds true. The goal is to trace the logical connections from the given facts and rules to reach a conclusion about Remy's supportiveness.

The rule linking industriousness to resourcefulness is crucial because it allows us to move from a known fact about Remy to a new property. Recognizing this implication sets up a chain that will later enable the use of the disjunction involving resourcefulness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Remy is industrious, then Remy is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(remy) :- industrious(remy)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "~resourceful(remy) v talented(remy)" tells us that at least one of the two literals must be true. This will become useful once we establish the truth of resourceful(remy), allowing us to eliminate the negative alternative and derive talent.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either Remy is not resourceful, or Remy is talented.</text>
<logic>~resourceful(remy) v talented(remy)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the clause "~talented(remy) v supportive(remy)" guarantees that either Remy lacks talent or he is supportive. Once we can confirm talent, the first option is ruled out, forcing the supportive conclusion.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Remy is not talented, or Remy is supportive.</text>
<logic>~talented(remy) v supportive(remy)</logic>
</step>

Carl being loyal is a standalone fact that does not directly affect Remy's supportiveness, but it satisfies the background knowledge of the scenario and mirrors the structure of the other premises.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Carl is loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(carl)</logic>
</step>

Remy's industriousness is the factual anchor for the rule in PREMISE_1. Knowing this fact enables us to apply the implication and obtain resourcefulness, which is needed for the subsequent disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Remy is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(remy)</logic>
</step>

From PREMISE_5 we have industrious(remy), and PREMISE_1 tells us that industrious implies resourceful. By applying modus ponens, we confidently assert resourceful(remy). This new fact will activate the first disjunction.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since Remy is industrious and industrious implies resourceful, we infer that Remy is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(remy)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have resourceful(remy), the disjunction in PREMISE_2 (¬resourceful ∨ talented) can no longer rely on the negative literal, because the positive literal is true. Disjunctive syllogism lets us drop ¬resourceful and conclude talented(remy). This talent predicate is the key to the next step.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given that Remy is resourceful and the disjunction that either not resourceful or talented holds, we conclude that Remy must be talented.</text>
<logic>talented(remy)</logic>
</step>

With talented(remy) established, the second disjunction in PREMISE_3 (¬talented ∨ supportive) loses its first option, as ¬talented is false. Applying disjunctive syllogism again forces the remaining literal, giving us supportive(remy).
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>With Remy being talented and the disjunction that either not talented or supportive, we infer that Remy is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(remy)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Remy is supportive, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

This rule provides a negative consequence: a balanced chisel cannot be fine_cut. Since we already have a fact that the chisel is fine_cut, this rule will help us see that the chisel cannot be balanced, which in turn influences the disjunction about the chisel being balanced or careful.

Knowing that the hammer is sharp activates PREMISE_2, allowing us to conclude that the hammer is hardworking. This derived fact will later interact with the disjunction concerning the hammer's industriousness and experience.

examples.context: Jack is supportive. Whenever something is supportive, it is also optimistic, so Jack being supportive guarantees that Jack is optimistic. Regarding Jack's traits, either Jack is not optimistic or Jack is sharp. Additionally, either Jack is not sharp or Jack is clever.
examples.question: Is it true that Jack is not clever?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to evaluate whether Jack is not clever. To answer this, we will use the given facts and logical relationships to see if we can establish Jack's cleverness.

The rule linking supportiveness to optimism is crucial because it lets us turn the known fact about Jack's support into a statement about his optimism. This connection will later enable us to use the disjunctive information involving optimism.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is supportive, then Jack is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(jack) :- supportive(jack)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction about optimism and sharpness tells us that at least one of those properties must hold for Jack. Once we know Jack's optimism, we can eliminate the negated alternative and deduce sharpness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either Jack is not optimistic or Jack is sharp.</text>
<logic>~optimistic(jack) v sharp(jack)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the disjunction concerning sharpness and cleverness ensures that either Jack lacks sharpness or he possesses cleverness. After we establish sharpness, the first option becomes impossible, forcing the second.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Jack is not sharp or Jack is clever.</text>
<logic>~sharp(jack) v clever(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Jack is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(jack)</logic>
</step>

Using Modus Ponens, we combine the fact that Jack is supportive with the rule that support implies optimism. Because the antecedent of the rule is satisfied, we can confidently assert that Jack is optimistic.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From Jack being supportive and the rule that support implies optimism, we infer that Jack is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(jack)</logic>
</step>

We now have Jack's optimism, which interacts with the disjunction "not optimistic or sharp". Since Jack is optimistic, the "not optimistic" side cannot be true, so the only way the disjunction holds is if Jack is sharp. This yields the conclusion that Jack is sharp.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since Jack is optimistic, the alternative that he is not optimistic cannot hold, so we conclude that Jack is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(jack)</logic>
</step>

With Jack identified as sharp, we turn to the second disjunction "not sharp or clever". The "not sharp" option is ruled out by our previous finding, leaving the clever alternative as the only viable truth. Hence, Jack must be clever.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Because Jack is sharp, the possibility that he is not sharp is ruled out, leaving that Jack is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(jack)</logic>
</step>

We derived Jack is clever, so the conclusion Jack is not clever is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the ballet studio, Sage is known to be proficient. Whenever someone is ambitious, that person is also fit. If a dancer is proficient, then the dancer is supportive. Any dancer who is not adaptable is automatically talented. Conversely, if a dancer is adaptable, then that dancer cannot be fit. Finally, either Sage is not supportive or Sage is ambitious.
examples.question: Is Sage talented?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Sage possesses the talent property. To answer, we will chain together the given facts, rules, and the disjunction until we can derive the desired conclusion.

This premise tells us that ambition guarantees fitness. It will become useful later once we establish that Sage is ambitious, allowing us to infer that Sage is fit.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any ambitious person is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(sage) :- ambitious(sage)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that proficiency guarantees supportiveness. Since we already know Sage is proficient, this rule will let us obtain support for Sage, which is needed for the disjunction reasoning.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any proficient person is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(sage) :- proficient(sage)</logic>
</step>

This rule links the lack of adaptability directly to talent. Our ultimate goal is to show Sage is talented, so if we can prove Sage is not adaptable, this premise will give us the final step.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that a non‑adaptable person is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(sage) :- ~adaptable(sage)</logic>
</step>

This conditional says that being adaptable forces a person to be not fit. It will be used in a contrapositive fashion (modus tollens) after we have established Sage is fit, enabling us to conclude Sage is not adaptable.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that an adaptable person is not fit.</text>
<logic>~fit(sage) :- adaptable(sage)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides an either‑or statement about Sage: either Sage is not supportive or Sage is ambitious. Once we have one of these components, we can resolve the disjunction and obtain the other.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating that either Sage is not supportive or Sage is ambitious.</text>
<logic>~supportive(sage) v ambitious(sage)</logic>
</step>

The fact states directly that Sage is proficient. This concrete piece of information will activate Premise 2, giving us Sage's supportive status.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Sage is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(sage)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Sage is proficient together with the rule that proficiency implies support, we can assert that Sage is supportive. This gives us a concrete truth about Sage's supportiveness, which we will need to handle the disjunction.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Sage being proficient and the rule that proficiency implies support, we infer Sage is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(sage)</logic>
</step>

We now have Sage supportive, and the disjunction says "not supportive or ambitious". Since the first option is false (Sage is supportive), the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second option, Sage ambitious, to be true. Thus we deduce Sage is ambitious.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Because we have Sage supportive and the disjunction that either Sage is not supportive or Sage is ambitious, we conclude Sage is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(sage)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Sage is ambitious, we can apply the rule that ambition leads to fitness. Therefore Sage must be fit, giving us a concrete fact about Sage's physical condition.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Since Sage is ambitious and ambition leads to being fit, we deduce Sage is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(sage)</logic>
</step>

We know Sage is fit, and the rule "if adaptable then not fit" tells us that any adaptable person cannot be fit. By modus tollens, because Sage is fit, Sage cannot be adaptable. Hence we conclude Sage is not adaptable.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Given Sage is fit and that adaptability would make someone not fit, we infer Sage is not adaptable.</text>
<logic>~adaptable(sage)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have shown Sage is not adaptable, we can invoke the rule that non‑adaptable individuals are talented. This directly yields the conclusion that Sage is talented.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>From Sage not being adaptable and the rule that non‑adaptable implies talented, we infer Sage is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(sage)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sage is talented, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the game, the non‑player character NPC Briar is known for being generous. Any character who is generous is also capable, so Briar's generosity makes him capable. Whenever a character is capable, that character is permitted to act, which means Briar's capability grants him permission. If Briar is permitted and there is no abnormality called ab_methodical preventing methodical behavior, then Briar is considered methodical. The default rule applies only when the abnormality ab_methodical is absent.
examples.question: Is NPC Briar thorough?
examples.completion:

The default rule provides a way to infer methodical behavior: if Briar is permitted and there is no abnormality labeled ab_methodical, then he is methodical. This rule only applies in the absence of that specific abnormality.

The fact supplied explicitly says that Briar is generous. This concrete piece of information will trigger the first rule and start the chain of inferences.
<step n="4" type="premise">

examples.context: In the research group, Alan, Freya and Wren are all known to be compassionate, while Hayden’s compassion is not mentioned directly. Alan, Freya, Wren and also Hayden are described as humble individuals. Whenever someone is humble, they are also articulate, so humble Hayden guarantees articulate Hayden. If a person is trustworthy, then that person is logical; thus trustworthy Hayden would make Hayden logical. Compassionate people are trustworthy, which means that if Hayden were compassionate, he would be trustworthy as well. Being brave forces a person not to be articulate, so brave Hayden would imply not articulate Hayden. Finally, it is known that Hayden is either brave or compassionate (or possibly both).
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Hayden is logical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the group member Hayden can be classified as logical using the supplied facts and rules. The goal is to trace a chain of implications from known properties of Hayden to the logical predicate.

This premise introduces the connection between humility and articulateness, which will be useful because we already know Hayden is humble. Recognizing that humble Hayden leads to articulate Hayden sets up later contradictions with other rules.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if someone is humble, then they are articulate; applied to Hayden, humble Hayden implies articulate Hayden.</text>
<logic>articulate(hayden) :- humble(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Here we are told that trustworthiness guarantees logical reasoning. This rule will be the final link in our chain, but we first must establish that Hayden is trustworthy.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if someone is trustworthy, then they are logical; applied to Hayden, trustworthy Hayden implies logical Hayden.</text>
<logic>logical(hayden) :- trustworthy(hayden)</logic>
</step>

This rule provides a way to obtain trustworthiness from compassion. If we can show Hayden is compassionate, we will immediately get trustworthiness, moving us toward the logical conclusion.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if someone is compassionate, then they are trustworthy; applied to Hayden, compassionate Hayden implies trustworthy Hayden.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(hayden) :- compassionate(hayden)</logic>
</step>

The rule states that bravery prevents articulateness. It will later allow us to eliminate bravery as a possibility for Hayden once we have both articulate and not‑articulate conditions.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if someone is brave, then they are not articulate; applied to Hayden, brave Hayden implies not articulate Hayden.</text>
<logic>~articulate(hayden) :- brave(hayden)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that at least one of bravery or compassion holds for Hayden. This binary choice will become decisive once we can rule out one of the alternatives.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Hayden is either brave or compassionate (or both).</text>
<logic>brave(hayden) v compassionate(hayden)</logic>
</step>

We now have articulate Hayden, and the rule that bravery forces a person to be not articulate. If Hayden were brave, he would have to be not articulate, contradicting the previously derived articulateness. To avoid inconsistency, we conclude that Hayden cannot be brave, i.e., ~brave Hayden holds.

examples.context: In the textile mill, the loom operator named Carl has just received a thread break alert. Whenever a loom operator gets a thread break alert, they become highly productive. Conversely, if a loom operator holds a positive market forecast, then they are not highly productive. No other information is given.
examples.question: Does the loom operator have a positive market forecast?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the loom operator possesses a positive market forecast. The goal is to see if the property positive_market_forecast applies to the loom operator based on the information provided.

The first rule tells us that a thread break alert guarantees high productivity. This connection is important because it links the known alert fact to a new attribute we can use later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the loom operator receives a thread break alert, then they become highly productive.</text>
<logic>high_productivity(loom_operator) :- thread_break_alert(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

The second rule provides a contrapositive relationship: holding a positive market forecast forces the loom operator to lack high productivity. This will allow us to reason about the forecast once we know the productivity status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the loom operator holds a positive market forecast, then they are not highly productive.</text>
<logic>~high_productivity(loom_operator) :- positive_market_forecast(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the loom operator has received a thread break alert gives us a concrete starting point. It supplies the antecedent needed to activate the first rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The loom operator has received a thread break alert.</text>
<logic>thread_break_alert(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

By applying modus ponens to the alert fact and the first rule, we can conclude that the loom operator is highly productive. The alert satisfies the condition of the rule, so the consequent follows directly.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From the alert and the first rule, we infer that the loom operator is highly productive.</text>
<logic>high_productivity(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the loom operator is highly productive, we can use the second rule in a modus tollens fashion: because high productivity is true, the antecedent of the second rule (positive market forecast) must be false, leading to the conclusion that the loom operator does not have a positive market forecast.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Given that the loom operator is highly productive and the second rule, we conclude that the loom operator is not holding a positive market forecast.</text>
<logic>~positive_market_forecast(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

We derived Loom_operator is not positive market forecast, so the conclusion Loom_operator is positive market forecast is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Tanya is known to be systematic. Whenever someone is diplomatic, they are also relaxed, so any diplomatic person is relaxed. If a person is cautious, then they are strong; being cautious guarantees strength. Anxious people can never be strong, meaning that if someone is anxious, they are not strong. Enthusiastic individuals are never systematic, so enthusiasm rules out being systematic. Either Tanya is anxious or she is diplomatic; one of those two must hold. Likewise, either Tanya is enthusiastic or she is cautious; at least one of those alternatives is true.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude that Tanya is relaxed?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the available facts and rules force Tanya to be relaxed. The goal is to chain the conditional statements and disjunctions until we can apply the diplomatic‑to‑relaxed rule.

This premise links diplomacy to relaxation, so if we can later establish that Tanya is diplomatic, we will immediately get the desired relaxed property. It sets up the final step of the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Tanya is diplomatic, then she must be relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(tanya) :- diplomatic(tanya)</logic>
</step>

The cautious‑to‑strong rule will become useful once we know Tanya is cautious; it will allow us to produce a strong predicate that later interacts with the anxiety rule. It is a bridge between caution and strength.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Tanya is cautious, then she becomes strong.</text>
<logic>strong(tanya) :- cautious(tanya)</logic>
</step>

The anxious‑to‑not‑strong rule gives a way to rule out anxiety once we have a strong fact. It will let us invert the direction: from strong we can infer not anxious, which is needed for the diplomatic disjunction.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule indicates that if Tanya is anxious, she cannot be strong.</text>
<logic>~strong(tanya) :- anxious(tanya)</logic>
</step>

The enthusiastic‑to‑not‑systematic rule connects enthusiasm with the systematic fact we already possess. By showing that systematic holds, we can use modus tollens to eliminate enthusiasm, which will later help resolve a disjunction.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule tells us that if Tanya is enthusiastic, she cannot be systematic.</text>
<logic>~systematic(tanya) :- enthusiastic(tanya)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction tells us that at least one of anxious or diplomatic is true for Tanya. This will be crucial after we eliminate anxiety, because it will force the diplomatic alternative to be true.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>We are told that either Tanya is anxious or she is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>anxious(tanya) v diplomatic(tanya)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction offers a choice between enthusiasm and caution. Once we rule out enthusiasm, this disjunction will compel caution, opening the path to strength.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>We also know that either Tanya is enthusiastic or she is cautious.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(tanya) v cautious(tanya)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Tanya is systematic is the starting point for applying the enthusiastic‑to‑not‑systematic rule. It gives us a concrete truth to work with in the early part of the proof.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Tanya is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(tanya)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Tanya is systematic, the rule that enthusiastic people are not systematic cannot be satisfied if she were enthusiastic. Using modus tollens, we infer that she must not be enthusiastic. This negation is essential for resolving the second disjunction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>From Tanya being systematic and the rule that enthusiasm would make her not systematic, we infer that Tanya is not enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>~enthusiastic(tanya)</logic>
</step>

We now have ~enthusiastic(tanya) together with the disjunction "enthusiastic or cautious". Since the enthusiastic alternative is false, the only way the disjunction can hold is if Tanya is cautious. Thus we derive cautious(tanya).
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>Since Tanya is not enthusiastic and we have the alternative that she is either enthusiastic or cautious, we conclude that she must be cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(tanya)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Tanya is cautious, we can invoke the cautious‑to‑strong rule. Modus ponens lets us conclude that she is strong, giving us a positive fact that will later help eliminate anxiety.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Because Tanya is cautious and caution implies strength, we derive that Tanya is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(tanya)</logic>
</step>

With strong(tanya) in hand, we turn to the rule that anxiety prevents strength. If Tanya were anxious, she would not be strong, but we have strong; therefore, she cannot be anxious. This yields ~anxious(tanya).
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Tanya is strong, and the rule that anxiety prevents strength lets us infer that she cannot be anxious.</text>
<logic>~anxious(tanya)</logic>
</step>

Now we have ~anxious(tanya) and the disjunction "anxious or diplomatic". Since the anxious option is false, the disjunction forces the diplomatic alternative to be true, so we infer diplomatic(tanya).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Given that Tanya is not anxious and the disjunction that she is either anxious or diplomatic, we deduce that she is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(tanya)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the diplomatic‑to‑relaxed rule tells us that any diplomatic person is relaxed. Applying modus ponens to diplomatic(tanya) gives us relaxed(tanya), achieving the target conclusion.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>With Tanya being diplomatic and the diplomatic‑to‑relaxed rule, we finally obtain that Tanya is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(tanya)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Tanya is relaxed, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In this musical world, the melody is known to be tonic. Whenever a melody is not harmonic, it is considered theoretical. If a melody happens to be harmonic, then it cannot be legato. Moreover, any melody that is structured would not be theoretical. Regarding possibilities, either the melody is not modulatory or it is legato, and also either the melody is not tonic or it is modulatory. These statements together capture all the given facts, rules, and disjunctions.
examples.question: Is the melody structured?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the melody possesses the property of being structured. To answer, we will chain together the given facts, conditional rules, and disjunctions step by step.

The first rule tells us that lacking harmonic quality guarantees theoretical status. This will become useful later when we establish the melody is not harmonic.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any melody that is not harmonic is theoretical.</text>
<logic>theoretical(melody) :- ~harmonic(melody)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links harmonicness to the absence of legato. Knowing whether the melody is legato will allow us to infer something about its harmonic nature.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a melody is harmonic, then it is not legato.</text>
<logic>~legato(melody) :- harmonic(melody)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that if something is structured, it cannot be theoretical. This contrapositive relationship will let us turn a theoretical conclusion into a negation of structuredness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a melody is structured, then it is not theoretical.</text>
<logic>~theoretical(melody) :- structured(melody)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers a choice between the melody not being modulatory or being legato. Once we know one side, we can resolve the other.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the melody is not modulatory or it is legato.</text>
<logic>~modulatory(melody) v legato(melody)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction provides a choice between the melody not being tonic or being modulatory. Combined with the fact that the melody is tonic, this will force the modulatory alternative.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either the melody is not tonic or it is modulatory.</text>
<logic>~tonic(melody) v modulatory(melody)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told the melody is tonic, which grounds the reasoning for the disjunction in the previous premise.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The melody is tonic.</text>
<logic>tonic(melody)</logic>
</step>

Because the melody is tonic, the clause "not tonic or modulatory" can only be satisfied by the modulatory option. Hence we deduce the melody is modulatory. This resolves the first disjunction and sets up the next inference.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,5">
<text>From the melody being tonic and the alternative "not tonic or modulatory," we infer that the melody is modulatory.</text>
<logic>modulatory(melody)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have modulatory, the clause "not modulatory or legato" must be satisfied by the legato alternative. Therefore the melody is legato. This gives us the positive literal needed for the harmonic rule.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given the melody is modulatory and the choice "not modulatory or legato," we deduce that the melody is legato.</text>
<logic>legato(melody)</logic>
</step>

We have legato, and the rule says any harmonic melody would be not legato. Since the melody is legato, the antecedent of that rule cannot hold, so the melody cannot be harmonic. Thus we obtain not harmonic.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since the melody is legato and any harmonic melody would be not legato, we conclude the melody is not harmonic.</text>
<logic>~harmonic(melody)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the melody is not harmonic, we can apply the first rule which states that lack of harmonicness implies theoretical. Consequently the melody is theoretical.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Because the melody is not harmonic, the earlier rule gives that it is theoretical.</text>
<logic>theoretical(melody)</logic>
</step>

The melody is now known to be theoretical, and the third rule tells us a structured melody would never be theoretical. By contraposition, if something is theoretical, it cannot be structured. Therefore we infer the melody is not structured.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Because the melody is theoretical and a structured melody would not be theoretical, we infer the melody is not structured.</text>
<logic>~structured(melody)</logic>
</step>

We derived Melody is not structured, so the conclusion Melody is structured is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the apiary, bee_henry has been designated as an assistant-promoted bee. Bee_keith holds a certification. Both bee_henry and bee_keith are known to be hardworking. By default, any hardworking bee that is not already assistant-promoted is considered promoted.

The default rule is crucial because it tells us how a promotion can be derived without explicit facts. It links the properties of being hardworking and not being assistant-promoted to the conclusion of being promoted.

<text>The default rule states that any hardworking bee that is not assistant-promoted will be promoted.</text>
<logic>promoted(bee_keith) :- hardworking(bee_keith), not assistant_promoted(bee_keith)</logic>

The fact that bee_henry is assistant-promoted establishes the existence of the assistant-promoted category, but it does not directly affect bee_keith's promotion. It also helps us understand the condition of the default rule.

Bee_keith being certified is an additional attribute of the bee, though it does not play a role in the promotion rule. It is recorded to reflect the complete set of facts given.

Knowing that bee_henry is hardworking confirms that the hardworking property can apply to multiple bees. This reinforces the relevance of the hardworking condition in the default rule.

Bee_keith is identified as hardworking, which satisfies one half of the default rule's antecedent. This fact is essential for later applying the rule to infer promotion.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Bee_keith is hardworking.</text>
<logic>hardworking(bee_keith)</logic>
</step>

Because bee_keith satisfies the hardworking condition and we have no evidence that bee_keith is assistant-promoted, the default rule activates, allowing us to conclude that bee_keith is promoted. This step uses default reasoning to fill the gap left by missing explicit information.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since bee_keith is hardworking and we have no information that bee_keith is assistant-promoted, the default rule lets us infer that bee_keith is promoted.</text>
<logic>promoted(bee_keith)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Bee_keith is promoted, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The third rule states that patience implies systematic behavior. This provides a way to propagate the properties forward, eventually reaching a contradiction that will help us eliminate one alternative in the disjunction.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If something is patient, then it is systematic; consequently Shay's patience makes Shay systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(shay) :- patient(shay)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that if Shay were precise, Shay could not be systematic. This rule sets up a potential conflict with the systematic property we will derive, allowing us to infer the negation of precision later on.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If something is precise, then it is not systematic; so a precise Shay would not be systematic.</text>
<logic>~systematic(shay) :- precise(shay)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities: Shay is precise or Shay is strong. Knowing that at least one of these must hold creates a logical fork that we can resolve once we determine whether precision is viable.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Shay is precise or Shay is strong; at least one of these statements must be true.</text>
<logic>precise(shay) v strong(shay)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Shay is calm is the starting point of the whole argument. It provides the initial truth that activates the first conditional rule about generosity.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Shay is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(shay)</logic>
</step>

Applying the calm‑to‑generous rule to the fact that Shay is calm yields generosity for Shay. This step directly uses Modus Ponens, turning the abstract conditional into a concrete attribute of Shay.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From Shay being calm and the rule that calm implies generous, we infer that Shay is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(shay)</logic>
</step>

With Shay now known to be generous, we invoke the generous‑to‑patient rule, again via Modus Ponens, to conclude that Shay is patient. This extends the chain of inferred properties further.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Using Shay's generosity together with the rule that generosity leads to patience, we conclude that Shay is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(shay)</logic>
</step>

Having established patience, we apply the patient‑to‑systematic rule, which gives us that Shay is systematic. This systematic status will later clash with the possibility of precision.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Given Shay's patience and the rule that patient leads to systematic, we deduce that Shay is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(shay)</logic>
</step>

We now have Shay systematic, but the rule about precision says a precise Shay would not be systematic. Since Shay is systematic, the only way to avoid contradiction is to assert that Shay is not precise. This is a classic use of Modus Tollens.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since Shay is systematic and the rule that being precise would make Shay not systematic, we infer that Shay cannot be precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(shay)</logic>
</step>

We know Shay is not precise and also that either Shay is precise or Shay is strong. By eliminating the precise alternative, the disjunction forces the remaining option: Shay must be strong. This step uses Disjunctive Syllogism to reach the desired conclusion.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>With the knowledge that Shay is not precise and the statement that either Shay is precise or strong, we determine that Shay must be strong.</text>
<logic>strong(shay)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Shay is strong, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True
