examples.question: Is Jamie not methodical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Jamie lacks the property of being methodical. The goal is to see if the information we have forces Jamie to be methodical, which would make the negation false. To answer, we will follow the chain of implications starting from the known fact that Jamie is decisive.

The first premise gives us a conditional link: decisiveness leads to determination. This will let us move from the known fact about Jamie to a new property, setting up the chain of reasoning.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any decisive individual is also determined.</text>
<logic>determined(jamie) :- decisive(jamie)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that being determined guarantees persistence. Once we have determined that Jamie is determined, we can immediately infer persistence, advancing our proof.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any determined individual is also persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(jamie) :- determined(jamie)</logic>
</step>

The third premise connects persistence to wittiness. This bridge will allow us to later interact with the rule about resilience and non‑wittiness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any persistent individual is also witty.</text>
<logic>witty(jamie) :- persistent(jamie)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise provides a contrapositive relationship: if someone is resilient, they cannot be witty. This will become useful after we establish Jamie's wittiness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if someone is resilient then they are not witty.</text>
<logic>~witty(jamie) :- resilient(jamie)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of the two properties—resilient or methodical—must hold for Jamie. This will be crucial for the final step where we eliminate one alternative.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating that Jamie is either resilient or methodical.</text>
<logic>resilient(jamie) v methodical(jamie)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Jamie is decisive, which is the starting fact for the chain of deductions.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Jamie is decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens to the fact that Jamie is decisive and the rule that decisiveness implies determination, we conclude that Jamie is determined. This gives us a new concrete property to work with.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From Jamie being decisive and the first rule we infer that Jamie is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Jamie's determination, we use the second rule (determination implies persistence) via modus ponens again, yielding Jamie's persistence. This continues the forward chaining process.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Jamie being determined and the second rule we infer that Jamie is persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(jamie)</logic>
</step>

With Jamie's persistence established, the third rule (persistence implies wittiness) lets us infer that Jamie is witty. At this point we have both wittiness and a rule that connects resilience to non‑wittiness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>From Jamie being persistent and the third rule we infer that Jamie is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(jamie)</logic>
</step>

We have Jamie being witty, but the fourth rule says that if Jamie were resilient, Jamie would not be witty. Since Jamie is witty, the antecedent (resilience) must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Hence we derive that Jamie is not resilient.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Using Jamie's wittiness together with the fourth rule, we conclude that Jamie cannot be resilient.</text>
<logic>~resilient(jamie)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that either resilience or methodicality holds for Jamie. Because we have just shown Jamie is not resilient, the only remaining way for the disjunction to be satisfied is that Jamie is methodical. Thus we deduce Jamie's methodical property.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Since Jamie is not resilient and the disjunction tells us one of resilient or methodical must hold, we deduce that Jamie is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(jamie)</logic>
</step>

We derived Jamie is methodical, so the conclusion Jamie is not methodical is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is the construction site not environmentally_friendly?

<logic>environmentally_friendly(construction_site) :- well_designed(construction_site)</logic>

<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>material_ready(construction_site) :- risk_aware(construction_site)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the material_ready is not true, or the site is well_designed.</text>
<logic>~material_ready(construction_site) v well_designed(construction_site)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The construction site is risk_aware.</text>
<logic>risk_aware(construction_site)</logic>
</step>

Starting from the fact that the site is risk_aware and using the rule that risk_aware guarantees material_ready, we can directly assert that the material is indeed ready. This is a straightforward application of the conditional relationship.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since the site is risk_aware and risk_aware guarantees material_ready, we conclude the material is ready.</text>
<logic>material_ready(construction_site)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have material_ready, we look at the disjunction stating that either material_ready is false or the site is well_designed. Since material_ready is true, the first alternative of the disjunction is ruled out, forcing the second alternative to hold, namely that the site is well_designed.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Given that the material is ready and we have the disjunction that either the material is not ready or the site is well_designed, we infer the site must be well_designed.</text>

With the site established as well_designed, we can invoke the earlier rule that any well_designed site is environmentally_friendly. This yields the conclusion that the site is environmentally_friendly, directly contradicting the query's negation.

We derived Construction_site is environmentally friendly, so the conclusion Construction_site is not environmentally friendly is False.

Answer: False

examples.context: Skyler is content. Hollis is energetic. Whenever someone is brave, that person is also decisive, so if Hollis is brave then Hollis is decisive. Whenever someone is clever, that person is not energetic, so if Hollis is clever then Hollis is not energetic. Either Hollis is clever or Hollis is brave; at least one of those holds. Any entity that is content is perceptive, which means Skyler being content makes Skyler perceptive. Any entity that is strong is productive, so if Skyler were strong she would be productive. Finally, either Skyler is not perceptive or Skyler is strong; at least one of those alternatives is true.
examples.question: Is it true that Hollis is not decisive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Hollis lacks the property of being decisive. The goal is to see if the negation of decisive can be supported from the given information. To do this we will work through the rules, facts, and the disjunction involving Hollis's bravery and cleverness.

The conditional linking bravery to decisiveness is important because if we can establish that Hollis is brave, the rule will let us infer decisiveness directly. This premise sets up a forward path from bravery to the target property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Hollis is brave then Hollis is decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(hollis) :- brave(hollis)</logic>
</step>

The rule that cleverness forces non‑energetic status will later help us eliminate the possibility that Hollis is clever, since we already know Hollis is energetic. It creates a conflict that we can exploit.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Hollis is clever then Hollis is not energetic.</text>
<logic>~energetic(hollis) :- clever(hollis)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us the only two ways Hollis could satisfy the earlier rule: either through cleverness or bravery. By narrowing down which of these actually holds, we can trigger the appropriate conditional.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction tells us that Hollis is either clever or brave (or both).</text>
<logic>clever(hollis) v brave(hollis)</logic>
</step>

Stating that Skyler is content is required for completeness of the context, though it does not directly affect the chain concerning Hollis. It also enables the rule that content implies perceptive, which is part of the overall knowledge base.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Skyler is content.</text>
<logic>content(skyler)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Hollis is energetic is the cornerstone for disproving cleverness, because the clever‑to‑not‑energetic rule says a clever person cannot be energetic. This fact will clash with that rule if we assume cleverness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Hollis is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(hollis)</logic>
</step>

Because we have Hollis energetic and the rule that cleverness would make him not energetic, the only way both could be true is if Hollis is not clever. Thus we derive the negation of cleverness. This step removes one side of the disjunction.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From the fact that Hollis is energetic and the rule that clever implies not energetic, we infer that Hollis cannot be clever.</text>
<logic>~clever(hollis)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that Hollis cannot be clever, the remaining option in the disjunction must be true: Hollis is brave. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism, where eliminating one disjunct forces acceptance of the other.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given that Hollis is not clever and the disjunction that Hollis is clever or brave, we conclude that Hollis must be brave.</text>
<logic>brave(hollis)</logic>
</step>

With the established fact that Hollis is brave, we can now apply the brave‑to‑decisive rule. The rule tells us that bravery guarantees decisiveness, so we conclude Hollis is decisive.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Hollis is brave and the rule that brave leads to decisive, we derive that Hollis is decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(hollis)</logic>
</step>

We derived Hollis is decisive, so the conclusion Hollis is not decisive is False.
</think>
Answer: False

Any time the spindle is running, it becomes soft to the touch. If the spindle happens to be well-worn, then it is not soft. No other facts or rules are present.
examples.question: Is the spindle not well-worn?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the spindle is not well-worn. The goal is to use the supplied facts and conditional statements to reach a conclusion about the spindle's well-worn status.

The first rule tells us that brightness of the spindle guarantees it is running. This connection will allow us to move from a fact about brightness to a fact about running later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if the spindle is bright then it is running.</text>
<logic>running(spindle) :- bright(spindle)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links running to softness, stating that any running spindle becomes soft. This will be crucial once we have established that the spindle is running.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if the spindle is running then it is soft.</text>
<logic>soft(spindle) :- running(spindle)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a negative relationship: if the spindle were well-worn, it could not be soft. This contrapositive relationship will let us infer the opposite of well‑worn when we know the spindle is soft.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if the spindle is well-worn then it is not soft.</text>
<logic>~soft(spindle) :- well_worn(spindle)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the loom is bright is mentioned in the context but does not directly affect the spindle. It is recorded here for completeness of the premises.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The loom is bright.</text>
<logic>bright(loom)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the bobbin being bright is a factual detail that does not participate in the chain of reasoning about the spindle. It is still part of the overall knowledge base.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The bobbin is bright.</text>
<logic>bright(bobbin)</logic>
</step>

The spindle being bright is a key fact because it activates the first rule. Knowing the spindle is bright gives us the starting point for deriving its running state.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The spindle is bright.</text>
<logic>bright(spindle)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the spindle is bright together with the rule that bright implies running, we can directly infer that the spindle is running. This is a straightforward application of the conditional relationship.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From the spindle being bright and the rule linking brightness to running, we infer that the spindle is running.</text>
<logic>running(spindle)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the spindle is running, the second rule allows us to conclude that the spindle is soft. The chain from brightness to running to softness is now complete.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Given the spindle is running and the rule that running implies softness, we conclude the spindle is soft.</text>
<logic>soft(spindle)</logic>
</step>

We have the spindle being soft, and the third rule tells us that a well‑worn spindle would be not soft. By applying modus tollens—if P implies not Q and Q holds, then P must be false—we deduce that the spindle cannot be well‑worn, i.e., it is not well‑worn.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since the spindle is soft and the rule that a well-worn spindle would be not soft, we apply modus tollens to infer that the spindle is not well-worn.</text>
<logic>~well_worn(spindle)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Spindle is not well worn, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Can we conclude that Tom is not well worn?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Tom can be shown to be not well worn. The goal is to derive the negation of the well‑worn predicate using the information given about his design, vision, and related properties. All the facts and conditional statements in the context will be used step by step.

The first rule tells us that bright vision guarantees glaze expertise. This will be useful later when we obtain bright vision for Tom, allowing us to move forward to the glaze‑expert property.

The third rule says that any glaze expert honors tradition. This creates a chain from bright vision all the way to honoring tradition, which is essential for the final contradiction involving well‑worn status.

<logic>honors_tradition(tom) :- glaze_expert(tom)</logic>

<logic>~honors_tradition(tom) :- well_worn(tom)</logic>

The sixth rule says that fine detail would make the process not ordered. This creates a potential conflict with the ordered process we will obtain, enabling us to eliminate the fine‑detail alternative later.

The disjunction tells us that Tom either possesses fine detail or a firm hand. This exclusive choice will be resolved after we rule out fine detail, leaving the firm‑hand option as the only possibility.

The fact that Tom has a balanced design is our starting point for the chain of deductions. It directly activates the fourth rule, giving us an ordered process right away.

</step>

Given that the project is deadline driven, the rule "if flexible then not deadline driven" would be violated if the project were flexible. To avoid this contradiction, we must conclude the project is not flexible in its design. This is a classic use of modus tollens: the antecedent must be false because the consequent is false while the overall implication holds.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Because the project is deadline driven and a flexible design would make it not deadline driven, we infer that the project is not flexible in its design.</text>
<logic>~flexible_design(bridge_project)</logic>
</step>

Now we know the project is not flexible, and the earlier disjunction says it is either flexible or well planned. Since the flexible option is ruled out, the only remaining possibility is that the project is well planned. This follows the pattern of disjunctive syllogism, where eliminating one disjunct forces acceptance of the other.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since the project is not flexible and it must be either flexible or well planned, we conclude that the project is well planned.</text>
<logic>well_planned(bridge_project)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Bridge_project is well planned, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Derek is cooperative. Whenever Derek is cooperative, he is cautious. If Derek is cautious, then Derek is active. Either Derek is not active or Derek is persistent. If Derek is persistent, then Derek is precise. If Derek is strategic, then Derek is not precise.
examples.question: Is Derek strategic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Derek can be considered strategic given a set of relationships about his behavior. The goal is to see if the assumption that Derek is strategic creates any conflict with the other information provided.

The rule linking cautiousness to activity will become important later, because once we know Derek is cautious we can immediately claim he is active. This connection helps us move from a property we can derive to another that participates in a disjunction.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Derek is cautious, then he is active.</text>
<logic>active(derek) :- cautious(derek)</logic>
</step>

The cooperative-to-cautious rule allows us to turn the known fact that Derek is cooperative into the new fact that he is cautious. Without this bridge we could not reach the active condition needed for later steps.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Derek is cooperative, then he is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(derek) :- cooperative(derek)</logic>
</step>

The persistent-to-precise rule tells us that any time Derek is persistent, he must also be precise. This will be used after we establish persistence, turning it into a precise status that later conflicts with the strategic assumption.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Derek is persistent, then he is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(derek) :- persistent(derek)</logic>
</step>

The strategic-to-not-precise rule creates a direct conflict: if Derek were strategic, he could not be precise. This conditional will be essential for applying modus tollens once we have shown Derek is precise.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Derek is strategic, then he is not precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(derek) :- strategic(derek)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "not active or persistent" gives us a way to infer persistence if we can confirm that Derek is indeed active. It sets up a choice that will be resolved by the truth of the active predicate.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Derek is not active or Derek is persistent.</text>
<logic>~active(derek) v persistent(derek)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Derek is cooperative is the starting point of the chain. It is the only concrete assertion about Derek, and it triggers the cascade of implications that follow.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Derek is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(derek)</logic>
</step>

Using modus ponens, we combine the fact that Derek is cooperative with the rule that cooperative implies cautious. Because the antecedent (cooperative) holds, we can safely conclude that Derek is cautious. This new fact moves us one step closer to activating the next rule.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Derek being cooperative we infer that Derek is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(derek)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Derek is cautious, we apply the rule that cautiousness leads to activity. Again, modus ponens lets us infer that Derek is active, because the condition for activity is satisfied.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Derek being cautious we infer that Derek is active.</text>
<logic>active(derek)</logic>
</step>

We know Derek is active, and we also have the disjunction "not active or persistent". Since the first part of the disjunction is false (Derek is active), the only way for the statement to remain true is for the second part, persistence, to hold. Thus we deduce that Derek is persistent by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Since Derek is active, the disjunction tells us that Derek must be persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(derek)</logic>
</step>

With Derek's persistence confirmed, we turn to the rule that persistence implies precision. By modus ponens, this yields that Derek is precise. This precise status is crucial for the final contradiction.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From Derek being persistent we infer that Derek is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(derek)</logic>
</step>

We now have Derek being precise, but the strategic rule tells us that a strategic Derek would be not precise. Applying modus tollens, the truth of the consequent's negation (precise) forces the negation of the antecedent, so Derek cannot be strategic. Hence we derive that Derek is not strategic.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Because Derek is precise, and if he were strategic he would be not precise, we conclude Derek is not strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(derek)</logic>
</step>

We derived Derek is not strategic, so the conclusion Derek is strategic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the veterinary records, the animal named fern is noted as analytical. The animal named scout is also recorded as analytical. Both fern and scout are described as proactive. Moreover, the animal rain is listed as proactive. A specific rule in the clinic states that whenever rain is proactive, rain is also analytical. Another rule says that if rain is analytical, then rain is intuitive. No other facts or rules are provided.
examples.question: Is rain not intuitive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a set of facts about three animals and two conditional rules that connect the properties proactive, analytical, and intuitive. Our goal is to determine whether the statement that rain is not intuitive can be justified from the information provided.

This rule tells us how the property proactive for rain leads directly to the property analytical for rain. It will be essential later when we try to turn the known fact that rain is proactive into an analytical statement.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if rain is proactive then rain is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(rain) :- proactive(rain)</logic>
</step>

The second rule establishes a bridge from analytical to intuitive, but only for rain. Once we have shown that rain is analytical, this rule will allow us to deduce that rain is also intuitive.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if rain is analytical then rain is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(rain) :- analytical(rain)</logic>
</step>

The fact that fern is analytical is part of the background knowledge about the animals. Although it does not involve rain, it illustrates that the analytical predicate is used in the domain.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Fern is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(fern)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, scout being analytical adds another example of an animal with that property. These examples help confirm that the analytical predicate is meaningful in the context.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Scout is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(scout)</logic>
</step>

Fern being proactive provides a concrete instance of the proactive predicate. It shows that the clinic tracks which animals are proactive, which will be relevant when we apply the rule about proactive rain.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Fern is proactive.</text>
<logic>proactive(fern)</logic>
</step>

Scout is also proactive, giving us another concrete case of the proactive property. Together with the previous proactive facts, it confirms that the proactive predicate is populated in the knowledge base.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Scout is proactive.</text>
<logic>proactive(scout)</logic>
</step>

The crucial fact for our query is that rain is proactive. This directly activates the first rule, allowing us to move from a proactive description of rain to an analytical one.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Rain is proactive.</text>
<logic>proactive(rain)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that rain is proactive (PREMISE_7) and the conditional rule that proactive rain implies analytical rain (PREMISE_1), we can infer that rain must be analytical. This step is an application of logical implication: the antecedent holds, so the consequent follows.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From rain being proactive and the rule linking proactive rain to analytical rain, we infer that rain is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(rain)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established rain is analytical (STEP_8), we can apply the second rule stating that analytical rain leads to intuitive rain (PREMISE_2). Because the premise of the rule is satisfied, we conclude that rain is intuitive.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since rain is now known to be analytical and the rule says analytical rain implies intuitive rain, we conclude that rain is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(rain)</logic>
</step>

We derived Rain is intuitive, so the conclusion Rain is not intuitive is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Chef Rio is known for being witty. In this kitchen, any chef who is witty is also goal‑oriented. Any chef who is honest becomes knowledgeable. If a chef is alert, then they are not goal‑oriented. Moreover, either Chef Rio is alert or Chef Rio is honest.
examples.question: Is it the case that Chef Rio is not knowledgeable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the claim that Chef Rio is not knowledgeable holds. To answer, we must use the given facts about Rio’s personality and the logical relationships among the properties.

The rule states that any chef who is witty is also goal‑oriented. This will let us turn Rio’s wittiness into a goal‑oriented status later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any chef who is witty is also goal‑oriented.</text>
<logic>goal_oriented(rio) :- witty(rio)</logic>
</step>

The second rule says that honesty guarantees knowledgeability. So if we can establish Rio is honest, we can infer he is knowledgeable.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Any chef who is honest becomes knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(rio) :- honest(rio)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that if a chef is alert then they cannot be goal‑oriented. This provides a way to rule out alertness once we know Rio is goal‑oriented.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a chef is alert then they are not goal‑oriented.</text>
<logic>~goal_oriented(rio) :- alert(rio)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction informs us that at least one of the two conditions holds for Rio: either he is alert or he is honest. This will be useful when we eliminate one alternative.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Chef Rio is alert or Chef Rio is honest.</text>
<logic>alert(rio) v honest(rio)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that Chef Rio is witty. This fact is the starting point for applying the first rule.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Chef Rio is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(rio)</logic>
</step>

Applying the first rule to the fact that Rio is witty, we deduce that Rio is goal‑oriented. This gives us a concrete property that can interact with the alert rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Rio’s wittiness and the rule that wittiness implies goal‑orientation, we infer that Rio is goal‑oriented.</text>
<logic>goal_oriented(rio)</logic>
</step>

Since we now know Rio is goal‑oriented, the alert rule would make him not goal‑oriented if he were alert. To avoid a contradiction, we conclude that Rio cannot be alert, i.e., he is not alert.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since Rio is goal‑oriented and the rule that alertness would make someone not goal‑oriented, we conclude that Rio cannot be alert.</text>
<logic>~alert(rio)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Rio is not alert and the earlier disjunction that Rio is either alert or honest, the only remaining option is that Rio is honest. Thus we infer Rio’s honesty.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given that Rio is not alert and that either alert or honest holds, we deduce that Rio must be honest.</text>
<logic>honest(rio)</logic>
</step>

Having established Rio’s honesty, we invoke the second rule that honesty leads to knowledgeability, allowing us to conclude that Rio is knowledgeable.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>From Rio’s honesty and the rule that honesty leads to knowledgeability, we infer that Rio is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(rio)</logic>
</step>

We derived Rio is knowledgeable, so the conclusion Rio is not knowledgeable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the game studio, the lead developer is strategic. Whenever the lead developer is hardworking, they become methodical. If the lead developer is passionate, then they are not methodical. Also, either the lead developer is not strategic or they are hardworking.
examples.question: Is the lead developer passionate?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the lead developer is passionate. To answer this, we must examine the facts and conditional statements about the lead developer's other traits and see how they interact.

The rule linking hardworking to methodical is important because it lets us turn a statement about work ethic into a statement about organization. Establishing this connection will be useful later when we know the lead developer is hardworking.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the lead developer is hardworking, then they are methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(lead_developer) :- hardworking(lead_developer)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that passion blocks methodical behavior. By having this conditional, we can later use the fact that the lead developer is methodical to argue against the possibility of passion.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the lead developer is passionate, then they are not methodical.</text>
<logic>~methodical(lead_developer) :- passionate(lead_developer)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the lead developer is not strategic, or they are hardworking.</text>
<logic>~strategic(lead_developer) v hardworking(lead_developer)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The lead developer is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(lead_developer)</logic>
</step>

We have the explicit fact that the lead developer is strategic, and we also have a disjunction saying either they are not strategic or they are hardworking. Since the first part of the disjunction is false (they are strategic), the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second part – that the lead developer is hardworking – to be true. This application of disjunctive syllogism gives us the hardworking property.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>From the fact that the lead developer is strategic and the statement that either they are not strategic or they are hardworking, we deduce that the lead developer must be hardworking.</text>
<logic>hardworking(lead_developer)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the lead developer is hardworking, we can apply the rule from Premise 1. That rule states that any hardworking individual is also methodical, so we can directly infer that the lead developer is methodical. This is a straightforward use of modus ponens.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since the lead developer is hardworking and the rule that hardworking implies methodical, we infer that the lead developer is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(lead_developer)</logic>
</step>

With the lead developer confirmed as methodical, we look at Premise 2, which says that if someone is passionate then they are not methodical. The contrapositive of this statement tells us that being methodical implies not being passionate. Since we have methodical, we can conclude the lead developer is not passionate, using modus tollens.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given that the lead developer is methodical, and the rule that if they are passionate then they are not methodical, we conclude that the lead developer cannot be passionate.</text>
<logic>~passionate(lead_developer)</logic>
</step>

We derived Lead_developer is not passionate, so the conclusion Lead_developer is passionate is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Vera, an experienced potter, is exempt from the kiln schedule and she is resourceful in gathering clay. Whenever Vera is strategic in her designs, she is also skillful at shaping the pots. Normally, if Vera is resourceful and there is no abnormality called ab_strategic_a affecting her strategic planning, then she becomes strategic. In the usual case, if Vera is skillful and there is no abnormality called ab_supportive obstructing her, then she is considered supportive to her fellow artisans. Also, if Vera is exempt and there is no abnormality called ab_strategic_b preventing the opposite, then Vera is not strategic. These are the only facts and rules we have about Vera.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude whether Vera is diligent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide if the potter Vera can be regarded as diligent given the facts and default rules about her. Our goal is to see whether any chain of reasoning leads to a conclusion about diligence, or whether the knowledge base leaves the query unresolved.

The rule linking strategic behavior to skillfulness tells us that strategic status is sufficient for skillfulness. <text>If Vera is strategic, then she is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(vera) :- strategic(vera)</logic>
</step>

This default rule says that Vera's resourcefulness, together with the absence of the abnormality ab_strategic_a, makes her strategic. Since we know Vera is resourceful, the only thing that could block the inference is the presence of ab_strategic_a, which we have no information about. Therefore, under normal circumstances we could conclude Vera is strategic, but the rule is defeasible.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Vera is resourceful and there is no ab_strategic_a abnormality, then Vera is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(vera) :- resourceful(vera), not ab_strategic_a(vera)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a second default that turns skillfulness into supportiveness, provided the abnormality ab_supportive does not hold. If we later manage to derive that Vera is skillful, this rule would let us obtain that she is supportive, though again it could be blocked by an unknown abnormality.
<step n="3" type="premise">

This default works in the opposite direction: because Vera is exempt, and assuming the abnormality ab_strategic_b does not apply, we can infer that Vera is not strategic. This creates a potential conflict with PREMISE_2, because one rule tries to make Vera strategic while this one tries to make her not strategic. The resolution depends on whether the respective abnormalities are assumed absent.
<step n="4" type="premise">

<logic>~strategic(vera) :- exempt(vera), not ab_strategic_b(vera)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Vera is exempt is a solid piece of information; it activates the default in PREMISE_4. Since we have no evidence that ab_strategic_b is true, the default gives us a reason to believe Vera is not strategic.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Vera is exempt.</text>
<logic>exempt(vera)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Vera is resourceful supports the default in PREMISE_2. In the absence of evidence for ab_strategic_a, this default would suggest Vera is strategic. Thus we have two competing defaults, each leaning on different abnormality conditions that remain unknown.
<step n="6" type="premise">

<logic>resourceful(vera)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Vera is diligent: no rule or disjunction has "diligent" as a consequent.</text>

</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Vera is not diligent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~diligent(vera))</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived">
<text>Neither Vera is diligent nor Vera is not diligent can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(diligent(vera))</logic>
</step>

</think>

examples.context: In the hidden mountain dojo known as Lake, the master is renowned for his physical strength. Because any practitioner who is strong is automatically considered scholarly, Lake's strength also makes him scholarly. Regarding his mental traits, it is known that Lake is either not perceptive or he is well prepared for any challenge. Moreover, it is also stated that Lake is either not scholarly or he is perceptive. These statements together capture all the information we have about Lake.
examples.question: Is Lake well prepared?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Lake possesses the quality of being well prepared. The goal is to show that the statement well_prepared(lake) follows from the information provided.

The first premise introduces a conditional relationship: strength guarantees scholarship. Knowing this will let us turn the fact about Lake's strength into a fact about his scholarly status, which is needed later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Lake is strong, then Lake is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(lake) :- strong(lake)</logic>
</step>

The second premise offers a choice: either Lake lacks perceptiveness or he is well prepared. This disjunction will become useful once we know whether Lake is perceptive or not.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>One of the given alternatives is that Lake is either not perceptive or well prepared.</text>
<logic>~perceptive(lake) v well_prepared(lake)</logic>
</step>

The third premise gives another alternative: either Lake is not scholarly or he is perceptive. This will allow us to derive perceptiveness once we have established Lake's scholarly nature.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Another alternative states that Lake is either not scholarly or perceptive.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(lake) v perceptive(lake)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise states directly that Lake is strong. This concrete fact is the starting point for applying the conditional rule from PREMISE_1.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Lake is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(lake)</logic>
</step>

Because we have both the fact that Lake is strong and the rule that strength implies scholarship, we can conclude that Lake is scholarly. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens, turning the antecedent into the consequent.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From Lake being strong and the rule linking strength to scholarship, we infer that Lake is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(lake)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Lake is scholarly, we look at the disjunction from PREMISE_3. Since the alternative "Lake is not scholarly" is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Lake to be perceptive. This is an instance of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Using the disjunction that Lake is either not scholarly or perceptive, together with the fact that Lake is scholarly, we conclude that Lake must be perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(lake)</logic>
</step>

With Lake established as perceptive, we revisit the disjunction from PREMISE_2. The option "Lake is not perceptive" is now false, so the remaining alternative must be true: Lake is well prepared. Again, this follows by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given the disjunction that Lake is either not perceptive or well prepared, and knowing that Lake is perceptive, we deduce that Lake is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(lake)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Lake is well prepared, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

Whenever someone is well prepared, that person is courageous, so if Ruth were well prepared then Ruth would be courageous. If something is brilliant, then it is determined; thus any brilliant thing becomes determined, and in particular if Eden were brilliant, Eden would be determined. Experience guarantees fairness, so any experienced individual is fair‑minded, which means Ruth being experienced makes Ruth fair‑minded. Logical people are goal‑oriented, so if Grace were logical then Grace would be goal‑oriented. Anything that is sharp is automatically polite, therefore Eden’s sharpness makes Eden polite. Goal‑oriented individuals are prepared, so Grace’s being goal‑oriented would imply Grace is prepared. Reflectiveness leads to a lack of confidence; any reflective person is not confident, so if Grace were reflective then Grace would not be confident. Patience prevents rest; any patient person is not rested, meaning if Grace were patient then Grace would not be rested. Additionally, Grace is either patient or logical (or both). Either Ruth is not fair‑minded or Ruth is well prepared. Either Eden is not polite or Eden is brilliant. Either Grace is not prepared or Grace is confident. Finally, either Grace is not principled or Grace is rested.
